text:
Exam
Full details on Moodle (https://moodle.unive.it/course/view.php?id=24682)
• Project + oral exam (both mandatory, 50% each)
• Project: find a massive-data problem and solve it implementing techniques learned in the 
course.
• Oral exam:
1. Simple screening test, 10 minutes. No theorem proofs. I will check that you have basic 
knowledge of the techniques discussed during the lectures. 
2. Detailed questions on the project. If the project is trivial or you cannot explain details: 
standard oral exam on the theory of the course.

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/01 intro
text:
Exam
Full details on Moodle (https://moodle.unive.it/course/view.php?id=24682)
• Project + oral exam (both mandatory, 50% each)
• Project: find a massive-data problem and solve it implementing techniques learned in the 
course.
• Oral exam:
1. Simple screening test, 10 minutes. No theorem proofs. I will check that you have basic 
knowledge of the techniques discussed during the lectures. 
2. Detailed questions on the project. If the project is trivial or you cannot explain details: 
standard oral exam on the theory of the course. 
3. For 30 - 30L: one additional facultative theoretical question (prove a theorem 
discussed during the lectures).

text:
Exam
Full details on Moodle
• If this exam is joint with Learning over Massive Data, you need to pass both modules to have 
the 12 ECTS registered. 
• Partial evaluations expire when the new course’s edition start (usually, end of February - 
beginning of March).
• For my course (AMD): 
• You can submit the project whenever you want (form on Moodle). 
• You can take the oral exam whenever you want: send me an email to fix a date. Must be at

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/01 intro
text:
Exam
Full details on Moodle
• If this exam is joint with Learning over Massive Data, you need to pass both modules to have 
the 12 ECTS registered. 
• Partial evaluations expire when the new course’s edition start (usually, end of February - 
beginning of March).
• For my course (AMD): 
• You can submit the project whenever you want (form on Moodle). 
• You can take the oral exam whenever you want: send me an email to fix a date. Must be at 
least 2 weeks after submitting the project.
• Once you submit project and fix a date for the oral exam, I’ll evaluate your project and send 
you the result via email. You can try project+oral at most 4 times. 
• If oral not passed but project is passed, project remains valid for the academic year.

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/01 intro
text:
Course’s goals
Why yet another course on algorithms and data structures?
• Standard ADS course: the input has size n (Bytes) => the data structure uses 
O(n) words of memory. 
• This is kind of ok if n ≤ 108 Bytes (my laptop has 16 GiB of RAM)
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\01 intro.pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/01 intro
text:
Course’s goals
Why yet another course on algorithms and data structures?
• Standard ADS course: the input has size n (Bytes) => the data structure uses 
O(n) words of memory. 
• This is kind of ok if n ≤ 108 Bytes (my laptop has 16 GiB of RAM)
• What if n ≈ Terabytes? RAM is not sufficient (not even disk)!
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\01 intro.pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/01 intro
text:
Course’s goals
Why yet another course on algorithms and data structures?
• Standard ADS course: the input has size n (Bytes) => the data structure uses 
O(n) words of memory. 
• This is kind of ok if n ≤ 108 Bytes (my laptop has 16 GiB of RAM)
• What if n ≈ Terabytes? RAM is not sufficient (not even disk)!
• We need to compress the data and the data structures.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\01 intro.pdf

text:
Asymptotic notation
CS students should know all this stuff … 
• To analyze the runtime and space of our algorithms, we will use asymptotic 
notation. 
• Idea: do not measure exactly how many operations the algorithm performs or 
how many bits the data structure occupies … 
• … measure time/space approximately: disregard multiplying constants.

precedes “ompile”, and more bits to any other letter.

text:
Compressing multiple bitvectors
A final observation. Our compression scheme is local: the encoding of a block does not 
depend on the encoding of other blocks.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\04 05 zero-order bitvectors [1.3].pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/04 05 zero-order bitvectors [1.3]
text:
Compressing multiple bitvectors
A final observation. Our compression scheme is local: the encoding of a block does not 
depend on the encoding of other blocks. 
This implies that, if we compress the concatenation of multiple bitvectors, then the space 
can be bounded by the entropies of the individual bitvectors.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\04 05 zero-order bitvectors [1.3].pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/04 05 zero-order bitvectors [1.3]
text:
Compressing multiple bitvectors
A final observation. Our compression scheme is local: the encoding of a block does not 
depend on the encoding of other blocks. 
This implies that, if we compress the concatenation of multiple bitvectors, then the space 
can be bounded by the entropies of the individual bitvectors.
This will be important later in the FM-index (Section 1.6)

Compressing multiple bitvectors
A final observation. Our compression scheme is local: the encoding of a block does not 
depend on the encoding of other blocks.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\05 zero-order bitvectors part 2 [1.3].pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/05 zero-order bitvectors part 2 [1.3]
text:
Compressing multiple bitvectors
A final observation. Our compression scheme is local: the encoding of a block does not 
depend on the encoding of other blocks. 
This implies that, if we compress the concatenation of multiple bitvectors, then the space 
can be bounded by the entropies of the individual bitvectors.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\05 zero-order bitvectors part 2 [1.3].pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/05 zero-order bitvectors part 2 [1.3]
text:
Compressing multiple bitvectors
A final observation. Our compression scheme is local: the encoding of a block does not 
depend on the encoding of other blocks. 
This implies that, if we compress the concatenation of multiple bitvectors, then the space 
can be bounded by the entropies of the individual bitvectors.
This will be important later in the FM-index (Section 1.6)

text:
Announcement
The creator of C++, Bjarne Stroustrup, will be holding a seminar here! 
We will have the opportunity to hear live from one of the most 
influential computer scientists of the last 40 years, who has literally 
written the history of programming.
- When: May 11, 2026, 3:30 PM (during our lecture! The lecture is replaced by this seminar) 
- Where: Mainardi Auditorium, Alfa Building 
- Title: 21st Century C++ (it will cover how the language has evolved and how it is modern, safe and efficient 
today) 
Registration for the event is reserved for students until March 31. Register NOW! The room is almost fully-booked! 
LINK FOR RESERVATION: https://apps.unive.it/prenotazioni/p/bjarne?_lang=en

text:
EF in practice
Elias-Fano is one of the most successful "theoretical-to-practical" stories in the field 
of compressed data structures.  
It’s the gold standard for representing the inverted lists in modern, high-performance 
search engines. 
Facebook uses Elias-Fano for internal social graph indexing and inverted indexes.

text:
A possible research project
• The EF data structure is the standard used in practice in many applications.
• It is very fast, but there may be space for improvements.
• In practice, the speed of queries is proportional to the number of cache misses that the query triggers       
(a cache miss is about 100x slower than a cache hit!)
• Existing implementations trigger on average 
 4 cache misses for a predecessor query.
≈
• Project: optimize the cache efficiency of the EF data structure. Thoroughly benchmark the proposed 
solution with existing implementations. 
• How? Re-organize the memory layout of the structure to maximize data locality, engineer the queries, 
possibly change the internal components with something more cache-efficient (RRR is not very cache-efficient).

• A magic string permutation: the Burrows-Wheeler transform (BWT).
• RSA queries on the BWT enable count queries on the original string.
• The BWT is inherently easier to compress than the original string.

text:
Back in 1994 …
• Michael Burrows and David Wheeler discover an incredible string permutation that boosts compression.
• They submit it to the Data Compression Conference (DCC, still running today)
• DCC rejects the paper. 
• People implement very efficient data compressors based on the BWT.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\09 BWT [1.6.2] [Lemma 1.6.12].pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/09 BWT [1.6.2] [Lemma 1.6.12]
text:
Back in 1994 …
• Michael Burrows and David Wheeler discover an incredible string permutation that boosts compression.
• They submit it to the Data Compression Conference (DCC, still running today)
• DCC rejects the paper. 
• People implement very efficient data compressors based on the BWT.
• A few years later, DCC invites David and Michael as keynote speakers. They refuse by sending back the 
DCC rejection message from a few years before.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\09 BWT [1.6.2] [Lemma 1.6.12].pdf

text:
Back in 1994 …
• Michael Burrows and David Wheeler discover an incredible string permutation that boosts compression.
• They submit it to the Data Compression Conference (DCC, still running today)
• DCC rejects the paper. 
• People implement very efficient data compressors based on the BWT.
• A few years later, DCC invites David and Michael as keynote speakers. They refuse by sending back the 
DCC rejection message from a few years before. 
• >30 years later, the BWT is at the core of:

• Michael Burrows and David Wheeler discover an incredible string permutation that boosts compression.

• DCC rejects the paper. 
• People implement very efficient data compressors based on the BWT.
• A few years later, DCC invites David and Michael as keynote speakers. They refuse by sending back the 
DCC rejection message from a few years before. 
• >30 years later, the BWT is at the core of:
• Several compressors (including bzip2)

text:
Back in 1994 …
• Michael Burrows and David Wheeler discover an incredible string permutation that boosts compression.
• They submit it to the Data Compression Conference (DCC, still running today)
• DCC rejects the paper. 
• People implement very efficient data compressors based on the BWT.
• A few years later, DCC invites David and Michael as keynote speakers. They refuse by sending back the 
DCC rejection message from a few years before. 
• >30 years later, the BWT is at the core of:
• Several compressors (including bzip2)
• Virtually every DNA-alignment software run every day hundreds of times in all labs around the world.

• Michael Burrows and David Wheeler discover an incredible string permutation that boosts compression.

text:
Exercise
• What’s the difference (the edit distance) between these two BWTs?
• BWT(BANANA$)
• BWT(ABANANA$)
• Try to derive a general rule showing that, when we prepend a character to the string, the BWT 
changes only in 2 places. Hint. divide this into two steps:
• In the BWT matrix, add the new character (‘A’ in the previous example) after every occurrence 
of $. How does the BWT change after these modifications?
• Finally, add a new matrix row corresponding to the new string (“ABANANA$” in the previous 
example). How does the BWT change after these modification? Show how to compute the 
position of this row efficiently (rank/select operations on F and L).

text:
Exercise
• What’s the difference (the edit distance) between these two BWTs?
• BWT(BANANA$)
• BWT(ABANANA$)
• Try to derive a general rule showing that, when we prepend a character to the string, the BWT 
changes only in 2 places. Hint. divide this into two steps:
• In the BWT matrix, add the new character (‘A’ in the previous example) after every occurrence 
of $. How does the BWT change after these modifications?
• Finally, add a new matrix row corresponding to the new string (“ABANANA$” in the previous 
example). How does the BWT change after these modification? Show how to compute the 
position of this row efficiently (rank/select operations on F and L).
• As a result, a dynamic string data structure supporting access/rank/insert operations in 
 time allows us computing the BWT online in 
 time! Advanced: design such a 
dynamic string data structure (can be even compressed!)

field of Compressed Data Structures with fundamental impact on Data

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/10 FM-index [1.6]
text:
A personal opinion
The BWT and FM-index should have been defined in reverse!
• Prepend $ (rather than appending it) to the string
• Sort the BWT-matrix rows co-lexicographically (rather than lexicographically)
• BWT = first row
• As a result: 
• The BWT and FM-index can be built left-to-right on streams (see last lecture)
• The backward search algorithm becomes forward search.
• Everything is much more natural!
That's how the online is implemented.
[NOTE] That's how the online is implemented.

text:
3. Beyond strings
• Note: a string is a (simple) labeled graph. 
 
• The Burrows-Wheeler transform and the FM-index can be generalized to arbitrary labeled 
graphs / finite-state automata!  
• Intuition: sort the graph’s nodes according to the co-lexicographic order of the strings 
entering in the nodes. 
• The resulting theory is incredibly rich and elegant. We can index and compress labeled 
graphs and (infinite) regular languages!

text:
Most of the times we will work with discrete RV (but everything can be generalized).
We will study three bounds, from the weakest to the strongest:
• Markov (linear)
• Chebyshev (quadratic)
• Chernoff-Hoeffding (exponential)
Each bound will require some assumptions on the RV.
Typically, the stronger assumptions you make on a RV, the stronger bound you can get. 
Finally, we will combine Chebyshev and Chernoff-Hoeffding into a very useful tool: the 
mean+median trick.

text:
Boosted Chebyshev - example
• Flip 100 fair coins for 10 times and take the average of the number of heads you see in the 10 
rounds.
• Then, the probability that the average exceeds 80 is 1/10 of what we obtained earlier with 
Chebyshev: ≈0.01389/10 = 0.001389
Same as flipping 1000 coins and prob > 800.

text:
Boosted Chebyshev - example
• Flip 100 fair coins for 10 times and take the average of the number of heads you see in the 10 
rounds.
• Then, the probability that the average exceeds 80 is 1/10 of what we obtained earlier with 
Chebyshev: ≈0.01389/10 = 0.001389
• Quite a powerful trick for the next lectures: if we run the algorithm s times and take the average of the 
results, then we boost the probability of success by a factor of s.

text:
Metrics
We will devise several sketches possessing property 4 (similarity preserving) under several 
metrics:
• Jaccard distance/similarity
• Hamming distance (we already did it in a previous lecture!)
• Euclidean distance (ell-2 norm)
But sketches exist for most metrics: edit distance, cosine distance, ell-p norm, etc. 
Remember that a metric is a non-negative binary function 
 satisfying:
d(x, y) ≥0
• Identity: d(x, x) = 0
• Symmetry: d(x, y) = d(y, x)
• Triangle inequality: d(x, z) ≤d(x, y) + d(y, z)

text:
A possible exam project
Excellent topic for a project!
Note that we have seen just the basics. Several optimizations exist in the literature. Consider 
reading some literature before implementing anything. 
Project examples: 
• Build a search engine that uses NNS to find web pages matching the query.
• Use NNS to implement a text index on massive DNA datasets (break the DNA into short 
substrings of a fixed length before applying Jaccard, see the 2024/2025 project on 
Moodle).
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\16 Locality-Sensitive Hashing [4.6].pdf

text:
A possible exam project
Excellent topic for a project!
Note that we have seen just the basics. Several optimizations exist in the literature. Consider 
reading some literature before implementing anything. 
Project examples: 
• Build a search engine that uses NNS to find web pages matching the query.
• Use NNS to implement a text index on massive DNA datasets (break the DNA into short 
substrings of a fixed length before applying Jaccard, see the 2024/2025 project on 
Moodle).
• Explore how LSH can be used in LLMs to reduce the amount of data on which the LLM is 
trained (deduplication). Check how this pre-processing affects training.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\16 Locality-Sensitive Hashing [4.6].pdf

text:
A possible exam project
Excellent topic for a project!
Note that we have seen just the basics. Several optimizations exist in the literature. Consider 
reading some literature before implementing anything. 
Project examples: 
• Build a search engine that uses NNS to find web pages matching the query.
• Use NNS to implement a text index on massive DNA datasets (break the DNA into short 
substrings of a fixed length before applying Jaccard, see the 2024/2025 project on 
Moodle).
• Explore how LSH can be used in LLMs to reduce the amount of data on which the LLM is 
trained (deduplication). Check how this pre-processing affects training. 
• Build a DBMS that uses NNS to answer queries (e.g. integrate approximate search in SQL).

Nicola Prezza
Ca’ Foscari University of Venice
Version: February 26, 2026

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes
text:
Overview of the course
The goal of this course is to introduce algorithmic techniques for dealing with massive data: data so
large that it does not ﬁt in the computer’s memory. In this course, massive data will be modeled
using two fundamental combinatorial objects: sequences (strings) and sets of integers. Solutions will
be divided into two broad categories, covered in the two modules of the course: (lossless) compressed
data structures and (lossy) data sketches.
Module 1 - Compressed data structures
A compressed data structure supports fast queries on the data and uses a space proportional to the
compressed data. This solution is typically lossless: the representation allows to fully reconstruct the
original data. Here we exploit the fact that, in some applications, data is extremely redundant and
can be considerably reduced in size (even by orders of magnitude) without losing any information
by exploiting this redundancy. Interestingly it turns out that, usually, computation on compressed
data can be performed faster than on the original (uncompressed) data: the ﬁeld of compressed data
structures exploits the natural duality between compression and computation to achieve this goal.
The main results we will discuss in Chapter 1, compressed dictionaries (solving various queries
on sets of integers) and compressed text indexes (solving indexed pattern matching on strings),
ﬁnd important applications in information retrieval. They allow us to turn large collection of docu-
ments/integer sets into a compressed data structure that allows locating substrings/ﬁnding integers
very quickly, without decompressing the data. These techniques today stand at the core of modern
search engines and sequence-mapping algorithms in computational biology.
Importantly, lossless techniques cannot break the information-theoretic lower bound for represent-
ing the input. For example, since the number of subsets of cardinality m of {1, . . . , n} is
� n
m

, the
information-theoretic lower bound for storing such a subset is log2
� n
m

= m log(n/m) + O(m) bits. No
lossless data structure can use asymptotically less space than this bound for all such subsets.
Module 2 - Data sketches
In the second module we will resort to lossy compression and throw away some features of the data in
order to dramatically reduce its size, usually breaking the information-theoretic lower bound. This feat
will be achieved through randomization (queries may fail with small probability) and approximation
(queries may return an approximate result).
In Chapter 3 we show that any set of cardinality m can be stored with a ﬁlter data structure in
just O(m) bits, provided that we accept a small probability that membership queries fail. Note that
this breaks the information-theoretic lower bound discussed above, m log(n/m) + O(m) bits.
In Chapter 4 we see how to shrink even more the size of our data while still being able to compute
some useful information on it. The most important concept here is sketching: our input data is
squeezed into sub-linear representations called data sketch. For example, a set of cardinality m over a
universe of cardinality n can be stored in just O(polylog(m) + polylog(n)) bits (exponential compres-
sion!) using a data sketch supporting useful queries such as set similarity and cardinality estimation
(approximately and with a bounded probability of error). Sketches can be computed oﬀ-line to reduce
the dataset’s size and/or speed up the computation of distances, or on-line on data streams, where
data is thrown away as soon as it arrives (only data sketches are kept in main memory).
Sources
The proofs of these notes have been put together from various sources, reported in the
bibliography.
The notes collect also material from Leskovec et al.’s book [32] (Mining of massive
data sets), Amit Chakrabarti’s notes on data stream algorithms [10], Gonzalo Navarro’s book [37]
(compressed data structures), and Demetrescu and Finocchi’s chapter on algorithms for data streams
from the book [46].

text:
Chapter 1
Compressed Data Structures
In this chapter we study data structures for representing strings and sets of integers. The goal is
to design data structures using a space proportional to the compressed input. We will see two main
notions of compressibility: worst-case entropy (used for sets of integers) and empirical entropy (used
for string data structures). First, in Section 1.1 we start with the pattern matching problem and give
some classical uncompressed solutions.
1.1
Full-text indexing - classical solutions
In this section we work with strings T ∈Σn of length n over alphabet Σ of size σ.
We consider the following problem:
Deﬁnition 1.1.1 (Full-text indexing). Given a string T ∈Σn (a text), build a data structure I(T )
(an index) solving the following queries:
1. locate(P): given a string P ∈Σm, with m ≤n, return the exact occurrences of P in T , i.e.
locate(P) = {i : T [i, i + m −1] = P}.
2. count(P): given a string P ∈Σm, with m ≤n, return the number occ of exact occurrences of P
in T , i.e. count(P) = occ = |locate(P)|.
If we store the string T along with the index I(T ), then it’s immediate that we can also access any
character T [i] in constant time. In Sections 1.5 and 1.6, however, we are going to present compressed
indexes: the bit-size of I(T ) is close to the size of the compressed T . It follows that we cannot aﬀord
to store T along with I(T ), because the former could be much larger than the latter! In such cases, it
will be useful that I(T ) also provides random access to the characters of T . More formally, we want
that I(T ) supports this extra query:
3. extract(i, ℓ): return string T [i, . . . , i + ℓ−1]
An index supporting query extract is called self-index, to reﬂect that it is a replacement of the
underlying string:
Deﬁnition 1.1.2. If I(T ) supports queries count and locate, it is called a full-text index. If I(T )
supports all three queries count, locate, and extract, it is called a full-text self-index.
1.1.1
Inverted indexes and suﬃx trie
An inverted index is a classical data structure solving a very restricted version of text indexing problem.
If the text T is formed by words — for example T = “to be or not to be” — then we may simply
4

text:
record the positions (counting from 1) where each word appears in T , using a dictionary data structure
H (for example, a hash table): H[to] = {1, 14}, H[be] = {4, 17}, H[or] = 7, etc. Inverted indexes do
not work on texts without word breaks (for example, DNA: T = ACCTGCATT . . . ), and can only
answer quick locate queries on words (not on arbitrary substrings).
A complete and fast (at query time) solution to full-text indexing is to build an inverted index
over all the substrings T [i, j] of our string T , and associate to each substring T [i, j] the result of
count(T [i, j]) and locate(T [i, j]). This solution solves fast queries, but is very space-ineﬃcient: there
are Θ(n2) such substrings, and each uses O(n) space. The total space of this solution is, therefore
O(n3) words of memory.
We ﬁrst show how to reduce the space to O(n2) words. The main observation we will use is that
every substring of T is a preﬁx of a suﬃx of T .
Example 1.1.3. Let T = BANANA$. Then, the substring NAN is a preﬁx of the suﬃx NANA$.
This observation allows us to index just the suﬃxes of a string (there are n of them), rather than
all substrings of the string (there are Θ(n2) of them). We introduce the suﬃx trie data structure; see
Figure 1.1 for an example.
Deﬁnition 1.1.4. The suﬃx trie of T is the trie of all suﬃxes of T . On each leaf, corresponding to
suﬃx T [i, n], we store position i. For each internal node x, corresponding to substring T [i, j] (i.e. the
string read from the root of the trie to node x) we store (1) count(T [i, j]), and (2) a pointer to the
leftmost and rightmost leaf in the subtree rooted in x. We moreover link all the trie’s leaves, from the
leftmost to rightmost, using a linked list.
We obtain:
Theorem 1.1.5. The suﬃx trie uses O(n2) words of space, supports count queries in optimal time
O(m), and locate queries in optimal time O(m + occ).
1.1.2
Suﬃx tree
To reduce the space of the suﬃx trie, we observe that the strings we can read on the paths on the trie
correspond to text’s substrings. For example, in Figure 1.1, consider the node x reached by reading
string b from the root, and the node y reached by reading string baab$. The path connecting x to
y is labeled by string aab$ = T [3, 6]. We can therefore replace this path by the two integers [3, 6],
indicating that in order to reconstruct the original path, we need to read the text’s substring T [3, 6].
We perform this operation for all maximal paths of nodes x1 →x2 →· · · →xk such that x1, . . . , xk−1
have only one child each, and xk is either a leaf or has at least two children. This process is called
path compression, and the resulting tree is called the suﬃx tree: see Figure 1.2.
The suﬃx tree has exactly n leaves, and its internal nodes are all branching (i.e. have at least 2
children). It follows that the total number of nodes is at most 2n−1, and the number of edges at most
2n −2. For each edge and each node, we store a constant number of pointers and integers (i.e. the
same extra information as in Figure 1.1). Pattern matching works as with the suﬃx trie, except that
(1) while descending the tree from the root, we also need to jump on the text to reconstruct the labels
of the tree’s edges, and (2) our search may end in the middle of an edge, for example: if we search baa
in Example 1.2, we end up in the middle of the edge entering in the leaf containing 2. Modiﬁcation (1)
does not increase the query running time, while issue (2) is not really an issue: if our search ends up
in the middle of an edge, just jump at the end of the edge. For example: if we search baa in Example
1.2, we end up in the middle of an edge; just jump at the end of the edge (corresponding to string
baab$): the result is correct, because all occurrences of baa continue with characters b$. We obtain:
Theorem 1.1.6. The suﬃx tree uses O(n) words of space, supports count queries in optimal time
O(m), and locate queries in optimal time O(m + occ).

text:
1.1.3
Suﬃx array
Despite using linear O(n) words of space, the constant hidden in the big-O notation of the suﬃx tree’s
space usage is very large. In fact, for every node and edge we need to record lots of information:
pointers to children, count information, pointers to the leftmost/rightmost leaf in the node’s subtree,
etc. A good suﬃx tree implementation uses at least (approximately) 8n words, and each word takes
4 bytes (even 8 bytes if the text is very long). In total, at least 32n bytes. For example, if T is the
DNA reference sequence of the Human genome, n is approximately 3.2·109. The text T can be stored
in about 3 Gigabytes, while the suﬃx tree requires at least 32n ≈95 GiB. In total, our data structure
(text + suﬃx tree) uses approximately 98 GiB: more than 32 times the text itself!
Observe that we ordered the leaves of the suﬃx tree and suﬃx trie according to the lexicographic
order of their corresponding suﬃxes:
$ (6) < aab$ (3) < ab$ (4) < abaab$ (1) < b$ (5) < baab$ (2)
This is not a coincidence: since suﬃxes are sorted, the idea is that we can use binary search to ﬁnd
eﬃciently all suﬃxes preﬁxed by any query pattern. This array of numbers on the leaves,
6, 3, 4, 1, 5, 2
is called the suﬃx array (SA). Suppose we want to ﬁnd the suﬃxes preﬁxed by pattern P = aa. We
do this by binary search. First, we jump in the middle of the suﬃx array, and read number 4. Then,
we jump on the text and start reading T [4..] = ab... We compare this string with the pattern aa,
discovering that it is larger: ab... > aa. It follows that we can ignore all entries of the suﬃx array
4,1,5,2, because they correspond to suﬃxes being larger than the pattern: aa < ab$ (4) < abaab$ (1) <
b$ (5) < baab. We repeat recursively on the remaining suﬃx array interval (6,3), until ﬁnding the range
of all suﬃxes starting with our pattern (exactly as when we perform binary search on an array of sorted
integers to ﬁnd all occurrences of some query integer). Note that we perform O(log n) binary search
steps, and in each step we need to compare the pattern with a text substring of length at most m.
Binary search thus runs in O(m log n) time and allows ﬁnding the suﬃx array range containing all
suﬃxes preﬁxed by the pattern P. The length of this range is the answer to count(P), while the
numbers contained inside the suﬃx array range are the answer to locate(P). We obtain:
Theorem 1.1.7. The suﬃx array uses n words of space on top of the text, supports count queries in
time O(m log n), and locate queries in time O(m log n + occ).
On the Human genome (n ≈3.2 × 109), each suﬃx array entry can be stored using words of 4
bytes. The text itself can be stored using 1 byte per character. In total, our data structure (text +
SA) uses 5n ≈15 GiB: this is more than 6 times smaller than the suﬃx tree.
1.2
Basics of information theory
Consider again our example of the previous section where the text T is the human genome, of length
n = 3.2 × 109 (we use equality here for simplicity). The human genome is a sequence over a 4-letters
alphabet: Σ = {A, C, G, T}. But then, there is no need of using the whole ASCII alphabet (1 byte per
character): 2 bits per character are suﬃcient! Let γ : Σ →{0, 1}+ be an encoding of our alphabet.
For example, we could encode γ(A) = 00, γ(C) = 01, γ(G) = 10, γ(T) = 11. Then, the whole human
genome ﬁts in 2n bits, which is just about 700 MiB! This makes the suﬃx tree (95 GiB) and suﬃx
array (12 GiB) appear even larger than they need to be: the former uses 135 times the space of the
text, while the latter 17 times.
Things could be much worse than this. Consider, for example, the following text:
T = ACGTTTTT . . . T

Where we have one occurrence of A,C,G, and 3.2 × 109 −3 occurrences of T. What if we encode
the characters as follows, γ(A) = 100, γ(C) = 101, γ(G) = 110, γ(T) = 0? The encoded text becomes
(spaces are added just for clarity):
γ(T ) = 100 101 110 0 0 0 0 . . . 0
Notice that we chose the encoding γ() in such a way that the concatenation of the encoded char-
acters, γ(T ) = γ(A) · γ(C) · γ(G) · γ(T) . . . γ(T) can be decoded unambiguously. A code with this
property is called preﬁx code and will be deﬁned more precisely later. The total number of bits in
γ(T ) is |γ(T )| = 3 · 3 + 1 · (3.2 · 109 −3) = 3.200.000.006, so we are using an average of |γ(T )|/n ≈1
bit per symbol! Now, the encoded text uses just approximately 350 MiB. We achieved compression by
observing that character’s frequencies are very unbalanced.
The comparison with the suﬃx tree and suﬃx array is even more dramatic than before: the former
uses 270 times the space of the compressed text, while the latter 34 times. The goal of this chapter
will be to design compressed indexes taking a space very close to the compressed text, while at the
same time including the text itself (i.e. also supporting access to the characters of T ). Using the above
example, the compressed index and text will use in total a space slightly larger than 350 MiB (let’s
say, around 400 MiB) and support very eﬃcient count, locate, and access (T [i]) operations.
1.2.1
Worst-case entropy
Consider our DNA alphabet Σ = {A, C, G, T} and the ﬁrst encoding we used: γ(A) = 00, γ(C) = 01,
γ(G) = 10, γ(T) = 11. Observe that we used the same number of bits (2) for each character. Can we
use 1 bit per character? It is easy to see that this will not work, since any encoding assigning 1 bit to
all characters, will assign the same code to at least two characters. For example: γ(A) = 0, γ(C) = 1,
γ(G) = 0, γ(T) = 1. Such a code cannot be decoded because we cannot distinguish C from T and G
from A.
This reasoning leads to the conclusion that no uniquely-decodable code can assign less than
⌈log2(|Σ|)⌉bits to all characters in Σ. We are now going to scale this result to sets of combinato-
rial objects. Let U be a set (universe) of combinatorial objects, for example: all strings of length n
over alphabet Σ, or the number of subsets of {1, . . . , n} of cardinality m.
Deﬁnition 1.2.1 (Worst-case entropy). Let U be a universe of combinatorial objects. The worst-case
entropy of U is
Hwc(U) = ⌈log2(|U|)⌉
We conclude:
Corollary 1.2.2. Any uniquely-decodable encoding γ : U →{0, 1}+ of objects from U cannote use less
than Hwc(U) bits for every object of U, i.e. there must exist x ∈U such that |γ(x)| ≥Hwc(U).
The name worst-case entropy comes from the fact that, for any encoding of U, the length (in bits)
of the longest code for any object in U must be at least Hwc(U).
Example 1.2.3. Let Bn be the set of all sequences of length n over binary alphabet {0, 1} (i.e. bitvec-
tors). Then, |Bn| = 2n and therefore Hwc(Bn) = n.
Example 1.2.4. Let Bn,m be the set of all bitvectors of length n containing exactly m ≤n bits equal
to 1. Then, |Bn,m| =

The goal of the research ﬁeld of succinct data structures is to design data structures using Hwc(U)
bits to represent objects from U while supporting fast queries on them. For example, U could be the
set of all texts of length n over alphabet Σ, and the queries to support could be pattern matching
queries (count, locate). With respect to this universe and these queries, the goal of a succinct data
structure would be to use a space close to Hwc(U) = n log |Σ| bits while supporting fast count and
locate queries.
Exercise 1
Consider example 1.2.6. Show how to encode any ordinal tree with n nodes using exactly 2n bits.
Note that such an encoding is (almost) worst-case optimal, because it (almost) matches the worst-case
entropy (up to an additive term of Θ(log n) bits, which can be ignored since it is exponentially smaller
than the worst-case entropy).
1.2.2
Preﬁx-free codes
Worst-case entropy tells us how much compressible is an object from some set U, in the worst case.
The goal of this chapter is to ﬁnd encodings for our objects (in general, strings and sets) approaching
this bound. If the objects we are trying to compress are strings, one way to approach worst-case
entropy (as we will see) is to encode the string’s individual characters using a good-enough preﬁx-free
code:
Deﬁnition 1.2.7. A preﬁx-free code is an injective function γ : Σ →{0, 1}+ such that γ(x) is not a
preﬁx of γ(y), for any x, y ∈Σ such that x̸ = y.
Example 1.2.8. The code γ(A) = 100, γ(C) = 101, γ(G) = 110, γ(T) = 0 is preﬁx-free because no
character’s encoding is a preﬁx of another.
It is easy to see that, when using a preﬁx-free code, we can just concatenate the encodings of all
the string’s characters, and the resulting bit sequence is invertible (i.e. we can reconstruct the string).
More formally:
Deﬁnition 1.2.9. If γ is a preﬁx-free code and S ∈Σn, we deﬁne the domain extension
γ(S) = γ(S[1]) · γ(S[2]) . . . γ(S[n])
Remark 1.2.10. If γ is preﬁx-free, we can reconstruct S given γ and γ(S).
Among many useful preﬁx-free codes we will use Huﬀman’s (1952) which can be proved to be
optimal among preﬁx codes. We show how to build a Huﬀman code via an example.
Example 1.2.11. Consider the string S = abracadabra. Count the frequency of every character in
the string: a appears 5 times, b and r appear 2 times each, and c and d appear 1 time each. The Figure
below shows a possible Huﬀman code for these frequencies (the Huﬀman code is not necessarily unique,
but all Huﬀman codes have the same cost). We are going to build a binary tree having in the leaves
all the characters together with their frequencies. From each internal node, the left outgoing edge will
be labeled with 0 and the right one with 1. The path from the root to each leaf will spell the code for
that character. To build the tree, begin by creating the leaves. Then, pick two nodes x, y without parent
having the smallest frequencies (in the example, we pick c and d, both with frequency 1), create a new
node xy whose frequency is the sum of the frequencies of x and y (in the example, we create node cd
of frequency 1+1=2), make it parent of x and y, and repeat recursively until we have only 1 node left
(the root) without any parent. In our example, we proceeded by grouping b and r (parented by br of

and a. At the end, we get the Huﬀman code γ(c) = 000, γ(d) = 001, γ(b) = 010, γ(r) = 011, γ(a) = 1.
Note that the most frequent character gets indeed the shortest code. The ﬁnal bit-cost of the encoding
is |γ(abracadabra)| = 23 bits. It can be proved that no preﬁx-free code for this string can use less than
23 bits: Huﬀman is optimal among all preﬁx-free codes.

Remark 1.2.12. Note that after the ﬁrst step in Example 1.2.11 (after grouping c and d) the nodes
without parents are cd(2), b(2), r(2), and a(5). There are multiple valid ways to proceed: we could
group cd and b, or cd and r, or b and r (in the example we chose the latter grouping). The resulting
Huﬀman trees will have a diﬀerent shape, but all the resulting codes will be optimal (i.e. use 23 bits,
in that example). As an exercise, build all the valid Huﬀman trees for these frequencies.
1.2.3
Zero-order empirical entropy
We will see that Huﬀman encoding allows us to approach the worst-case entropy of the set of all
strings with the same character frequencies of our input string. We show this by introducing a new
compressibility measure for strings, which will turn out to be tightly related with worst-case entropy.
Deﬁnition 1.2.13. Let S ∈Σn be a string. The zero-order empirical entropy of S is deﬁned as:
H0(S) =

Where nc = |{i : S[i] = c}| is the number of occurrences of character c in S. When S is clear from
the context, we will just write H0 instead of H0(S).
Shannon’s source coding theorem (1948) implies that (as n grows to inﬁnity) no preﬁx-free code can
use less than nH0 bits to encode a string S ∈Σn. On the other hand, it can be shown that Huﬀman
code uses no more than n(H0 + 1) bits: at most 1 bit per character over the empirical entropy. Other
encodings (for example, arithmetic encoding) achieve nH0 +O(1) bits. In the next sections, we will see
encodings achieving nH0 +o(n log σ) bits which support also fast queries on the string (i.e. compressed
data structures).

Remark 1.2.15. In the example above, in order to achieve nH0 we would need to use on average
0.8 bits per bit. Observe, however, that Huﬀman is useless when encoding bitvectors, because it uses
at least one bit per symbol! In the next sections we will see that the trick to achieve this (apparently
impossible) goal is to encode blocks of bits, rather than single bits.
It turns out that worst-case entropy and zero-order entropy are tightly connected. Later we will
use the following lemma on bitvectors. Try to prove it by yourself as an exercise (full proof in [37, Sec.
2.3.1]), expanding the binomial coeﬃcient and using Stirling’s approximation of the logarithm of the
factorial (log2(n!) = n log2 n −n log2 e + O(log n)):
Lemma 1.2.16. Let Bn,m be the set of all bitvectors of length n with m bits equal to 1, and let
x ∈Bn,m. Then:
Hwc(Bn,m) =

A similar relation as the one of Lemma 1.2.16 holds between the worst-case entropy of the set of
strings with ﬁxed symbol probabilities, and the zero-order empirical entropy of any member of that
family of strings (see [37, Sec. 2.3.1]).
1.2.4
High-order empirical entropy
Zero-order empirical entropy exploits knowledge about the character’s frequencies to achieve compres-
sion. Can we do better? Yes: intuitively, we could apply the same reasoning of zero-order compression
to the characters followed by the same context of k characters, for some ﬁxed (small) integer k. For
example, consider a book about algorithms. If we pick k = 8 and see string (context) lgorithm (of
length k = 8), then we are almost sure that the preceding character is a. In other words, a has fre-
quency 100% among the set of characters followed by string lgorithm, while all other characters have
frequency 0%: the set of characters followed by lgorithm is extremely compressible. By repeating the
same reasoning to all possible contexts of length k, we get to high-order empirical entropy.
Deﬁnition 1.2.17. Let S ∈Σn, and choose k ≤n. Let w ∈Σk. We deﬁne Sw as the string formed by
all characters preceding occurrences of w in S, from the leftmost to rightmost occurrence, considering
the string S as being circular (i.e. S[1] is preceded by S[n]).
Example 1.2.18. Let S = aababbabab and k = 2. Consider context w = ab. Sab is the string formed
by all characters that precede occurrences of ab in S: Sab = abbb. Similarly, Sbb = a, Saa = b, and
Sba = abaa (remember that we consider the string to be circular).
Deﬁnition 1.2.19 (k-th order empirical entropy). Let S ∈Σn, and choose k ≤n. The k-th order
empirical entropy of S is:

This means that a compressor achieving second-order entropy would use just 0.65 bits per symbol to
encode S. On the other hand, the zero-order entropy of S is H0(S) = 1: no compression at all (note
that the alphabet’s size is equal to 2, so a naive encoding using 1 bit per symbol achieves this result as
well).
Remark 1.2.21. The larger k is, the better compression we get. In fact, it holds:
log2 σ ≥H0 ≥H1 ≥H2 ≥. . .
However, we cannot use an arbitrarily large k: an information-theoretic lower bound prevents from
using k larger than logσ n (see [22] for more details).
As a concrete example (see [38]), a zero-order compressed XML ﬁle is typically 35% smaller than
its uncompressed version. Using high-order compression helps a lot: nH5 is about 90% smaller than
the uncompressed ﬁle. This means that XML ﬁles are very predictable, given short contexts.
1.3
Zero-order compressed bitvectors
The topics of this section are treated more in detail in [37, Sec. 4.1.1].
Observe that Huﬀman encoding uses an integer number of bits to encode each character of our
string: it cannot use, for example, 3.7 bits to encode a particular character! as a result, Huﬀman is
useless on bitvectors: it will always use 1 bit to encode each bit of the bitvector. No compression.
In this section, the goal is to encode a bitvector in nH0 +o(n) bits while also supporting particular
queries on it in constant time:
Deﬁnition 1.3.1 (zero-order bitvector data structure). Given a bitvector B ∈{0, 1}n, a zero-order
bitvector data structure is a structure using nH0 + o(n) bits and supporting the following queries in
O(1) time (we enumerate positions of B from 1):
1. Random access: extract any bit B[i]
2. Rank: B.rankb(i) is the number of bits equal to b in the preﬁx B[1, . . . , i].
3. Select: B.selectb(i) is the position in B of the i-th bit equal to b.
Example 1.3.2. Let B = 011100010100110011. Then:
• B[5] = 0
• B.rank0(4) = 1
• B.rank0(6) = 3
• B.rank1(8) = 4
• B.select1(4) = 8
• B.select0(3) = 6
We will show a simpliﬁed version of the RRR structure [44] (the name comes from the initials of
the authors). A nice self-contained description is also given in this blog [6]. We will only show how to
solve random access and rank queries (for select, see the book [37, Sec 4.1.1]).

text:
1.3.1
Random access in n bits
First, we show how to pack a bitvector using exactly n bits (plus a constant number of memory words,
which we will ignore for simplicity since they are negligible with respect to the n bits).
While a bit can be stored as an integer, it is not a good idea to allocate n integers to represent
B in your favorite programming language: each integer typically uses 32 bits (4 bytes), so the whole
array uses 32n bits!
The idea is that, precisely because an integer is formed by 32 bits, we can use it to pack 32 bits of
B. To be more general, let us denote with w the size of an integer in our computer. Parameter w is
known in the literature as memory word size. In standard computers, we have w = 32 (or w = 64 if
we use long int). Note that it is reasonable to assume w ∈Ω(log n) because it should be possible to
store any position i ∈[n] pointing inside B using one (or a constant number of) integers. A standard
assumption in the research ﬁeld is w ∈Θ(log n). We will follow this assumption.
The following example shows how we can pack a bitvector, so to use just n bits.
Example 1.3.3. Let B = 011100010100110011. Assume the word size is w = 6. Then, we break B
in n/w blocks of w bits:
B = 011100 010100 110011
We store B as an array B′ of integers of w bits each (i.e. treat each block as a binary integer of w
bits):
B′ = 28, 20, 51
Et voil`a. B′ is a standard array of integers (you can create it in your favorite programming language),
contains exactly the same information as B, and it uses n bits.
Remark 1.3.4. To be precise, the encoding shown in Example 1.3.3 uses n + Θ(w) = n + Θ(log n)
bits because the computer also needs to store the address of B′ in memory and its length.
Those
extra integers however use Θ(w) = Θ(log n) bits so they are negligible with respect to the size n of
the bitvector. To simplify our discussion, in the rest of the notes we will ignore this small overhead.
Be careful, however, that in general it is not always safe to ignore it! (it will be safe in our data
structures).
Remark 1.3.5. In general, w does not divide n so the last block could contain less than w bits. In this
case, just pad B with bits ’0’ until its length is a multiple of w. This adds just O(w) bits of overhead
and we will still be able to solve all our queries.
We use notation (·)2 to indicate an integer written in base 2. Otherwise, integers are expressed in
base 10. For example, (011)2 = 3.
Let’s see how to do random access in constant time, with an example.
Example 1.3.6. Consider again bitvector B of the previous example, divided in blocks:
B = 011100 010100 110011
Stored as the following integer array:
B′ = 28, 20, 51
Assume we want to extract bit B[10](= 1). The idea is that we locate the block containing the 10-th
bit: this is the second block, B′[2] = 20. In general, B[i] is inside the block B′[⌊(i −1)/w⌋+ 1].
Now, we know that our bit B[10] is hidden inside the binary representation of B′[2] = 20. In
particular, B[10] is the 4-th bit in the binary representation of B′[2] = 20 = (010100)2. In general,
B[i] is the bit in position i′ = (i −1) mod w + 1 of B′[⌊(i −1)/w⌋+ 1].
To extract the 4-th bit of B′[2] = 20 = (010100)2, we ﬁrst right-shift this number by w −4 = 2 bits,
i.e. move every bit to the right by 2 positions; the rightmost 2 bits will disappear, and 2 new bits equal
to 0 will appear to the left. Our bit (shown underlined here) will end up at the end of the block of bits:
20 ≫2 = (010100)2 ≫2 = (000101)2 = 5
13

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes
text:
This operation (right shift) is supported in O(1) time in our computers. In general, to move the bit
B[i] at the end of our block B′[⌊(i −1)/w⌋+ 1] we will perform shift B′[⌊(i −1)/w⌋+ 1] ≫w −((i −1)
mod w) −1.
We have only one operation left to do: set to 0 all bits except the rightmost one. This can be done
applying a bitwise AND (symbol &) with mask 1 = (000001)2:
5 & 1 = (000101)2 & (000001)2 = (000001)2 = 1 = B[10]
Also this operation is supported in O(1) time on any computer.
Remark 1.3.7. In general, with almost the same technique we can extract in O(1) time any block of
at most w contiguous bits from B. The extracted block will ﬁt in an integer of w bits. Exercise: show
how to do it.
We obtained a packed bitvector data structure:
Theorem 1.3.8 (Packed bitvector). Any bitvector B ∈{0, 1} can be stored with a data structure of n
bits supporting the extraction of any block B[i, . . . , i + ℓ−1] of ℓ≤w contiguous bits in O(1) time.
We will often use the above theorem to represent eﬃciently packed arrays of integers:
Theorem 1.3.9 (packed integer array). Assume we want to store eﬃciently an array of n integers of
size at most 2b each, with b ≤w (i.e. each integer uses exactly b bits). Then, Theorem 1.3.8 can be
used to store the array in optimal nb bits while supporting random access to any integer in O(1) time.
1.3.2
Encoding a bitvector in nH0 + o(n) bits
Let B ∈{0, 1} be our bitvector. We choose a block size b = ⌈(log2 n)/2⌉= Θ(log n), and divide B
in blocks of b bits each. We call the blocks B1, B2, . . . , Bn/b (again, we assume for simplicity that b
divides n).
Example 1.3.10. Let
B = 010101001110000011111
The bitvector’s length is n = 21 so b = ⌈(log2 n)/2⌉= 3. The division in blocks is the following:

The class Ci of block Bi is simply the number of bits equal to 1 in Bi:

1.3.3
Random access and rank in nH0 + o(n) bits
To extract any bit B[i], we proceed in a similar way as in Example 1.3.6, except that now we replace
w with b since our blocks are made of b bits. To extract B[i], we:
1. Identify which block Bj contains position i. Like in Example 1.3.6, the block number is j =
⌊(i −1)/b⌋+ 1,
2. Extract Cj and Oj,
3. Decompress the block: from (Cj, Oj), obtain the bitvector Bj of length b (packed in a memory
word: note that b ≤w), and
4. Like in Example 1.3.6, extract bit in position i′ = (i −1) mod b + 1 of Bj.
Steps 1 and 4 can be performed in O(1) time, as seen in Example 1.3.6. In step 2, we already know
how to extract Cj in constant time: recall that each class Ci is stored using ⌈log2(b + 1)⌉bits, so we
can use Theorem 1.3.9. We cannot use the same technique for the oﬀsets, because they are encoded
using a variable-length encoding. Let’s see how to extract also any Oj in constant time.
Extracting oﬀsets
In Example 1.3.15, our oﬀsets are stored as the following bitvector: 011000100000 (no separators!).
The idea is to store extra information telling us the position where each oﬀset starts in this bitvector.

two-levels scheme:
1. We group the blocks into macroblocks of b blocks each,

Rank
The idea for supporting B.rank1() is almost the same as for random access.
Instead of absolute
and relative positions in the encoded oﬀsets, we store the rank (number of bits equal to 1) before
each macroblock (excluding the ﬁrst macroblock position) and before each block inside its macroblock
(excluding the ﬁrst block position). The solution for B.rank0() is the same.
In addition, we store a three-dimensional table R pre-computing the answer to any rank query on
any possible bitvector of length b, speciﬁed as a class and oﬀset:
R[Cj][Oj][i] = Bj.rank1(i)

Let’s see how to solve B.rank1(17) (position 17 is underlined). The rank before the macroblock contain-
ing position 17 is 4. Position 17 is inside block B6, and the relative rank before B6 in its macroblock
is 2.
Finally, position 17 is the second inside B6 = 011.
We extract C6 = 2 and O6 = 0, and
use table R to get the rank inside B6: R[2][0][2] = 1. Finally, we add up the three partial ranks:
B.rank1(17) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7.
Table R uses in total at most (b + 1) · 2b · b · ⌈log2(b + 1)⌉= O(√n log2 n log log n) = o(n) bits
(number of classes × number of oﬀsets × number of positions inside a bitvector Bj of length b ×
number of bits to express the result of a rank query on Bj).
To solve B.rank0(), simply observe that B.rank0(i) = i −B.rank1(i).
The solution for query select uses similar ideas, except that we build blocks of variable length
containing a ﬁxed number of bits equal to ’1’. See [37] for the details. We ﬁnally obtained the main
result of the section:
Theorem 1.3.23 (RRR bitvector [44]). Any bitvector B ∈{0, 1}n can be stored in a data structure
of nH0 + o(n) ≤n + o(n) bits supporting random access, rank, and select queries in O(1) time.
Finally, it is not hard to prove that the bit-size of the RRR bitvector data structure built on the
concatenation of two bitvectors B1 and B2 is almost the same as the sum of the sizes of the RRR data
structure built separately on B1 and B2. More in general (prove this as an exercise):

size b = O(log n).
If we ignore the t −1 blocks overlapping the borders of each pair of adja-
cent bitvectors BiBi+1, the analysis of this section shows that the remaining blocks use at most

This result will be useful later when extending access, rank, and select queries on arbitrary strings
and ﬁnally using them to index text in high-order compressed space.
1.4
Sets of integers - the Elias-Fano data structure
The topics of this section are treated more in detail in [37, Sec. 4.4].
Consider the problem of encoding a set of integers S ⊆{0, . . . , n −1} of cardinality |S| = m. As
seen in Section 1.2.1, m log(n/m) + O(m) bits are necessary to encode S. In this section, we show
that m log(n/m) + Θ(m) bits are suﬃcient by showing a data structure of this size supporting several
queries on S.
In Section 1.2.1 we observed that sets of cardinality m over universe {0, . . . , n −1} and bitvectors
of length n with m bis equal to ’1’ are essentially the same objects. In fact (note: in this section the
smallest integer in our sets is 0):
Example 1.4.1. Consider the set S = {3, 7, 8} ⊆[9]. The following bitvector of length 9 with 3 bits
equal to ’1’ is an encoding of this set: 0001000110. The other way round is also true: any bitvector of
length n with m bis equal to ’1’ can be represented as a set of cardinality m on universe {0, . . . , n −1}.
Then, this means that the data structure of the previous section, of size nH0+o(n) = m log(n/m)+
O(m) + o(n) can be used to store also any set of cardinality m on universe {0, . . . , n −1}. Notice that
this space is almost optimal, except for the term o(n). If m ≈n, then this term is indeed small and
can be ignored. On the other hand, if m ≪n then this term is too large! Recall that in our data
structure this extra term is O(n log log n/ log n). This can be much larger than m if the set is very
sparse.
Example 1.4.2. Consider the problem of storing m = 1000 IPv4 addresses. Each such address is
an integer of 32 bits, i.e. n = 232. A naive solution using 32 bits per address would use 32 · 1000 =
32000 bits ≈4 KiB. The information-theoretic lower bound, on the other hand, is approximately
m log(n/m) ≈3 KiB. The solution of the previous section, on the other hand, uses m log(n/m) +
O(n log log n/ log n) + O(m) ≥m log(n/m) + n log log n/ log n ≈6.7 · 108 bits ≈80 MiB! The term
O(n log log n/ log n) = o(n) is negligible only when m ≈n (not in this case).
We now introduce a data structure removing this extra o(n) term.
In the literature, the data
structure is known with the name Elias-Fano (EF), from the name of the researchers that ﬁrst described
these ideas. We present the EF data structure via an example. For simplicity we assume that n and
m are powers of two (but with more care the structure can be described for arbitrary n, m).
Example 1.4.3. We are going to build the Elias-Fano data structure on set {0, 5, 8, 12, 14, 17, 20, 31} ⊆
{0, . . . , 31}. The universe’s size is n = 32, and the set’s cardinality is m = 8. We will ﬁrst write the

this diﬀerence as δ8 = 021 = 001. The ﬁrst diﬀerence is deﬁned as δ1 = p1. The concatenated bits of

We build the data structure of Theorem 1.3.23 on bitvector EF2 = 101010110101001. The Elias-Fano
data structure is the pair EF = (EF1, EF2).
Observe that the bitvector EF2, storing diﬀerences in unary, has m bits equal to ’1’. Also the
number of ’0’ is at most m, because the total number of ’0’ is equal to the diﬀerence between the
largest integer (in the example, (111)2) and (000)2; this diﬀerence is at most m −1 since the integers’
preﬁxes are formed by log m bits each. As a result, EF2 has at most 2m bits. We conclude that the
Elias-Fano data structure EF = (EF1, EF2) uses in total m log(n/m) + Θ(m) bits.
We now show how to access any integer eﬃciently:
Example 1.4.4. Consider the previous example. Suppose we want to extract the 5-th integer 14 =
(01110)2.
The suﬃx (10)2 of this number is explicitly stored in the packed array EF1, so we can
extract it in constant time. To extract the preﬁx 011 of the number, observe that (011)2 = 3 is equal
to the number of ’0’s before the 5-th ’1’ in EF2. In general, the preﬁx of the i-th integer is equal
to EF2.rank0(EF2.select1(i)). Why? because the ’0’s in EF2 store the diﬀerences between adjacent
preﬁxes, so by adding up those diﬀerences we retrieve the preﬁxes.
Once obtained the preﬁx p and suﬃx s of an integer, we combine them in constant time to get the
original integer: (p ≪k) + s, where k = log(n/m) is the length of the suﬃxes. We obtain:
Theorem 1.4.5. The Elias-Fano data structure represents a sorted list of m integers from universe
{0, . . . , n −1} using optimal m log(n/m) + Θ(m) bits of space, and allows retrieving the i-th integer
(random access) in O(1) time.
The Elias-Fano structure actually supports other interesting queries such as membership of integers
in the set (i.e. testing x ∈S), and ﬁnding predecessors (i.e. ﬁnding the largest x ∈S such that x < y,
where y is the query) and successors, all in logarithmic time. Notice that these query times are larger
than the O(1)-query times of the structure of Section 1.3. This is the price we have to pay to remove
the O(n log log n/ log n) term from the space of our data structure! We do not describe these queries
since we will not need them in these notes. Exercise: try to show how to support these queries using
EF = (EF1, EF2).
1.5
The compressed suﬃx array (CSA)
We are ﬁnally ready to show how to compress the suﬃx array (SA) of Section 1.1.3 in nH0 + Θ(n)
bits of space.
We are going to introduce the Compressed Suﬃx Array (CSA) via a running example. Consider
Figure 1.4, showing the suﬃx array of string S = BANANA$.

Remember, from Section 1.1.3, that all we needed to run binary search on the suﬃx array was the
ability to extract any suﬃx in lexicographic order. It follows that, if we have constant-time random
access on F and ψ, then we can solve query count in the same time as in section 1.1.3: O(m log n). We
now show how to compress arrays F and ψ while guaranteeing constant-time random access to them.
1.5.1
Compressing F and ψ and counting patterns
String F contains all characters of S, in alphabetic order. This string is therefore easy to compress.
The idea is to store a bitvector FO (ﬁrst occurrence) marking with a bit ’1’ the ﬁrst occurrence of
each character in F, and build our constant-time rank data structure of Section 1.3 on FO. Then, we
store in array Σ all alphabet characters in alphabetic order (without duplicates), stored using w bits
each (or less; for example, on ASCII alphabet, 7 bits per character suﬃce). Note: array Σ contains all
the alphabet’s characters, so we use for it the same symbol Σ used to denote the alphabet. Finally,
we observe that F[i] = Σ[FO.rank1(i)]. See Figure 1.7 for an example.

Remark 1.5.6. Note that ASCII and UNICODE alphabets are constant-sized (their size does not
depend on the text length n), so in those cases σ = O(1) and the space in the above lemma simpliﬁes
to O(n).

Lemma 1.5.9. Let j = FO.select1(FO.rank1(i)) and c = F[i]. Then:
ψ[i] = ψc[i −j + 1]
Remark 1.5.10. j = FO.select1(FO.rank1(i)) is the position of the bit ’1’ immediately preceding (or

Example 1.5.11. Suppose we want to extract ψ[4]. Then, F[4] = A (we know how to retrieve F[4]

Lemma 1.5.12. The Compressed Suﬃx Array uses nH0+Θ(n)+Θ(σ log n) bits of space and supports
count queries in O(m log n) time.
Remark 1.5.13. Again, ASCII and UNICODE alphabets are constant-sized (their size does not depend
on the text length n), so in those cases σ = O(1) and the CSA uses nH0 + Θ(n) bits of space.
1.5.2
Locating patterns and extracting substrings
We are left to show how to locate all occ occurrences of a pattern P in the indexed string S, i.e. how
to retrieve SA[l], SA[l + 1], . . . , SA[r] where [l, r] is the suﬃx array range of all suﬃxes preﬁxed by P
and occ = l −r + 1 (for instance, in our running example: if P = “AN ′′ then l = 2 and r = 3; if
P = “A′′ then l = 2 and r = 4). With Lemma 1.5.12 we can ﬁnd l and r by binary search in O(m log n)
time and nH0 + Θ(n) bits of space. We now show how to extract any SA[i] in O(log n) time without
changing asymptotically the space usage.
The idea is that function ψ moves us forward in the text by one position. For instance, in our
running example: SA[4] = 2, SA[ψ[4]] = SA[7] = 3, SA[ψ[7]] = SA[3] = 4, etc. Choose ρ = ⌈log n⌉;
parameter ρ is called the sample rate.
We explicitly store SA[i] either if SA[i] mod ρ = 0, or if

SA[i] = SSA[M.rank1(ψk[i])] −k, where k ≥0 is the smallest integer s.t. M[ψk[i]] = 1

Array SSA contains n/ρ + 1 = O(n/ log n) integers of log n bits each, so it uses O(n) bits of space.
Array M occupies n + o(n) bits. Each application of function ψ costs O(1) time, and to extract any
SA[i] we apply ψ at most O(log n) times. It follows that we can extract any SA[i] (i.e. solve locate
queries) in O(log n) time using in total nH0 + Θ(n) bits of space.
Exercise 2
Show how to support query extract(i, ℓ): given integers i, ℓ, extract from the compressed index the
substring S[i, . . . , i + ℓ−1]. The query’s running time must be O(log n + ℓ) and the space must not
increase: nH0 + Θ(n) bits in total. Hint: sample the array SA−1, similarly to how we sampled SA to
support locate.
An index supporting query extract of Exercise 2 is called a self-index because it does not need the
string S to be stored: the index is suﬃcient to solve pattern matching and extracting any substring
of S eﬃciently.
We ﬁnally obtained our ﬁrst full-text compressed self-index:

text:
Theorem 1.5.15. The Compressed Suﬃx Array (CSA) uses nH0 + Θ(n) + Θ(σ log n) bits of space
and supports:
• count queries in O(m log n) time,
• locate queries in O((m + occ) log n) time, and
• extract queries (extract any substring of length ℓof S) in O(log n + ℓ) time.
Remark 1.5.16. Again, ASCII and UNICODE alphabets are constant-sized (their size does not depend
on the text length n), so in those cases σ = O(1) and the CSA uses nH0 + Θ(n) bits of space.
More advanced techniques [25] work for any alphabet size σ and can reduce the space to nHk +
o(n log σ) bits, for an appropriate entropy order k.
1.6
The FM-index
In this section we present an index of size nHk + o(n log σ) bits supporting faster queries than the
CSA. We need two ingredients:
• Wavelet trees (Section 1.6.1): given a string S on arbitrary alphabet, WT(S) is an elegant data
structure supporting rank, select, access, and other queries on S.
• The Burrows-Wheeler transform (Section 1.6.2). BWT(S) is a magic permutation of S with
many interesting properties. In particular:
1. local zero-order entropy compression of BWT(S) is equivalent to high-order entropy com-
pression of S, and
2. count queries on S can be reduced to simple operations on BWT(S) (mainly rank).
The FM-index of S is simply WT(BWT(S)). This data structure (explained in Section 1.6.3)
achieves high-order entropy compression and supports count queries on S. Adding the same additional
structures of Section 1.5.2, we will ﬁnally gain also locate and extract queries.
1.6.1
Wavelet Trees: WT
In this section we generalize the result obtained in Section 1.3 to strings on general alphabets. More
formally, we want to design a data structure satisfying these bounds:
Deﬁnition 1.6.1 (zero-order string data structure). Given a string S ∈Σn, a zero-order string data
structure is a structure using nH0(S) + o(n log σ) bits (where σ = |Σ|) and supporting eﬃciently the
following queries:
1. Random access: extract any symbol S[i]
2. Rank: S.rankc(i) is the number of characters equal to c ∈Σ in the preﬁx S[1, . . . , i].
3. Select: S.selectc(i) is the position in S of the i-th character equal to c ∈Σ.
Wavelet trees, introduced by Grossi et al. [25], are an extremely elegant data structure that permits
to reduce the above (and many other) queries to rank/select/access queries on bitvectors (a problem
that we learned how to solve in Section 1.3).
The wavelet tree depends on a preﬁx-free encoding γ : Σ →{0, 1}+ of the alphabet. In a wavelet
tree, the string is represented as a binary tree with σ leaves. Each internal node has at most two
outgoing edges, labeled ’0’ and ’1’. The concatenated labels of the edges on the path from the root to
the leaf corresponding to character c yield γ(c). Each internal node of the tree stores a bitvector data
structure. We describe the wavelet tree data structure in Example 1.6.2 and Figure 1.9:

Figure 1.9: Wavelet tree of the string mississippi. The i-th level stores the i-th bit in the encoding
of the characters. Since we use a balanced encoding (log σ bits per character), the wavelet tree is
balanced and has height log σ. There are σ leaves, each labeled with a character. Characters in gray
are shown just for clarity but are not explicitly stored in the data structure.
Example 1.6.2. We show how to build the WT of string S = mississippi. We need any preﬁx-free
binary encoding of the alphabet; here we choose γ(i) = 00, γ(m) = 01, γ(p) = 10, γ(s) = 11. Figure
1.9 depicts WT(S). The bitvector in the root is formed by the ﬁrst bit of the binary encodings of
the string’s characters. The root has two outgoing edges, labeled ’0’ and ’1’. We send to child ’0’
all string’s characters whose encoding starts with a ’0’, and to child ’1’ all string’s characters whose
encoding starts with a ’1’. We repeat recursively: in the second level, we extract the second bit of
the encoding of every character and use this bit to decide if the character goes to child ’0’ or ’1’. In
general, in level i (i = 1 is the root) the bitvectors store the i-th bit in the encodings of the characters.
Remark 1.6.3. We do not store characters (shown in gray in the ﬁgure) inside internal nodes. In
Example 1.6.2, characters are shown inside internal nodes only for explanatory purposes.
We now show how to solve access and rank queries on WT(S). We will not need select in these
notes, so we do not show how to solve it (exercise: do it yourself).
We use the following notation: root is the root node of the WT, N.bitvector is the bitvector
associated with the node N, and N.child(b), with b ∈{0, 1} is the node child of N reached following
the edge labeled with bit b from node N. If N is a leaf, N.label is the character stored in the leaf.
Random access
To extract S[i] using WT(S), start from the bitvector root.bitvector at the root of
the wavelet tree. root.bitvector[i] is the ﬁrst bit of γ(S[i]). Note that the bitvector root.child(0).bitvector
in child ’0’ of the root stores the second bit of the encoding of characters in S whose encoding starts
with bit ’0’ (similarly for bitvector root.child(1).bitvector). Let b = root.bitvector[i]. It follows that
the second bit of γ(S[i]) is root.child(b).bitvector[root.bitvector.rankb(i)]. By recursively repeating
this strategy, we ﬁnally reach the leaf of WT(S) labeled with character S[i]. Since we descend the tree
from the root to a leaf and spend only constant time at each level (one access and one rank), running
time is O(|γ(S[i])|) (number of bits of the encoding of S[i]). If the tree is balanced, this running time
is O(log σ).
28

text:
Algorithm 1: access S[i]
N ←root;
while N is not leaf do
B ←N.bitvector;
b ←B[i];
N ←N.child(b);
i ←B.rankb(i);
return N.label;
Rank
Rank does not diﬀer much from access. Let us start from bitvector root.bitvector at the root
of the tree. In order to answer S.rankc(i) using WT(S), ﬁrst count how many characters in S start
with bit b = γ(c)[1] (ﬁrst bit of γ(c)) before position i: i′ = root.bitvector.rankb(i). Then, move at
position i′ of the bitvector root.child(b).bitvector and repeat with the second bit of γ(c). By recur-
sively repeating this strategy for all bits of γ(c), we reach the leaf of WT(S) labeled with character c.
The last rank operation performed at the parent of this leaf yields exactly the value S.rankc(i). The
running time of this procedure is O(|γ(c)|). If the tree is balanced, this running time is O(log σ).
Algorithm 2: rankc(i)
N ←root;
k ←1;
while N is not leaf do
B ←N.bitvector;
b ←γ(c)[k];
i ←B.rankb(i);
N ←N.child(b);
k ←k + 1;
return i;
Space
We now analyze the space of the WT data structure.
Let n′ be the total number of bits in the bitvectors of WT(S). The space used by the wavelet tree
is equal to the space used by the data structure chosen for storing those bitvectors, plus O(σ log n) bits
for the tree topology and the characters stored in the leaves. If we use a balanced encoding (like in
the previous example), then n′ = n log σ. We are going to build the data structure of Theorem 1.3.23
(supporting rank/select/access) on each bitvector in WT(S). Remember that this data structure uses
at most n′ + o(n′) bits. Since n′ = n log σ, we immediately obtain that the data structure WT(S) uses
at most n′ + o(n′) + Θ(σ log n) ≤n log σ + o(n log σ) + Θ(σ log n) bits.
Remarkably, it is possible to improve this bound. Zero-order compression of the bitvectors propa-
gates to zero-order compression of S:
Lemma 1.6.4. Using the RRR bitvector of Theorem 1.3.23 to store the bitvectors of WT(S), the
structure WT(S) uses in total nH0(S) + o(n log σ) + Θ(σ log n) bits of space and answers all queries
in O(log σ) time.
Proof. The proof works inductively: take a node x of the wavelet tree. The node has two children
(left/right). Assuming inductively that the sub-Wavelet trees of the two children achieve zero-order
compression of their respective sequences, it is possible to prove that also the sub-Wavelet tree rooted
in x achieves zero-order compression of its associated sequence. The inductive assumption holds in the
lowest nodes, which contain a RRR bitvector (zero-order compressed). Propagating the induction up
to the root, this will prove the main claim: the sub-Wavelet tree rooted in the root achieves zero-order
compression.
We show a simpliﬁed proof on a Wavelet tree of 2 levels of internal nodes (i.e. ignoring leaves):
the root and two children, each containing a RRR bitvector. Extending the proof to an arbitrary

To sum up, we obtained:
Theorem 1.6.5. Given a string S ∈Σn, the balanced wavelet tree WT(S) described in this section
uses nH0(S) + o(n log σ) + Θ(σ log n) bits of space and supports queries access, rank, and select in
O(log σ) time.
Let S1, . . . , St ∈Σ∗be t strings. What happens when building the Wavelet tree on the concate-
nation S1 . . . St of the t strings? It is not hard to see that the bitvectors on corresponding nodes (i.e.
nodes reached by reading on the tree’s edges the same sequence of bits from the root) of the t Wavelet
trees of S1, . . . , St get concatenated. Since the Wavelet tree has O(σ) nodes, Lemma 1.3.24, directly
gives us:
Lemma 1.6.6. Denote with WT size(S) = |S|H0(S) + o(|S| log σ) + O(σ log |S|) the bit-size of the
Wavelet tree (Theorem 1.6.5) built on string S ∈Σ∗. Let Si ∈Σni, for i = 1, . . . , t be t strings of total
length n = Pt

This lemma will be crucial to analyze the size of the FM-index in Section 1.6.3.
Using another encoding
In the previous paragraphs, we used a balanced encoding assigning log σ bits to each character. The
techniques we described, however, work with any preﬁx-free encoding of the alphabet.
Even if in the FM-index of Section 1.6.3 we will use a balanced encoding (so you can safely ignore
this paragraph), it is interesting to see what happens when other encodings are used.
The Huﬀman encoding represents an interesting case: with this encoding, the total number of bits
stored in the bitvectors of the wavelet tree is at most n(H0 + 1). This number is compressed, even if
we do not use zero-order compressed bitvectors inside the internal nodes of the tree!
Example 1.6.7. Consider again string S = mississippi.
Use the Huﬀman encoding γ(i) = 01,
γ(m) = 001, γ(p) = 000, γ(s) = 1. Figure 1.10 depicts WT(S) with the shape of this encoding.

proportional to the frequency of its label. This means that if we access S[i] at a uniform position i,
on average we traverse H0 + 1 levels of the wavelet tree. On average, this is less than the log σ levels
that we always traverse in balanced wavelet trees (but it can be larger than log σ in the worst case).

1.6.2
The Burrows-Wheeler Transform: BWT
In 1994, Michael Burrows and David Wheeler [9] introduced the following string permutation, today
known as the Burrows-Wheeler transform. In this section we will assume that the string S ∈Σn ends
with a special character $ being alphabetically smaller than all the other characters and not appearing
anywhere else in S.
1. Sort all circular rotations of S. This yields a matrix: the BWT matrix of S. See Figure 1.11 for
an example.
2. Concatenate in a new string BWT(S) the last character of each rotation of S, in sorted order.

Figure 1.11: BWT matrix of S = mississippi$. The rows of the BWT matrix contain all and only the
circular rotations of S. The last column L (in green) is BWT(S) = ipssm$pissii. We have already
encountered the ﬁrst column F = $iiiimppssss in Section 1.5.
As we will see, the BWT possesses several remarkable properties which boost compression and
enable indexing. At the core of most properties, stands the LF property (see Figure 1.12):
Theorem 1.6.8. LF property.
The i-th occurrence of c ∈Σ on column L (i.e.
L[L.selectc(i)])
corresponds to the i-th occurrence of c on column F (i.e. F[F.selectc(i)]). In other words, these two
characters correspond to the same position in the original string S.
It is not hard to prove the LF-property. The key idea is to think about the ordering of equal
characters on the two columns.
In column F, equal characters (for example, the ’i’s) are ordered
according to the lexicographic order of the text suﬃxes that follow them. For example, in Figure 1.11
the second ’i’ in column F is followed by ppi$, which is smaller than the suﬃx ssissippi$ which follows
the fourth ’i’ in column F. It is easy to see that the same property holds for equal characters on the L
column. For example, in Figure 1.11 the second ’i’ in column L is followed by ppi$, which is smaller
than the suﬃx ssissippi$ which follows the fourth ’i’. It follows that occurrences of any c ∈Σ appear
in the same order in the F and L columns.
As shown in Figure 1.12, the LF property yields a function (in fact, a permutation) LF : [n] →[n].
Note that LF = ψ−1 (ψ is the function we deﬁned in Section 1.5 to compress the suﬃx array). In fact,
the FM-index we are going to describe in Section 1.6 is just another way to compress the suﬃx array!
A ﬁrst important consequence of the LF mapping is that BWT(S) can be inverted. As displayed in
Figure 1.13, by repeatedly applying function LF starting from position 1 (note: L[1] always contains
the character preceding $), we reconstruct S backwards.
Note that F can be obtained by sorting
BWT(S), so all we need to reconstruct S is just BWT(S).

In the next section we will use two additional properties of BWT(S):
1. In the BWT matrix, the string’s suﬃxes are sorted lexicographically. This suggests that it should
be possible to use BWT(S) to solve pattern matching queries (for the same reason we are able
to solve this task on the CSA of Section 1.5).
33
