text:
4.8.1
Karp-Rabin’s algorithm
Rabin’s hashing is the main tool we will use to solve the problem. First, we note that the technique
itself yields a straightforward solution, even though in O(n) space. In the next section we reﬁne this
solution to use O(log n) space.
Suppose we have processed the stream up to x1, . . . , xi (i ≥n) and that we know the hash values
κq,z(xi−n+1xi−n+2 . . . xi) and κq,z(y). By simply comparing these two hash values (in constant time)
we can discover whether or not the patter occurs in the last n stream’s characters. The crucial step
is to update the hash of the stream when a new element xi+1 arrives. This is not too hard: we have
to subtract character xi−n+1 from the stream’s hash and add the new character xi+1. This can be
achieved as follows:
κq,z(xi−n+2xi−n+2 . . . xi+1) = (κq,z(xi−n+1xi−n+2 . . . xi) −xi−n+1 · zn−1) · z + xi+1
mod q
The value zn−1 mod q can be pre-computed, so the above operation takes constant time. Note that,
since we need to access character xi−n+1, at any time the algorithm must keep the last n characters
seen in the stream, thereby using O(n) space.
Analysis
From Section 4.2, the probability that the pattern collides with a stream’s substring is at most n/q.
By union bound, the probability that the pattern collides with at least one substring is mn/q ≤m2/q.
We want this to happen with small (inverse polynomial probability): this can be achieved by choosing
a prime q in the range [mc+2, 2 · mc+2], for any constant c. Such a prime (and therefore the output of
Rabin’s hash function) can be stored in O(log m) bits = O(1) words. We obtain:
Theorem 4.8.2. The Karp-Rabin algorithm solves the pattern matching problem in the streaming
model using O(n) words of memory and O(1) delay. The correct solution is returned with high (inverse-
polynomial) probability 1 −m−c, for any constant c ≥1 chosen at initialization time.
There exist also deterministic algorithms with O(1) delay and O(n) space. However, as we show in
the next section, Karp-Rabin’s randomization enables an exponentially more space-eﬃcient solution.
4.8.2
Porat&Porat’s algorithm
The big disadvantage of Karp-Rabin’s algorithm is that it uses too much memory: O(n) words per
pattern. In this section we study an algorithm described by Benny Porat and Ely Porat in [41] that
uses just O(log n) words of space and has O(log n) delay per stream’s character 4. Other algorithms
are able to reduce the delay to the optimal O(1) (see [7]). For simplicity, assume that n is a power of
two: n = 2e for some e ≥0. The algorithm can be generalized to any n in a straightforward way. The
overall idea is to:
• Keep the hashes of all 1 + e = 1 + log2 n preﬁxes of y whose length is a power of two.
• Keep the occurrences of those preﬁxes of y on the stream, working in e levels: level 0 ≤i < e
stores the set Wi of all occurrences of the preﬁx y[1, 2i] in the window containing the last 2i+1
stream’s characters. Using a clever argument based on string periodicity, show that this set can
be “compressed” in just O(1) words of space per level (O(log n) space in total).
• When a new stream character xj arrives:
– If it is an occurrence of y1, insert j in W0.
4Note that, no matter how large n is, O(log n) words will ﬁt in cache. O(log n) delay in cache is by far more desirable
than O(1) delay in RAM: the former is hundreds of times faster than the latter.
80

using ﬁngerprints). If this is the case, then insert p′ in Wi+1 (i.e. promote it to the next

Crucially, in the last step we need to compute the ﬁngerprint of a whole window. We will show
that this can be done using just logarithmic space.
Figure 4.5 depicts two steps of the algorithm: before and after the arrival of a new stream character.
Algorithm 4 implements one step of the above procedure (hiding details such as compression of the

Compressing the occurrences
We have log n levels, however this is not suﬃcient to claim that the algorithm uses O(log n) space: in
each level i, there could be up to 2i occurrences of the pattern’s preﬁx y[1, 2i]. In this paragraph we
show that all the occurrences in a window can be compressed in just O(1) words of space.
The key observation is that, in each level, we store occurrences of the pattern’s preﬁx of length
K = 2i in a window of size 2K = 2i+1. Now, if there are at least three such occurrences, then at least
two of them must overlap. But these are occurrences of the same string y[1, 2i], so if they overlap then
the string must be periodic. Finally, if the string is periodic then all its occurrences in the window
must be equally-spaced: we have an occurrence every p positions, for some integer p (a period of the
string). Then, all t occurrences Wi = {r1, r2, . . . , rt} in the window can be encoded in just O(1) space
by storing the ﬁrst occurrence r1, the number t of occurrences, and the period p. This representation
is also easy to update (in constant time) upon insertion of new occurrences to the right (which must
follow the same rule) and removal of an occurrence to the left. We now formalize this reasoning.
Deﬁnition 4.8.3 (Period of a string). Let S be a string of length K. We say that S has period p if
and only if S[i] = S[i + p] for all 1 ≤i ≤K −p.
Example 4.8.4. The string S = abcabcabcabca, of length K = 13, has periods 3, 6, 9, 12.
81

Figure 4.5: Each colored dot at level i represents an occurrence of a preﬁx of length 2i of the pattern
(underlined with corresponding color). (A) Suppose that the last stream’s character (’b’, underlined)
has just arrived. Since the new character ’b’ matches the pattern’s preﬁx of length 20 = 1, at level 0
we store the corresponding occurrence. In each level, some of the stored occurrences are candidates
that could be promoted to the next level: these are the leftmost occurrences at levels i = 0 and i = 1.
None of these occurrences can be promoted since they are not occurrences of a preﬁx of length 2i+1
of the pattern: the leftmost occurrence at level 0 is not an occurrence of “ba” and the occurrence
at level 1 is not an occurrence of “baab”. (B) A new stream character (’a’, underlined) has arrived.
Some occurrences of ﬁgure (A) exit their window and get deleted: the leftmost red (level 0) and green
(level 1) occurrences of ﬁgure (A). Now, in ﬁgure (B) three occurrences are candidates that could be
promoted to the next level. The occurrence at level 0 (red) is indeed an occurrence of “ba”, therefore it
is promoted to level 1. The occurrence at level 2 (blue) is not an occurrence of “baabbaab”, therefore
it is not promoted. Finally, the leftmost occurrence at level 3 (orange) is promoted. Since this is the
last level, we found a pattern occurrence.
Theorem 4.8.5 (Wilf’s theorem). Any string having periods p, q and length at least p + q −gcd(p, q)

also has gcd(p, q) as a period.
Example 4.8.6. Consider the string above: S = abcabcabcabca. The string has periods 6, 9 (with
gcd(6, 9) = 3) and has length 13 > 6 + 9 −3 = 12. Wilf’s theorem can be used to deduce that the string
must also have period gcd(6, 9) = 3.
Wilf’s theorem can be used to prove the following:
Lemma 4.8.7. Let P be a string of length K, and S be a string of length 2K. If P occurs in S at
positions r1 < r2 < · · · < rt, with t ≥3, then rj+1 = rj + p, where p = r2 −r1.
The lemma provides a compressed representation for all the occurrences Wi = {r1, r2, . . . , rt} in
the window at each level i: just record (r1, t, p). This representation is easy to update in constant
time when r1 is removed from Wi and when a new occurrence rt+1 (greater than rt) is inserted into
Wi (these are the only two updates we perform on the sets Wi).
Updating the ﬁngerprints
The last thing to show is how to eﬃciently compute wi = κq,z(x[j −2i+1, j −1]) at level i (needed
at Line 1 of the algorithm), that is, the ﬁngerprint of the whole window when the ﬁrst occurrence
stands at the beginning of the window: r1 = j −2i+1. Consider the window Wi at level i, and the two
smallest positions r1, r2 ∈Wi. Let x = x[1, j −1] be the current stream. We keep in memory three
ﬁngerprints (see Figure 4.6):
(A) κq,z(x): the ﬁngerprint of the whole stream.
(B) κq,z(x[r1, r2 −1]): the ﬁngerprint of the stream’s substring standing between r1 (included) and
r2 (excluded), whenever Wi contains at least two positions.
(C) κq,z(x[1, r1 −1]): the ﬁngerprint of the stream’s preﬁx ending at r1 −1, whenever Wi contains
at least one position.

and O(log n) delay. The correct solution is returned with high (inverse-polynomial) probability 1−m−c,
for any constant c ≥1 chosen at initialization time.
Breslauer and Galil in [7] reduced the delay to O(1) while still using O(log n) words of space.
4.8.3
Extension to approximate pattern matching
We describe a modiﬁcation of Porat&Porat’s algorithm that allows ﬁnding all stream occurrences
xi,n = xi . . . xi+n−1 of a pattern y = y1 . . . yn such that DH(xi,n, y) ≤k for any parameter k, where
DH is the Hamming distance between strings:
DH(A, B) =
n
X
i=1
(A[i]̸ = B[i])
In the next paragraphs we introduce a sketch for Hamming distance between strings of the same
length n. Then, we will apply this sketch to the approximate pattern matching problem on streams.
Diﬀerently from the sketch of Section 4.4 (which can estimate dH(A, B) = DH(A, B)/n with bounded
error and bounded failure probability), the technique that we will describe here will allow us to compute
(with high probability of success) the exact Hamming distance DH(A, B) between two strings, only
if this distance is below some threshold (maximum distance) k (otherwise, we simply report that
DH(A, B) ≥k). The sketch’ space usage will depend on k and n.
For simplicity, we ﬁrst describe the sketch and pattern matching algorithm for k = 1, i.e. zero or
one mismatch between the pattern and the stream. Then, we will extend the idea to arbitrary k.
A sketch for Hamming distance between strings of the same length
Let x, y be two strings
of length n. For some parameters 1 ≤i, d ≤n, let yi:d be deﬁned as
yi:d = yi yi+d yi+2d . . .
In other words, yi:d is the sub-string of y built by extracting every other d-th character from y, starting
from character yi. We call yi:d a shift of y.
Clearly, x = y if and only if xi:d = yi:d for all i = 1, . . . , d. Assume now that DH(x, y) = 1. Then,
note that the error is captured by exactly one of the d shifts: there exists one i′ ∈[1, d] such that
xi′:d̸ = yi′:d, and xi:d = yi:d for all i̸ = i′.
Example 4.8.9. Let x =abracadabra and y =abbacadabra, with DH(x, y) = 1 (the mismatch is
underlined). Pick d = 2 and consider the two shifts (per string) x1:2 = arcdba, x2:2 =baaar, y1:2 =
abcdba, y2:2 =baaar. Then:
• x1:2̸ = y1:2
• x2:2 = y2:2
What if DH(x, y) = k > 1? Then, the number of shifts i′ such that xi′:d̸ = yi′:d could be smaller
than k (but never larger). Notice that this happens precisely when the distance |j′ −j| between two
mismatches xj̸ = yj and xj′̸ = yj′ is a multiple of d.
Example 4.8.10. Let x =abracadabra and y =abbacaaabra, with DH(x, y) = 2 (the two mismatches
are underlined). Pick d = 2 and consider the two shifts (per string) x1:2 = arcdba, x2:2 =baaar, y1:2 =
abcaba, y2:2 =baaar. Then:
• x1:2̸ = y1:2
• x2:2 = y2:2
In particular, the Hamming distance is 2 but only one of the two shifts generates a mismatch. This
happens because the two mismatches are distanced 4 positions, which is a multiple of d = 2.

text:
It is easy to see that the above issue does not happen if d does not divide the distance between the
two mismatches.
Example 4.8.11. Let x =abracadabra and y =abbacaaabra, with DH(x, y) = 2 (the two mismatches
are underlined). Pick d = 3 and consider the three shifts (per string) x1:3 =aadr, x2:3 =bcaa, x3:3 =rab,
y1:3 =aaar, y2:3 =bcaa, y3:3 =bab. Then:
• x1:3̸ = y1:3
• x2:3 = y2:3
• x3:3̸ = y3:3
Now, two shifts generates a mismatch.
This property can be summarized in a corollary:
Corollary 4.8.12. Let x, y be two strings of length n, and consider their d shifts xi:d, yi:d for i =
1, . . . , d. Then xi:d̸ = yi:d for at least two values i ∈[1, d] if and only if (i) DH(x, y) > 1 and (ii) there
exist two mismatches whose distance |j −j′| is not a multiple of d.
Consider the distance |j −j′| between (the positions of) any two mismatches between x and y.
Consider moreover the smallest ⌈log2 n⌉prime numbers P = {p1, p2, . . . , p⌈log2 n⌉}. Clearly, |j −j′|
cannot be a multiple of all numbers in P: this would imply that |j−j′| ≥Q
p∈P p > n. This immediately
implies the following corollary, which directly gives us a small sketch for deciding DH(x, y) ≤1:
Corollary 4.8.13. Let x, y be two strings of length n.
Let P = {p1, p2, . . . , p⌈log2 n⌉} be the set
containing the ⌈log2 n⌉smallest prime numbers. Then, DH(x, y) > 1 if and only if there exists d ∈P
such that xi:d̸ = yi:d for at least two values i ∈[1, d].
The corresponding sketch is:
sketch(x) = ⟨κ(xi:d)⟩d∈P,i∈[d]
Where κ is Rabin’s hash function (for an opportune prime modulo guaranteeing low collision
probability, see Section 4.2). Notice that P
d∈P d = O(log2 n/ log log n) = O(log2 n) (we are going to
use the latter bound for clarity, even if it is less precise), so the sketch uses O(log2 n) words of space.
Given sketch(x) and sketch(y), we can decide DH(x, y) ≤1 with the following algorithm:
Algorithm 5: Decide if DH(x, y) ≤1 for two strings x, y ∈Σn
foreach d ∈P do
c ←0;
foreach i ∈[d] do
if κ(xi:d)̸ = κ(yi:d) then
c ←c + 1;
if c > 1 then
return “DH(x, y) > 1”;
return “DH(x, y) ≤1”;
As an exercise, try to modify Algorithm 5 so that it returns DH(x, y) if DH(x, y) ≤1.
Approximate pattern matching on streams
It is not hard to use the above sketch in the stream-
ing scenario, where |x| = m > n: create |P| copies of the stream x 5 and, for each d ∈P, divide the
5Note: this means that, when the stream character xj arrives, we just duplicate it |P| times. Each copy is appended
to the corresponding copy of the stream. This means that the original stream is read only once (i.e. we are not reading
the stream |P| times, which we cannot do in our setting!).

text:
corresponding copy of the stream into d sub-streams xi xi+d xi+2d . . . , for all i ∈[d]. Using the Po-
rat&Porat’s algorithm, we then decide if yj:d matches a suﬃx of xi:d, for all possible values of i, j ∈[d].
By the discussion in the previous section, this is enough to decide if y and the last n characters of the
stream x match up to Hamming distance 1, with high probability. Note that we run O(log3 n) parallel
instances of Porat&Porat’s algorithm. Since each instance uses O(log n) words of memory, we obtain:
Theorem 4.8.14. Let m be the stream’s length and n ≤m be the pattern’s length. The above modiﬁ-
cation of Porat&Porat’s algorithm ﬁnds all occurrences of the pattern at Hamming distance at most 1
in the stream using O(log4 n) words of memory and O(log4 n) delay. The correct solution is returned
with high probability.
Extension to any threshold k of mismatches
We can extend the above idea to k ≥1 mismatches.
Take x, y ∈Σn, and assume that DH(x, y) > k. Consider any group of k + 1 mismatches between x
and y, at positions i1 < · · · < ik+1. We want to ﬁnd a prime number d ≥k + 1 such that d does not
divide ij −ij′, for all 1 ≤j′ < j ≤k + 1. Then, we are guaranteed that xi:d̸ = yi:d for at least k + 1
shifts i, since no pair of mismatches ij, ij′ can fall in the same shift (which would imply that d divides
|ij −ij′|). Note: we require that d is greater than or equal to k + 1 because the number of shifts is d
and we need at least k + 1 distinct mismatching shifts of x and y in order to detect Hamming distance
> k.
The integer d does not divide ij −ij′ for all 1 ≤j′ < j ≤k + 1 if and only if d does not divide
their product Q
1≤j′<j≤k+1(ij −ij′) ≤n(k+1)2. We will surely ﬁnd such an integer d in the set Pk of
the smallest log2 n(k+1)2 = O(k2 log n) prime numbers larger than or equal to k + 1.
The new sketch is:
sketch(x) = ⟨κ(xi:d)⟩d∈Pk,i∈[d]
From here, to simplify notation we use the ˜O notation:
Deﬁnition 4.8.15 (tilde-O notation). ˜On(t) denotes any complexity in O(t·polylog n), where polylog n =
(log n)O(1) is any polynomial of log n of constant degree. When n is clear from the context, we just
write ˜O(t).
In other words, the tilde-O notation hides polylogarithmic factors. In streaming scenarios this
notation makes sense, since polylog n is negligible with respect to the input size n and it is more useful
to focus on the main complexity term, which in this particular scenario depends on k.
Since |Pk| ∈˜O(k2) and the sketch contains d hashes for every d ∈Pk, the size of the sketch is of
˜O(k4) words.
The algorithm for deciding DH(x, y) ≤k works exactly as in the case k = 1, except that we now
look for a prime d ∈Pk such that xi:d̸ = yi:d for at least k + 1 values of i ∈[1, d]:
Algorithm 6: Decide if DH(x, y) ≤k for two strings x, y ∈Σn
foreach d ∈Pk do
c ←0;
foreach i ∈[d] do
if κ(xi:d)̸ = κ(yi:d) then
c ←c + 1;
if c > k then
return “DH(x, y) > k”;
return “DH(x, y) ≤k”;
As an exercise, try to modify the above algorithm so that it returns the exact value of DH(x, y) if
DH(x, y) ≤k.

In the streaming scenario, for every d ∈Pk and every i, j ∈[1, d] we need to run a parallel instance
of the Porat&Porat’s algorithm to check if yj:d matches a suﬃx of xi:d: this is a cubic (in |Pk|) number
of instances of Porat&Porat’s algorithm. We obtain:
Theorem 4.8.16. The above modiﬁcation of Porat&Porat’s algorithm ﬁnds all occurrences of the
pattern at Hamming distance at most k in the stream using ˜O(k6) words of memory and ˜O(k6) delay.
The correct solution is returned with high probability.
The above bounds can be easily improved with slightly more soﬁsticated techniques.
In their
original article [41], Porat and Porat describe a more eﬃcient solution using ˜O(k3) space and ˜O(k2)
delay. Cliﬀord et al. in [11] improved this to ˜O(k2) space and ˜O(
√
k) delay. These bounds were further
improved in [12] to ˜O(k) space and ˜O(
√
k) delay. The authors of [12] prove that the latter bounds are
optimal (up to poly-logarithmic factors).
4.9
Counting ones in a window: Datar-Gionis-Indyk-Motwani’s
algorithm
The DGIM algorithm [15] addresses the following basic problem. Consider an input stream of m bits.
What is the sum of the last ¯m ≤m elements of the stream?
This problem models several practical situations in which storing the entire stream is not practical,
but we may be interested in counting the number of interesting events among the last ¯m ≤m events.
Example 4.9.1. Consider a stream of bank transactions for a given person; we mark a transaction
with a 1 if it exceeds a given threshold (say, 50 euros) and with a 0 otherwise. Then, knowledge about
the number of 1s in the last ¯m transactions can be used to detect if the credit card’s owner has changed
behaviour (for example, has started spending much more than usual) and detect potential frauds (e.g.
credit card has been cloned).
It is easy to see that an exact solution requires m bits of space (i.e.
the entire stream).
For
any 0 < ϵ ≤1, the DGIM algorithm uses O(ϵ−1 log2 m) bits of space and returns a multiplicative
(1 + ϵ)-approximation (with certainty: DGIM is a deterministic algorithm).
DGIM works as follows. Let B = ⌈1/ϵ⌉. We group the stream’s bits in groups G1, G2, . . . , Gt that
must satisfy the following rules:
1. Each Gi begins and ends with a 1-bit.
2. Between two adjacent groups Gi, Gi+1 there are are only 0-bits, i.e. the stream is of the form
0m0 · G1 · 0m1 · G2 · · · · · Gt · 0mt for some m0, . . . , mt ≥0.
3. Each Gi contains 2k 1-bits, for some k ≥0.
4. For any 1 ≤i < t, if Gi contains 2k 1-bits, then Gi+1 contains either 2k or 2k−1 1-bits.
5. For each k except the largest one, the number Zk of groups containing 2k 1-bits satisﬁes B ≤
Zk ≤B + 1 (note that these groups must be adjacent).
For the largest k, we only require
Zk ≤B + 1.
See Figure 4.11 for an example.

Figure 4.11: DGIM with parameter B = 1. The head of the stream (the most recent element) is the
rightmost bit. For each k, there are at least B = 1 groups containing 2k 1-bits, and at most B + 1 = 2
groups containing 2k 1-bits.
4.9.1
Updates
It is easy to see how to maintain the rules when a new bit arrives. If the bit is equal to 0, then nothing
has to be done. If the bit is equal to 1, then:
1. Create a new group with the new bit.
2. If there are B + 2 groups containing 20 = 1 1-bits, merge the two leftmost such groups so now
there are B groups containing one 1-bit. This creates a new group containing 21 = 2 1-bits.
3. Repeat with the groups containing 2i 1-bits, for i = 1, 2, . . . .
It is easy to see that one update step takes O(log m) worst-case time using doubly linked lists (this
time is the delay of the algorithm). Deﬁne a global list L = ℓq ↔ℓq−1 ↔· · · ↔ℓ1. Element ℓi
contains all the groups with 2i 1-bits and is itself a doubly-linked list: ℓi = Gj1 ↔· · · ↔Gjs, where
Gj1, . . . , Gjs are all the groups (listed from left to right in the stream) containing 2i 1-bits. Each group
Gj is simply a pair of integers Gj = (left, right): the leftmost and rightmost positions of the group
in the stream. For each linked list ℓi, we store its head, tail, number of stream’s bits contained in its
groups, and number of ’1’-bits contained in its groups. Then, ﬁnding the leftmost two groups in a
given ℓi, merging them, and moving the merged group to the end of ℓi+1 takes O(1) time. Overall, an
update takes therefore O(q) = O(log m) time.
Even better, updates take O(1) amortized time. To see this, suppose that a particular update
increases Zk by one unit (recall that Zk is the number of groups containing 2k 1-bits). But then, this
means that before that update Zk′ = B + 1 for all k′ < k. In turn, this conﬁguration required 2k −1
previous updates, which added to the new update yields 2k updates in total. This shows that only one
over 2k updates costs k: the amortized cost is therefore at most P∞
k=1 k/2k = O(1).
4.9.2
Space and queries
The algorithm uses in total O(ϵ−1 log2 m) bits of memory: each group uses O(log m) bits, and there
are at most B + 1 = O(ϵ−1) groups containing 2k 1-bits, for each k = 0, . . . , log m. The lists’ pointers
also use asymptotically this space.
A query is speciﬁed by an integer ¯m ≤m (the window size); our goal is to return the number of
1-bits contained in the most recent ¯m bits of the stream. To solve a query, we simply ﬁnd all the groups
intersecting with (i.e. containing at least one of) the last ¯m stream’s bits, and return the total number
of 1-bits they contain. This can be implemented by navigating the main list ℓ1 →ℓ2 →· · · →ℓi until
the number of bits contained in cells ℓ1, ℓ2, . . . , ℓi exceeds (or becomes equal to) ¯m. Then, we enter the
cell ℓi and start navigating the linked list contained in ℓi until ﬁnding the leftmost group overlapping
the last ¯m stream’s characters. Finally, we add up all ’1’ bits contained in the cells ℓ1, ℓ2, . . . , ℓi−1

and in the groups scanned in the last cell ℓi. The total running time is O(ϵ−1 + log ¯m). Finally, if ¯m
is ﬁxed then it is easy to see that queries take O(1) time: at any time, we keep in memory only the
groups overlapping with the last ¯m stream’s bits (together with the total number of 1-bits that they
contain). This also reduces the total space usage to O(ϵ−1 log2 ¯m) bits.
4.9.3
Approximation ratio
Next, we analyze the approximation ratio of the algorithm. Consider Figure 4.12, corresponding to
the worst-case approximation ratio.

4.10
Estimating frequencies on a stream
Suppose we observe a stream of m integers x1, x2, x3, . . . , xm, each with domain xi ∈[1, n]. For any
y ∈[1, n] deﬁne the frequency of y as the number of times y appears in the stream:
fy = |{i : xi = y}|
In this section and in and the following we tackle a class of problems related with estimating
particular functions of the frequency vector (f1, . . . , fn). In this section, we discuss how to estimate
each individual frequency fy. Then, in the next sections we move to frequency moments. The k-th
frequency moment, for k ≥0, is deﬁned as follows:
Fk =
X

If we take 00 = 0, then F0 = |{x1, . . . , xm}| is simply the number of distinct elements in the stream.
Counting distinct elements in a stream is probably the most emblematic problem in the ﬁeld, and it
is tackled in Section 4.11.
Quantity F1, instead, corresponds simply to the stream’s length: F1 = P
y∈[1,n] fy = m. While it
is trivial to compute F1 exactly on a stream using O(log m) bits of space, using asymptotically less
space is not trivial at all. In Section 4.12 we will show an algorithm estimating F1 in O(log log m) bits
of space.
The second-order frequency moment, F2 = P
y∈[1,n] f 2
y , is also called the surprise number of the
stream since it reﬂects how unbalanced the frequencies in the stream are: the more uneven the fre-
quencies are, the larger F2 is. Estimating this quantity ﬁnds unexpected deep applications in other
contexts, such as estimating the size of table joins in relational databases, and sketches for dimension-
ality reduction under the Euclidean distance. These topics are covered in Section 4.13.

4.10.2
Count-Min sketch
Count-Min sketch uses less asymptotic space than the sampling-based sketch described in the previous
section, and additionally oﬀers a one-sided error guarantee. As we now show, the estimator returned
by Count-Min sketch always satisﬁes ˜fy ≥fy. In addition, with probability at least 1 −δ the bound
˜fy ≤fy + ϵ · m will hold.
Deﬁnition of the sketch
The Count-Min sketch is a matrix of (non-negative) integers CM ∈Nt×s,
initialized with all entries equal to zero. The size s × t of the matrix determines the error rate and
the success probability, and will be determined later. Each row is associated with a universal hash
function (see Section 2.3.2) hj : [1, n] →[1, s], for j = 1, . . . , t.
Insertion of an element in the sketch
To insert a stream element x in the sketch, we perform
the following operation:
CM[j, hj(x)] ←CM[j, hj(x)] + 1, for all j = 1, . . . , t.
In other words, in the j-th row we increment by one unit the entry at column hj(x).
Estimating a frequency
Observe that, by the way we deﬁned the sketch, the counter CM[j, hj(y)]
is incremented each time an occurrence of y is seen in our stream (for each row j = 1, . . . t). In fact, if
the stream contains only one distinct integer y, then CM[j, hj(y)] = fy is exactly the frequency of y,
for any row j = 1, . . . , t. Problems start occurring when the stream contains more than one distinct
integer, due to hash collisions. Assume there is an element y′̸ = y such that hj(y) = hj(y′), for some
j ∈[1, t]. Then, CM[j, hj(y)] ≥fy + fy′ (the inequality is strict if even more integers collide with y
and y′).
Due to hash collisions, we can therefore only assume CM[j, hj(y)] ≥fy for every j = 1, . . . , t.
Then, our best estimate for fy is the minimum of those estimates:
˜fy = min{CM[j, hj(y)] : j = 1, . . . , t}
As observed above, ˜fy ≥fy is always true. The next goal is to show an upper bound as well. Fix
a row j ∈[1, t]. We study the probability that CM[j, hj(y)] > fy + ϵ · m, for some error ϵ > 0.
Deﬁne the boolean indicator 1j
x,y to be equal to 1 if and only if hj(x) = hj(y), i.e. if x and y collide
through hash function hj. Let S = {x1, . . . , xm} denote the set of distinct elements appearing in the
stream. The expected value of CM[j, hj(y)] can be written as:
E[CM[j, hj(y)]]

text:
P(CM[j, hj(y)] −fy ≥2m/s) ≤1/2
Choose any desired error ϵ > 0. By setting 2m/s = ϵ · m, we obtain:
Lemma 4.10.3. By choosing the number of columns in the CM Sketch to be s = ⌈2ϵ−1⌉, for any row
j ∈[1, t] it holds that:
P(CM[j, hj(y)] ≥fy + ϵ · m) ≤1/2
What is the probability that ˜fy ≥fy + ϵ · m? By deﬁnition, ˜fy is the minimum of CM[j, hj(y)], for
all rows j = 1, . . . , t. Then, if the minimum ˜fy exceeds fy +ϵ·m, it means that CM[j, hj(y)] ≥fy +ϵ·m
for all j = 1, . . . , t. Since the functions h1, . . . , ht are independent, we apply Lemma 4.10.3 and obtain
that
P( ˜fy ≥fy + ϵ · m) ≤(1/2)t
We want this probability to be (1/2)t = δ, for any desired failure probability δ > 0. Solving in t,
we ﬁnally obtain that by choosing t = ⌈log2(1/δ)⌉our estimate ˜fy satisﬁes fy ≤˜fy ≤fy + ϵ · m with
probability at least 1 −δ. Assume that each hj can be evaluated in constant time. Putting everything
together we obtain:
Theorem 4.10.4. Choose any desired error rate ϵ > 0 and failure probability δ > 0. The CM Sketch
uses O(ϵ−1 log(1/δ)) words of space and, for any y ∈[1, n], returns an estimate ˜fy such that:
• ˜fy ≥fy
• P( ˜fy ≤fy + ϵ · m) ≥1 −δ
Frequency estimation queries are supported in O(log(1/δ)) time.
When a new stream element
arrives, the CM sketch can be updated in O(log(1/δ)) time.
Note that this error is one-sided from above: the sketch never under-estimates fy, but it could
over-estimate it. Recall that in the previous subsection we obtained a double-sided error with larger
space. It follows that Count-Min sketch is strictly better than sampling.
4.10.3
Misra-Gries sketch
Interestingly, the problem can be solved also without randomization. Here we describe the Misra-Gries
sketch, from [33]. The sketch provides a one-side error from below: it never over-estimates fy, but it
could under-estimate it (the opposite of the CM Sketch).
Deﬁnition of the sketch
The sketch is a dictionary H : [1, n] →N (a set of key-value pairs)
that associates frequencies (natural numbers) to the stream’s integers. We require H to implement a
function: at any point in time for any x ∈[1, n] the dictionary will contain at most one key-value pair
(x, f), for some f ∈N. We denote with H[x] such a frequency f. If the dictionary does not contain
any pair of the form (x, f), we deﬁne H[x] = 0 (in this way, H implements a complete function). We
abuse notation and write x ∈H to indicate an integer x ∈[1, n] such that the dictionary contains a
key-value pair of the form (x, f), for some f ∈N.
We denote with |H| the number of pairs (x, f) ∈[1, n] × N stored in H. Initially, the dictionary is
empty (|H| = 0). We require the dictionary to use O(|H|) words of space.
Increments on H[x], denoted as H[x] ←H[x] + 1, have the following natural meaning. If x /∈H,
then H[x] ←H[x] + 1 inserts the pair (x, 1) in H. Otherwise, the operation replaces the (unique) pair
(x, f) in H with (x, f + 1). Decrements have an analogous meaning. If x ∈H, then H[x] ←H[x] −1
replaces the (unique) pair (x, f) in H with (x, f −1). If, after such a replacement, the pair is of
the form (x, 0), then we remove it from H (in particular, |H| decreases by one). We will never call
decrements H[x] ←H[x] −1 when x /∈H.
The dictionary H (with the above operations) can be implemented easily with a hash table or, to
avoid randomization, with a self-balancing tree.

text:
Insertion of an element in the sketch
Let s > 1 be an integer parameter to be determined later.
To insert a stream element x in the sketch, we perform the following two operations sequentially:
1. We increment H[x] ←H[x] + 1.
2. If |H| = s, then for every x ∈H we decrement H[x] ←H[x] −1.
Note that, if operation 1 increases |H| by one, then (if |H| = s is true) operation 2 erases the pair
(x, 1) inserted at step 1. From this observation it is immediate to see that the invariant |H| < s is
always true, so H always uses O(s) words of space.
Estimating a frequency
Our estimate for y ∈[1, n] is simply ˜fy = H[y].
First, for any y ∈[1, n] clearly it holds ˜fy ≤fy. To see this, observe that H[y] is incremented only
when a stream element equal to y is observed.
We now prove that ˜fy ≥fy −m/s always holds (deterministically!). Observe that:
Remark 4.10.5. When H[y] is decremented by one unit in operation 2, in total s frequencies H[x]
are decremented.
We can imagine each decrement of H[x] ←H[x] −1 as removing one occurrence of x from the
stream. But then, H[y] cannot diﬀer from fy by more than m/s: this would imply that we remove
more than (m/s)·s = m elements from the stream (impossible, since the stream contains m elements).
We conclude that it must be H[y] = ˜fy ≥fy −m/s for any y ∈[1, n].
By choosing s = ⌈ϵ−1⌉for any desired error rate ϵ > 0, we conclude:
Theorem 4.10.6. Choose any desired error rate ϵ > 0. The Misra-Gries Sketch uses O(ϵ−1) words
of space and, for any y ∈[1, n], returns an estimate ˜fy such that fy −ϵ · m ≤˜fy ≤fy.
By implementing the dictionary as a self-balancing tree, frequency estimation queries are supported
in O(log(ϵ−1)) time. When a new stream element arrives, the Misra-Gries sketch can be updated in
O(ϵ−1) time.
4.11
Estimating F0 (counting distinct elements)
As mentioned earlier, by deﬁning 00 = 0, then F0 = |{x1, . . . , xm}| is the number of distinct elements
in the stream. We ﬁrst discuss naive solutions, then move to a simpliﬁed (assuming fully-uniform
hashing) version of the venerable Flajolet-Martin algorithm [21]. We then discuss a more realistic
version (the Bottom-k algorithm, requiring only pairwise independent hashing), and conclude with a
broad discussion of cardinality-estimation algorithms used in practice.
4.11.1
Naive solutions
We start with the problem of counting the number of distinct integers in the stream, i.e. F0 = d =
|{x1, x2, . . . , xm}|. We cannot aﬀord to use too much memory (and m and d are very large — typically
in the order of billions).
We report some illuminating examples of the practical relevance of the count-distinct problem.
Some of these examples are taken from the paper [18].
Example 4.11.1 (DoS attacks). Denial of Service attacks can be detected by analyzing the number of
distinct ﬂows (source-destination IP pairs contained in the headers of TCP/IP packets) passing through
a network hub in a speciﬁc time interval. The reason is that typical DoS software use large numbers of
fake IP sources; if they were to use few IP sources, then those sources could be easily identiﬁed (and
blocked) because of the large traﬃc they must generate in order for the DoS attack to be eﬀective.
96

text:
Example 4.11.2 (Spreading rate of a worm). Worms are self-replicating malware whose goal is to
spread to as many computers as possible using a network (e.g. the Internet) as medium. In order to
count how many computers have been infected by the worm, one needs to (1) ﬁlter packets containing
the worm’s code, and (2) count the number of distinct source IPs in the headers of those packets. From
https: // www. caida. org/ archive/ code-red/ (an analysis of the spread of the Code-Red version
2 worm between midnight UTC July 19, 2001 and midnight UTC July 20, 2001):
“On July 19, 2001 more than 359,000 computers were infected with the Code-Red (CRv2) worm in
less than 14 hours. At the peak of the infection frenzy, more than 2,000 new hosts were infected each
minute.”
Example 4.11.3 (Distinct IPs/post views). Suppose we wish to count how many people are visiting
our web site. Then, we need to count how many distinct IP numbers are connecting to the server that
hosts the web site. The same problem occurs with post views; in this case, the problem is more serious
since the problem must be solved for each post! For example, Reddit uses a randomized cardinality
estimation algorithm (HLL) to count post views.
A ﬁrst naive solution to the count-distinct problem is to keep a bitvector B[1, n] of n bits, initialized
with all 0’s. Then it is suﬃcient to set B[xi] = 1 for each element xi of the stream. Finally, we count
the number of 1’s in the bitvector. If n is very large (like in typical applications), this solution uses
too much space. A second solution could be to store the stream elements in a self-balancing binary
search tree or in a hash table with dynamic re-allocation. This solution uses O(d log n) bits of space,
which could still be too much if the number d of distinct elements is very large.
Using MinHash
The MinHash sketch described in Section 4.5 can be used to solve the problem in
O(log n) bits of space with additive error ϵn. Let A = {x1, . . . , xm} ⊆[n] be the set of which we want
to estimate the cardinality d = |A|. Observe that A = A ∩[n], so |A| = |A ∩[n]|. We can use MinHash
to estimate the Jaccard distance J(A, [n]) = |A∩[n]|
|A∪[n]| = |A|
n between A and the universe [n]. Then:
|A| = n · J(A, [n])
Recalling that MinHash gives us an additive ϵ-approximation J+(A, [n]) of J(A, [n]) with probability
at least 1 −δ, we obtain that the estimator n · J+(A, [n]) satisﬁes (with probability ≥1 −δ):
n · J+(A, [n]) = n · (J(A, [n]) ± ϵ) = |A| ± ϵn
This estimate is good if |A| is close to n, because then the error ϵn is close to |A|. However, for small
|A| this estimate is very bad: the error ϵn is much larger than |A|. Unfortunately, in typical scenarios
|A| ≪n. Consider, for example, the task of counting how many distinct IPv4 addresses sent a query to
a server: the answer is surely much smaller than the total number n = 232 of possible IPv4 addresses!
In the next sections, the goal will be to achieve a multiplicative error ϵ|A| proportional to the set’s
cardinality.
An observation: recall, from Section 4.5, that our estimator ˆJh(A, [n]) is a Bernoullian RV which
takes the value 1 if and only if ˆh(A) = ˆh([n]), where ˆh(X) = min{h(x) : x ∈X} and h is a uniform
permutation of [n]. But then, ˆh([n]) = 1 so our estimator ˜d = n· ˆJh(A, [n]) can equivalently be deﬁned
as:
˜d =
(
n
if ˆh(A) = 1
0
otherwise
(4.2)
4.11.2
Flajolet-Martin’s algorithm
We now show algorithms for estimating set cardinalities with multiplicative (i.e. relative) error. The
following solution is an idealized version (requiring totally uniform hash functions) of the algorithm

text:
described by Flajolet and Martin in [21]. Let [1, n] denote the range of integers 1, 2, . . . , n and [0, 1]
denote the range of all real values between 0 and 1, included.
We use a uniform hash function
h : [1, n] →[0, 1]. Note that such a function actually requires Θ(n) words of space to be stored, see
Section 2.3: the algorithm is not practical, but we describe it for its simplicity and elegance.
Algorithm 7: FM
input : A stream of integers x1, . . . , xm.
output: An estimate ˆd of the number of distinct integers in the stream.
1 Initialize y = 1;
2 For each stream element x, update y ←min(y, h(x));
3 When the stream ends, return the estimate ˆd = 1
y −1;
Intuitively, why does FM work? First, note that repeated occurrences of some integer x in the
stream will yield the same hash value h(x). Since h is uniform, we end up drawing d uniform real
numbers y1 < y2 < · · · < yd in the interval [0, 1] 7. At the end, the algorithm returns 1/y1 −1. The
more distinct yi’s we see, the more likely it is to see a smaller value. In particular, h will spread the yi’s
uniformly in the interval [0, 1]; think, for a moment, about the most “uniform” (regular) way to spread
those numbers in [0, 1]: this happens when the intervals [0, y1], [yi, yi+1], [yd, 1] have all approximately
the same length 1/(d+1) ≈yi+1 −yi ≈y1 −0 = y1. But then, our claim 1/y1 −1 ≈d follows. It turns
out that this is true also on average (not just in this idealized “regular” case): the average distance
between 0 and the smallest hash y1 seen in the stream is precisely 1/(d + 1). Next, we prove this
intuition.

Theorem 4.11.6. For any desired relative error 0 < ϵ ≤1 and failure probability 0 < δ < 1, by

distinct elements in the stream with relative error at most ϵ, i.e. we can compute a value ˆd such that:

4.11.3
Bottom-k algorithm
Motivated by the fact that a uniform h : [1, n] →[0, 1] takes too much space to be stored (see Section
2.3), in this section we present an algorithm that only requires a pairwise-independent hash function
h : [1, n] →[0, 1]. See Section 2.3.4 for a discussion on how to implement such a function in practice.
The Bottom-k algorithm is presented as Algorithm 8. It is a generalization of Flajolet-Martin’s
algorithm: we keep the smallest k distinct hash values y1 < y2 < · · · < yk seen in the stream so far,
and ﬁnally return the estimate k/yk. In our analysis we will show that, by choosing k ∈O(ϵ−2), we
obtain a (1±ϵ)-approximation with constant probability. Finally, we will boost the success probability
with a classic median trick.
Algorithm 8: Bottom-k
input : A stream of integers x1, . . . , xm and a desired relative error ϵ ≤1/2.
output: A (1 ± ϵ)-approximation ˆd of the number of distinct integers in the stream, with
failure probability 1/3.
1 Choose k = 24/ϵ2;
2 Initialize (y1, y2, . . . , yk) = (1, 1, . . . , 1);
3 For each stream element x, update the k-tuple (y1, y2, . . . , yk) with the new hash y = h(x) so
that the k-tuple stores (in increasing order) the k smallest hashes seen so far;
4 When the stream ends, return the estimate ˆd = k/yk;
Analysis
Crucially, note that the proof of the following lemma will only require pairwise-independence of h.
Lemma 4.11.7. For any ϵ ≤1/2, Algorithm 8 outputs an estimator ˆd such that
P(| ˆd −d| > ϵ · d) ≤1/3

Example 4.11.9. We want to estimate how many distinct IPv4 addresses (32 bits each) are visiting
our website.
Then, n = 232.
Say we choose a function h′ : [1, n] →[0, M] that is collision-free
with probability at least 1 −n−2. Then (see Section 2.3.3), M = n4 and each hash value requires
log2 M = 4 log2 n = 128 bits (16 bytes) to be stored. We want Bottom-k to return an answer that is
within 10% of the correct answer (ϵ = 0.1, 1/ϵ2 = 100) with probability at least 1 −10−5 (δ = 10−5,
ln(1/δ) < 12). Then, replacing the constants that pop up from our analysis we obtain that Bottom-k
uses at most around 32 MiB of RAM.

text:
Note that to prove our main Theorem 4.11.8 we used rather loose upper bounds. Still, Bottom-k’s
memory usage of < 32 MiB is rather limited if compared with the naive solutions. A bitvector of
length n would require 4 GiB of RAM. On the other hand, C++’s std::set uses 32 bytes per distinct
element8, so it is competitive with our analysis of Bottom-k only for d up to ≈10 · 105; this is clearly
not suﬃcient in big-data scenarios such as a search engine: with over 5 billion searches per day9,
Google would need gigabytes of RAM to solve the problem with a std::set (even assuming as many
as 10 searches per distinct user, and even using more space-eﬃcient data structures). Even better,
practical optimized implementations of distinct-count algorithms solve the same problem within few
kilobytes of memory10 (see also [20]).
4.11.4
The LogLog family of count-distinct algorithms
The original algorithm by Flajolet and Martin [21] is a discrete version of the algorithm presented in
Section 4.11.2 and it is based on the following idea: map each element to a N-bits hash h′(xi) ∈[0, 2N),
remember the maximum number L = lz(h′(xi)) of leading zeros seen in any h′(xi), and ﬁnally return
the estimate 2L. For example, the number of leading zeros of 00111010 is lz(00111010) = 2. To see
why this works, note that in a set of hash values of cardinality 2L, we expect to see one hash h′(xi)
preﬁxed by L zeroes (the pattern 0L does not have anything special: it is used just because lz() can be
computed very eﬃciently on modern architectures). It is not hard to see that our idealized algorithm
presented in Subsection 4.11.2 is essentially equivalent to this variant: h′(x)/(2N −1) ≈h′(x)/2N = y
is approximately a uniform number in [0, 1] (approximately because h′ is discrete). Let L = lz(h′(x)).
Note that 2N−L is a 2-approximation of h′(x), so y = h′(x)/2N ≈2−L. Then, the analysis of the
algorithm of Section 4.11.2 tells us that 2L = 1/y ≈1/y −1 is a good estimator for d, the number
of distinct elements. Observe that the algorithm only needs to keep in memory the maximum value
L = lz(h′(xi)), which, by the discussion above, is L ∈O(log d). But then, bitsize(L) = O(log log d):
the algorithm uses space proportional to O(log log d) bits (doubly exponentially less space than the
naive solution using O(d) words!).
Durand and Flajolet [17] later reﬁned this algorithm, giving it the name LogLog (from the space
used by the algorithm). Their algorithm computes a (1 ± 1.3/
√
k) approximation of the result with
high probability, where k is the number of independent instances of the algorithm. In the same paper
they proposed a more accurate variant named SuperLogLog which, by removing 30% of the largest
h(xi)’s, improves the approximation to (1 ± 1.05/
√
k). In 2007, Flajolet, Fusy, Gandouet and Meunier
[20] further improved the approximation to (1 ± 1.04/
√
k). This algorithm is named HyperLogLog
and uses an harmonic mean of the estimates. Also Google has its own version: HyperLogLog++. See
Heule, Nunkesser and Hall [28].
4.12
Estimating F1 (probabilistic counting)
Consider the basic task of counting. In order to count up to m we clearly need log m bits (logarithms
are base 2). What if we allow for a 2-approximation, that is, we allow our answer to be oﬀby at
most a factor of two? then, it is easy to see that log log m bits are suﬃcient: instead of storing our
number x ≤m, we store y = ⌈log x⌉. Then, m ≤2y ≤2m (2-approximation) and, since y takes
integer values between 0 and log m, it uses just log log m bits. In general, we may ﬁx a relative error
0 < ϵ ≤1 and approximate x with the smallest integer power of (1+ϵ) being larger than or equal to x:
(1+ϵ)y ≥x, with y = ⌈log1+ϵ x⌉=
l
log x
log(1+ϵ)
m
. Then, y requires at most log log m−log log(1+ϵ)+1 ≤
log log m + O(log(ϵ−1)) bits to be stored (logarithms are base 2 unless otherwise speciﬁed; we used the
bound log(1 + ϵ) ≥
1
1/ϵ+1/2 and assumed 0 < ϵ < 1) and is a (1 + ϵ)-approximation of x.
8https://lemire.me/blog/2016/09/15/the-memory-usage-of-stl-containers-can-be-surprising/
9https://review42.com/resources/google-statistics-and-facts
10https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HyperLogLog

text:
Example 4.12.1. Suppose we allow for a 10% relative error (i.e. ϵ = 0.1). Then, we need approxi-
mately just log log m+3 bits. What is the largest number we can store in 8 bits? Solving log log m+3 = 8
we obtain log log m = 5, i.e. we can store a number as large as 225 = 232 with 10% relative error.
While the above reasoning shows how one can store approximately a large counter in a small number
of bits, it does not show how to increment such counter: in real-case applications, we may wish to
start from an approximate counter initialized to 0 and increase it one unit at a time (for example,
every time a certain event occurs). In the next section we see that this goal can be achieved by using
randomization, incrementing the counter with some small probability.
4.12.1
Morris’ algorithm
In 1978 Robert Morris11, a computer scientist working at Bell labs, studied the problem of counting
large numbers using very small (8 bits) registers. Using just 8 bits, the largest number that can be
stored (without skipping any positive integer) is clearly 255. However, as seen above, this is true
only if we wish to store exact counts; if we allow for some error, then the register can actually hold
larger numbers. Algorithm 9 shows the basic algorithm devised by Morris, ﬁrst described in [35].
The algorithm uses just one register (X) and its failure probability 1/(2ϵ2) makes it useful only for
ϵ > 1/
√
2, since otherwise the bound on the failure probability 1/(2ϵ2) is larger than one (thus not
meaningful). To reduce the error probability, we will run several parallel versions of the algorithm
(mean + median trick).
Algorithm 9: Morris
input : A stream of m events and a desired relative error 0 < ϵ ≤1
output: A (1 ± ϵ)-approximation ˆm of the number m of events in the stream, with failure
probability 1/(2ϵ2)
1 Initialize a register X = 0;
2 For each event to be counted: increment X with probability 2−X;
3 Finally, output 2X −1;
Analysis
This analysis of the algorithm has been adapted from [26, 39, 23]. We ﬁrst prove that the
estimator 2X −1 returned by the algorithm is unbiased:
Lemma 4.12.2. E[2X −1] = m
Proof. We proceed by induction on m. Let us denote with Xi the register’s content after event i.
For m = 0, we have X0 = 0 and E[2X0 −1] = 0 so we are done. Assume inductively that the claim
holds for m, i.e. E[2Xm −1] = m (equivalently, E[2Xm] = m + 1). Then, applying the law of total
expectation to the partition {Xm = i} of the event space we have:
E[2Xm+1 −1]
=
E[2Xm+1] −1

�P∞
i=0 P(Xm = i) · E[2Xm+1 | Xm = i]


2i + 1

Estimating F2
We now present a very simple and elegant algorithm to estimate F2 = P

in the Euclidean space and sketches for relational algebra.
4.13.1
The tug-of-war sketch
This technique is due to Alon, Matias, and Szegedy [3] and it is also known as the AMS sketch. We
use a 4-wise independent hash function f : [1, n] →{−1, +1}. As we show next, the extremely simple
(and elegant) Algorithm 10 returns an unbiased estimator for F2.
Algorithm 10: Tug of war
input : A stream of integers x1, . . . , xm.
output: An estimate of the second-order frequency moment F2.
1 Initialize z = 0;
2 For each stream element x, update z ←z + h(x);
3 When the stream ends, return the estimate z2;
In other words, after seeing the j-th stream element we maintain a sketch z = Pj
i=1 h(xi). Observe
that the sketch z is easy to update upon additive frequency updates (i.e. fy ←fy + ∆, for any ∆∈N
and y ∈[1, n]). It is also easy to combine the sketches of two streams and obtain the sketch of the
concatenation of the two streams (solve this as an exercise).

4.13.2
Dimensionality reduction
We now show that the idea behind the tug-of-war sketch solves another very important problem
in massive data processing: dimensionality reduction under the Euclidean distance. The main idea
is to simply view the frequencies fy of the previous subsections as a size-n vector. Note that the
calculations of the previous subsections did not rely on the fact that the frequencies fy are integers.
We will now switch to real numbers and show that this slight change of perspective allows us to perform
dimensionality reduction. The reason why this technique is called dimensionality reduction and not
just “distance approximation” is that it is much powerful than simple distance approximation: our
sketch will be a linear map mapping Rn to Rc for some small constant c (depending on the desired
relative error) preserving the Euclidean distance up to some relative error ϵ.
Let a = (a1, . . . , an) ∈Rn and b = (b1, . . . , bn) ∈Rn be two real-valued vectors of dimension n. As
in the previous subsections, let h : [1, n] →{−1, +1} be a 4-wise independent hash function. Our ﬁnal

text:
Theorem 4.13.5 (Dimensionality reduction). Let a, b ∈Rn. Let s = ⌈9ϵ−2⌉, for any desired error
rate ϵ > 0. Let H ∈Rs×n be a matrix ﬁlled with uniform values from the set {−1/√s, 1/√s}. Then,
with probability at least 2/3 the following holds:
||H · a −H · b||2 ∈(1 ± ϵ)||a −b||2.
In other words: H : Rn 7→RΘ(ϵ−2) is a linear map that preserves the Euclidean distance up to relative
error ϵ with probability at least 2/3.
To boost the probability of success, we can apply the median trick (Lemma 2.2.9): we compute
t ∈Θ(log(1/δ)) independent maps H1, . . . , Ht, obtaining t estimates for the distance between vectors
a and b. Finally, we return the median of the t estimated distances. By Lemma 2.2.9, the result is a
multiplicative (1 ± ϵ)-approximation of ||a −b||2 with probability at least 1 −δ.
Note that the median is not a linear operator, so the median trick does not give us a linear
transformation. The Johnson-Lindenstrauss transform [30] removes the need for computing the me-
dian by ﬁlling H with values following a normal distribution (rather than with uniform values from
{−1/√s, 1/√s}).
This technique provides a linear transformation H : Rn 7→RΘ(ϵ−2 log(1/δ)) that
preserves the Euclidean distance up to relative error ϵ with probability at least 1 −δ.
4.13.3
Sketches for relational algebra (SQL)
Relational algebra is a mathematical framework for manipulating data stored in the forms of relations
(also called tables). SQL is the most inﬂuential implementation of relational algebra, being employed
in most commercial implementations of relational databases. While until now we studied sketches for
conceptually-simple operations such as counting distinct elements, in the following we show that the
tug-of-war sketch can be used to estimate the answer to simple SQL queries whose ﬁnal result is an
aggregation [2, 16]. This result is very powerful: imagine collapsing an entire SQL database (gigabytes)
into a tiny sketch (kilobytes) allowing to compute approximate answers to SQL queries!
Aggregations on one relation
Let R be a relation containing tuples from [n]r. Let us call ρ1, . . . , ρr
the r attributes of R. We start from a very simple case of aggregation: the sum under a SELECT
operation on one relation. For example, let R be the relation shown in Table 4.1, with r = 4 attributes.

Aggregations on two relations (equi-joins)
Let R, P be two relations containing tuples from
[n]r and [n]p, respectively. Let us call ρ1, . . . , ρr the r attributes of R and π1, . . . , πp the p attributes
of P. For example, let R be the relation of Table 4.1 and let P be the relation shown in Table 4.2,
with p = 2 attributes.
Then the following SQL query
SELECT sum(evaluation)
FROM R, P
WHERE matriculation = 12345 AND active = 1 AND R.course_code = P.course_code
will return 25 + 27 = 52: the sum of evaluations of student 12345, only for active courses. More in
general, we want to design a sketch able to estimate the answer to a query of the form:
-- query Q2
SELECT sum(ρi)
FROM R,P
WHERE ρj = x AND πk = y AND ρq = πt
In other words: an aggregation (sum) over a join query with one equi-join condition and two other
equality conditions (generalizing to any number of join and equality conditions is then immediate).
For simplicity, we now assume that h : [1, n] →{−1, 1} is fully-independent (exercise: after reading
this section, try to put an upper-bound to the amount of independence needed for h). Assume moreover
that the domains of the attributes of P and R are disjoint, unless two attributes have the same name
(for example, course code is an attribute of both R and P in our example, so the intersection of
those two columns will possibly be nonempty). This is true in our example: it will never happen, for
example, that a matriculation number is equal to a course code.
We build one tug-of-war sketch for table R as follows:
Q2(R) =
X

h(xj)h(xq) · xi

condition ρq = πt, and xi is included for the ﬁnal sum() aggregate.

While we proved that our estimators for SQL queries are unbiased, we did not try to compute
their variance in order to derive guarantees on the approximation ratio. Dobra et al. [16] prove that
the variance grows exponentially with 2N, where N is the number of equality conditions in the WHERE
clause (in query Q2, N = 3). Still, these estimators can be used in practice and produce accurate
results with a suﬃciently large number of repetitions.
113

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes
text:
Chapter 5
Exercises
Exercise 1
You are given a string S ∈Σn containing r equal-letter runs. For example: if S = AAABBBBCCAAA,
then r = 4 (the four runs are AAA, BBBB, CC, and AAA). Design a data structure using O(r)
words of space able to answer rank and select queries on S in O(log n) time. Can you improve the
space and query time of the data structure?
Exercise 2
Design a compressed data structure implementing the following predictive text model.
The data
structure should store a string S ∈Σn in high-order entropy-compressed space and should be able to
answer quickly the following query: given a substring T of S, what is the character that follows T in S
more frequently? For example, if Ta appears 50 times in S, Tb appears 10 times in S, and Tc appears
123 times in S, then the answer is c.
Exercise 3
Prove that you cannot encode (lossless) in m bits a set containing m integers.
Exercise 4
We want to implement a SPAM ﬁlter for tweets formed by at most 100 words (in English). The ﬁlter
should maintain a list L of 106 blacklisted phrases, where each phrase is formed by a minimum of one
to a maximum of ﬁve words. For example, one of the phrases in L could be:
“you have won 1000 EUR”
When concatenated together, the phrases of L form an ASCII text of length 20 MiB with second-order
empirical entropy H2 = 0.2.
Given a tweet X, we want to put it in the SPAM folder if and only if it contains at least one
sentence belonging to L.
We tolerate a 1% probability that a tweet is incorrectly ﬂagged as SPAM. Show how to solve the
problem by using the least possible amount of space. What if we want this probability to be 0%? How
much space do your solutions use?
114
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes.pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes
text:
Exercise 5
You are given two Bloom ﬁlters for sets S1 and S2 (the sets are unknown: you know only their ﬁlters).
The two Bloom ﬁlters have been built with the same parameters (M, k) and the same hash functions.
Show how to compute the Bloom ﬁlter for S1 ∪S2. Solve the same exercise with Counting Bloom
ﬁlters.
Exercise 6
The “Algorithms and learning over massive data” (ALMD) 12-ECTS course is formed by two modules:
AMD and LMD. Let [1, n] be the students enrolled in ALMD. Let moreover A ⊆[1, n] and L ⊆[1, n]
be the sets of students who have passed the AMS and LMS modules, respectively.
Design a data sketch for A and L supporting the following queries:
1. Insert a new student in the set (A or L). We assume that the student does not already belong
to the set.
2. Check if A = L. The answer should be correct with high probability (i.e. with probability 1−n−c
for any desired constant c).
3. Estimate the fraction of students who have passed exactly one module between AMD and LMD
(i.e. do not count students that have passed zero or both modules). For example, if there are in
total n = 30 students, 7 students have passed only AMD, and 8 students have passed only LMD,
then this fraction is (7 + 8)/30 = 0.5.
4. Focus on the set of students who passed at least one module. Among these students, estimate
the fraction of students that passed both modules. For example, if 20 students passed at least
one between AMD and LMD, and 5 students passed both AMD and LMD, this fraction is
5/20 = 0.25.
Assuming constant absolute error 0 < ϵ < 1 and constant failure probability 0 < δ < 1 for queries
(3-4), the sketch should use O(log n) bits of space.
Exercise 7
An embedded device with few KiB of RAM contains a sensor that measures the environment’s temper-
ature (an integer number) every 10 seconds. We want to store all the generated data, in a streaming
fashion, in a sketch using just few Kilobytes of space (note that the sensor produces several MiB of
data per year). At any point in time, the data sketch should be able to answer quickly to the following
query, with relative error ϵ: what was the average temperature in a speciﬁc time range? (e.g. April
15th 2021, 10:15:40 AM - September 21th 2023, 09:10:20 PM).
Exercise 8
We want to store a relational table t(a1, a2, . . . , ak) in a small (polylogarithmic-space in the table’s
size) sketch supporting the following two queries: (i) insert a new row into the table, and (ii) given q
attributes ai1, . . . , aiq at query time, estimate with relative error ϵ and failure probablity δ the number
of distinct rows in the projection πai1,...,aiq (t).
Exercise 9
We want to store a relational table t(a1, a2, . . . , ak), where columns a1, a2, . . . , ak store integer numbers,
in a small (polylogarithmic-space in the table’s size) sketch supporting the following two queries (we
allow a small error in the answer): (i) insert a new row into the table, and (ii) given an attribute ai
and two integers ℓ≤r, estimate the number of rows (not necessarily distinct) such that ℓ≤ai ≤r.
115
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes.pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes
text:
Exercise 10
An embedded device with few KiB of RAM contains a GPS sensor that outputs the absolute position
of a train (an integer number) on a railway every second. We want to store all the generated data, in
a streaming fashion, in a small-space sketch. At any point in time, the data sketch should be able to
answer quickly to the following query, with relative error ϵ: what was the average speed of the train
in a speciﬁc time range? (e.g. on April 15th 2021, from 10:15:45 AM to 12:10:22 PM).
Exercise 11
Design an algorithm to be implemented on a router with few MiB of memory, to solve the following
problem. We have the original code of a huge virus, occupying 1 GiB of space. To elude antivirus
software, after infecting a new computer the virus modiﬁes itself by swapping some characters from a
small subset of ﬁxed positions in the code (let’s say, 5 positions) that are unknown to us. Then, the
infected computer sends the modiﬁed virus’ code on the network. Before and after sending the virus’
code, the infected computer could send other unrelated data. Assume that our router receives all
packets passing through the network and, for simplicity, assume that IP packets are sorted by sender.
The algorithm should be able to estimate how many computers have been infected.
Exercise 12
Design an algorithm for a web crawler that explores the web and has the task to identify clusters of
web pages being similar to each other, where similarity is measured according to the words contained
in the web page.
Exercise 13
You are hired at CIA with the task of preventing terrorist attacks. Design an algorithm to be im-
plemented on a router with few KiB of memory, to estimate the number of IP addresses that issued
Google searches for both these questions (possibly, the searches do not belong to the same IP packet):
(i) where to buy ammonium-nitrate fertilizers, and (ii) how to activate a mechanical relay from distance
with a mobile phone.
Exercise 14
Design an algorithm to be implemented on a router with 3 GiB of free RAM, to solve the following
problem: at any point in time, given an IPv4 address estimate how many IP packets have been sent
from that address. You can assume that each IP address sends no more than 263 IP packets. The
answer must not exceed relative error 82% with probability at least 1/4.
Exercise 15
Design an algorithm to be implemented on a router with 100 MiB of free RAM, to solve the following
problem: at any point in time, given an IPv4 address estimate how many IP packets have been sent
from that address. You can assume that the number of IP packets sent in total by all addresses does
not exceed 232. The answer must not exceed absolute error 1000.
Exercise 16
Let c1, . . . , cn be a list of computers that send IP packets to two servers A and B; we see the packets
arriving in a streaming fashion as a list of pairs (ci, X), where i ∈[n] and X ∈{A, B}. Assuming that
every computer ci sends at least one packet to either A or B, estimate with small absolute error how
many computers sent a packet to both A and B (i.e. computers ci such that both pairs (ci, A) and
(ci, B) appear on the stream).
116
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Algorithms for massive data\Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes.pdf

doc_id: Algorithms for massive data/Algorithms_for_massive_data___notes
text:
Exercise 17
Let c1, . . . , cn be a list of computers that send IP packets to two servers A and B; we see the packets
arriving in a streaming fashion as a list of pairs (ci, X), where i ∈[n] and X ∈{A, B}. Estimate
with small absolute error how many computers sent a packet to both A and B (i.e. computers ci such
that both pairs (ci, A) and (ci, B) appear on the stream). Diﬀerently from the previous exercise, there
could exist computers that do not send packets to A nor B.
Exercise 18
A router ﬁlters all IP packets — of the form (S, M) — from our company’s intranet, where S is
the sender’s IP address, and M is the message. We know that two worms w1 and w2 (self-replicating
viruses) circulate on the network. The code of each of the two worms ﬁts in n bytes, and we can assume
that if an IP packet (S, M) contains a worm’s code, the whole worm’s code is contained inside the
message M. Design a streaming algorithm that uses O(log n) words of space and solves the following
problem. Let W S
i , for i ∈{1, 2}, be the event “S has been infected by wi”, where S is a uniform IP
address in our intranet. Estimate the correlation
P(W S
1 |W S
2 )
i.e. the probability of being infected by w1 given that we’ve been infected by w2 where, again, S is a
uniform IP address in our intranet.
117

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Ranked retrieval
▪So far, our queries have all been Boolean.
▪Documents either match or don’t.
▪Good for expert users with precise understanding of 
their needs and the collection.
▪Also good for applications: Applications can easily consume 
1000s of results.
▪Not good for most users.
▪Incapable of writing Boolean queries (or they are, but they 
think it’s too much work).
▪Don’t want to wade through 1000s of results.
▪This is particularly true of web search.
Ch. 6
Completely unsorted.
[NOTE] Completely unsorted.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Problem with Boolean search:
feast or famine
▪Boolean “conjuntive queries” often result in either 
too few (=0) or too many (1000s) results.
▪Query 1: “standard user dlink 650” → 200,000 hits
▪Query 2: “standard user dlink 650 no card found”: 0 hits
▪It takes a lot of skill to come up with a query that 
produces a manageable number of hits.
▪AND gives too few; OR gives too many
Ch. 6
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Ranked retrieval models
▪Rather than a set of documents satisfying a query 
expression, in ranked retrieval, the system reorders 
the (top) documents in the collection for a given 
query
▪Free text queries: Rather than a query language of 
operators and expressions, the user’s query is just 
one or more words in a natural language
▪the user specifies her/is information need through an ad-
hoc query which initiates a search
▪Ranked retrieval has normally been associated with 
free text queries and vice versa
7
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Feast or famine: not a problem in 
ranked retrieval
▪When a system produces a ranked result set, 
large result sets are not an issue
▪Indeed, the size of the result set is not an issue
▪We just show the top k ( ≈ 10) results
▪We don’t overwhelm the user
▪Premise: the ranking algorithm works ☺
Ch. 6
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Scoring as the basis of ranked retrieval
▪We wish to return “in order” those documents 
▪First the ones most likely to be useful to users
▪How can we rank-order the documents in the 
collection with respect to a query?
▪Assign a score – say in [0, 1] – to each document 
given a query
▪This score measures how well document and query 
“match”
▪We need a way of assigning a score to a 
query/document pair
Ch. 6
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Jaccard coefficient for scoring?
▪Recall: A commonly used measure of overlap of two 
sets A and B
▪jaccard(A,B) = |A ∩B| / |A ∪B|
▪jaccard(A,A) = 1
▪jaccard(A,B) = 0 if A ∩ B = 0
▪A and B don’t have to be of the same size.
▪Always assigns a number between 0 and 1
▪Higher is better
Ch. 6
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Jaccard coefficient for scoring
▪What is the query-document Jaccard score for each 
of the two documents below?
▪Query: ides of march
▪Document 1: “Caesar died in March”
▪Document 2:  “The long march”
▪J(q, d1) = 1 / (3+4-1) = 1/6
▪J(q, d2) = 1 / (3+3-1) = 1/5
▪Using Jaccard d2 appears to be similar to q than d1 
▪However, the issue here is not Jaccard
Ch. 6
Assume lowercase 
term normalization
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Issues with Jaccard for scoring
▪It doesn't consider term frequency (how many times 
a term occurs in a document)
▪Jaccard doesn’t consider this information, as texts are 
represented as “sets” of terms, as in the Boolean model of 
IR 
▪Term frequency
▪The more frequent the query term in the document, the 
higher the score (should be)
▪Rare terms in a collection are more informative than 
frequent terms
Ch. 6
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Bag of words model (multiset)
▪Count vector (bag of words) representation doesn't 
consider the ordering of words in a document
▪John is quicker than Mary and Mary is quicker than John
have the same vectors
▪In a sense, this is a step back: The positional index was 
able to distinguish these two documents.
▪We will look at “recovering” positional information later in 
this course.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Notation
16
▪t is a generic vocabulary term
▪cft is collection frequency: the number of occurrences 
of term t in the document collection 
▪dft is the document frequency: the total number of 
documents that contain term t in the corpus
▪tft,d is the term frequency: The total number of 
occurrences of term t in a document d
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Term frequency tf
▪The term frequency tft,d of term t in document d is 
defined as the count/number of times that t occurs in d
▪We want to use tft,d when computing query-document 
match scores. But how?
▪Raw term frequency is not what we want:
▪A document with 10 occurrences of the term is more relevant 
than a document with 1 occurrence of the term.
▪But not 10 times more relevant
▪Relevance does not increase linearly with term 
frequency
frequency = count in IR
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

text:
Log-frequency weighting
▪Log-frequency weight of term t
in d to smooth its effects:
▪0 → 0,  1 → 1,  2 → 1.3,  10 → 2,  1000 → 4,  etc.
▪Score for a document-query pair: sum over terms t in 
both q and d:
Scoreqd
▪The score is 0 for document d if none of the query 
terms q is present in d.

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Document frequency
▪Rare terms in the collection are more informative than 
frequent terms
▪Small dft w.r.t. the size N of the document corpus
▪dft ≪N
▪Consider a term in the query that is (globally) rare in 
the collection (e.g., arachnocentric)
▪A document containing this term is very likely to be relevant 
to the query arachnocentric
→ We want higher weights for rare terms like 
arachnocentric
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Document frequency, continued
▪Frequent terms in the collection are less informative 
than rare terms
▪Consider a query term that is frequent enough in the 
collection (e.g., high, increase, line)
▪A document containing such a term is more likely to be 
relevant than a document that doesn’t contain it
▪But it’s not a sure indicator of relevance wrt the user 
information need, as a large part of the documents
contains this query term
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Document frequency, continued
▪We will use document frequency (dft) to capture this
▪we want high positive weights for words like high, 
increase, and line (characterized by a large dft, but still 
much smaller than the collection size)
▪but lower than weights assigned to globally rare terms, 
characterized by a very small dft
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

▪Does idf have an effect on ranking for one-term 
queries, like “iPhone”?
▪idf doesn’t rank documents for "one term" queries 
▪all documents in which the term occurs have the same score:

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
How do we weight doc terms in the vectors?
▪Here’s the intuition:
▪Terms that appear often in a document (tf) should get high 
weights
▪Terms that appear in many documents (df) should get low 
weights
▪
How do we capture this mathematically?
▪Term frequency
▪Inverse document frequency
The more often a document contains the term “dog”, 
the more likely that the document is “about” dogs.
Words like “the”, “a”, “of” appear in (nearly) all 
documents.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
tf-idf weighting
▪The tf-idf weight of a term is the 
product of its tf weight (normalized wf) and its idf weight.
▪Best known weighting scheme in Information Retrieval
▪Note: the “-” in tf-idf is a hyphen, not a minus sign!
▪Alternative names:  tf.idf,   tf x idf
▪Increases with the number of occurrences within a document
▪Increases with the rarity of the term in the collection
Sec. 6.2.2
Alternative smaller:
log(1 + tft,d)
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Documents as vectors
▪So, we have a |V|-dimensional vector space
▪Vocabulary’s terms are axes of the space
▪Documents are points or vectors in this space
▪Very high-dimensional: tens of millions of 
dimensions when you apply this to a web search 
engine
▪Very sparse vectors - most entries are zero.
Sec. 6.3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Queries as vectors
▪Key idea 1: Do the same for queries: represent them 
as vectors in the space
▪Key idea 2: Rank documents according to their 
proximity to the query in this vectorial space
▪proximity = similarity of vectors
▪proximity ≈ inverse of distance
▪Recall: We do this because we want to get away from 
the either-in-or-out of the Boolean model.
▪Instead: rank more relevant documents higher than 
less relevant documents
Sec. 6.3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Formalizing vector space proximity
▪First cut: distance between two points
▪( = distance between the end points of the two vectors)
▪Euclidean distance?
▪Euclidean distance is a bad idea . . .
▪. . . because Euclidean distance is large for vectors of 
different lengths.
▪Euclidean distance works however for normalized 
vectors
Sec. 6.3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Why Euclidean distance is a bad idea
The Euclidean
distance between q
and d2 is large
although the
distribution of terms
in query q and 
document d2 are
very similar
Sec. 6.3
Euclidean distance
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Use angle instead of distance
▪Experiment: take a document d and append it to 
itself. Call this document d′
▪d′ and d have the same semantic content
▪Vectors d′ and d overlap, but the magnitudes d′ is double
▪The Euclidean distance between the two documents 
d′ and d can be quite large
▪Although the angle between the two vectorial 
representations of the two documents is 0o (in the vector 
space)
▪Key idea: Rank document vectors according to the 
“angle” with the query vector.
Sec. 6.3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
From angles to cosines
▪The following two notions are equivalent.
▪Rank documents in decreasing order of the angle between 
query and document     angle=distance
▪Rank documents in increasing order  of 
cosine(query,document) 
cosine=similarity
▪Cosine is a monotonically decreasing function in the 
interval [0o, 180o]
▪from 1 (max similarity) to -1 (max dissimilarity)
Sec. 6.3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Length normalization
▪A vector can be (length-) normalized by dividing each 
of its components by its length – for this we use the L2
norm:
▪Dividing a vector by its L2 norm makes it a unit (length) 
vector (on the surface of the unit hypersphere)
▪Effect on the two documents d and d′ (d appended to 
itself) from the earlier slide: they have identical vectors 
after length-normalization
▪Long and short documents have now comparable weights
Sec. 6.3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

cos(q,d) is the cosine similarity of q and d … or,
equivalently, the cosine of the angle between q and d.

tf-idf weighting has many variants
Sec. 6.4
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Weighting may differ in queries vs 
documents
▪Search engines adopt different weighting schemes for 
queries and documents
▪SMART Notation
▪the notation ddd.qqq (using the acronyms from the previous 
table) denotes the combination in use in a search engine
▪A very standard weighting scheme is: lnc.ltc
▪Document: logarithmic tf (l as 1st character), no idf (n as 2nd
character), cosine normalization (c as 3rd character)
▪Query: logarithmic tf (l as 1st character),  idf (t as 2nd character), 
cosine normalization (c as 3rd character)
▪Indeed, raw tft in queries is usually 1 if a term occurs 
Sec. 6.4
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf
