text:
Best Match 25
▪… also known as BM25, full name “Okapi BM25”
▪developed under the Okapi information retrieval system, 
implemented at London's City University in the 1980s and 
1990s.
▪used everywhere nowadays (strong baseline)
52
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
BM25
▪Okapi BM25 (BM: best matching) is a probabilistic 
ranking function, a state-of-the-art method better
than Cosine
▪Using term independence assumption to approximate 
the document probability of being relevant
▪
▪IDFt=log(N/dft) is the usual inverse document 
frequency
𝑡∈𝑞∩𝑑
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
BM25
▪ft,d is the term frequency of term t in document d
▪a.k.a. tft,d
▪ld is the length of document d
▪longer documents are less important
▪L is the average document length in the collection
▪b determines the importance of ld
If ld = L, then 
Ft = ft,d
𝑡∈𝑞∩𝑑
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Pivoted transformation for Doc length,
where L = avdl (average doc length)
BM25
This is the denominator of Ft
For b>0, we penalize long 
documents, because they 
naturally have a better chance 
to match query terms
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
BM25
▪𝜏is a saturation function, modeling non-linearity of 
term frequencies contribution
▪For high values of Ft, increments in term frequency don’t 
continue to contribute significantly to the score
▪b and k are hyper-parameters (should be tuned on the 
collection)
▪Typical values:  b =0.75 and k ∈[1.2, 2.0]
𝑡∈𝑞∩𝑑
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/index 0408
text:
Summary – vector space ranking
▪Represent the query as a weighted tf-idf vector
▪Represent each document as a weighted tf-idf vector
▪Compute the cosine similarity score for the query 
vector and each document vector
▪Rank documents with respect to the query by score
▪Return the top K (e.g., K = 10) to the user
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\index 0408.pdf

David Marr - 3 Levels of Analysis
D. Marr - Vision : a computational investigation into the human representation and processing of visual information
Computational level: 
what does the system do (e.g.: what problems does it solve or overcome) 
and similarly, why does it do these things.  Simply gives us the description 
of input and output behaviour of a (visual) system.
Algorithmic/representational level: 
how does the system do, what it does, speciﬁcally, what representations 
does it use and what processes does it employ to build and manipulate 
the representations. How the input is transformed to the output.
Implementational/physical level: 
how is the system physically realized (in the case of biological vision, what 
neural structures and neuronal activities implement the visual system)
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 00a - Introduction.pdf

LeNET v5
Perceptron 1958
One of the earliest algorithms that could learn from data 
Implemented in hardware! 
Weights stored in potentiometers, updated with electric 
motors during learning
Connected to a camera that used 20x20 cadmium 
sulﬁde photocells to make a 400-pixel image 
Could learn to recognize letters/faces of the alphabet 
Today we would recognize it as a linear classiﬁer

text:
Scene Understanding with Scene Graphs
https://cs.stanford.edu/people/ranjaykrishna/sgrl/index.html
Scene understanding is more complex than detecting individual objects, as it involves interpreting the 
relationships between multiple objects within a given context. By analyzing spatial arrangements, lighting 
conditions, and other elements that contribute to the overall scene composition, it helps construct a cohesive 
representation (maybe with a graph structure) of the environment.

text:
The exam!
An oral exam (60%) on the whole course
contents
An individual (small project) or a group 
project (40%): see next slides for details
The exam:
1.
Oral exam must be given within an exam session (no outside the oﬃcial slots)
2.
The project can be discussed when ready … I suggest you to do the oral and 
project’s discussion together.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 00b - Introduction.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 00b - Introduction
text:
Practical Activity: Project
Two types of projects:
Individual Projects Examples:
●
Implementing, training and evaluating an 
existing model
●
Deepening a course topic/model
●
Developing a solution to an existing CV 
problem
●
…
Collaborative/Research Projects (up to 2 people) 
Examples:
●
Developing a NEW solution to an existing 
CV problem
●
Deﬁning a NEW problem & developing a 
(proof-of-concept) solution
●
Create a NEW dataset and prepare 
baselines
●
MODIFY an existing model to cope with 
its limitations
●
Studying model’s limitations
●
… 
In both cases we should agree on the topic
Project Topic:
●
I/we have an idea :) Try to formalize it and  drop 
me an email so we can agree. 
●
I/we don’t have an idea :( … no problem, I can 
give one to you. Please drop me an email.
Project Review
Once you have formalized the project idea/topic, you can 
ask for a project review to jumpstart its development.
In case of problems with the project:
1.
try to solve them on your own (google, 
stackoverﬂow, quora…)
2.
reach out to us (me or the tutor). We will not 
write code for you.
Project Evaluation Criteria:
●
Soundness of the idea
●
Methodology
●
Experimental Evaluations
●
for collaborative/research paper: novelty
Project Submission:
●
Due date: when ready;
●
Write a ﬁnal report (max 8 
pages excluded references 
using CVPR template)
●
A 15/20 minutes presentation 
(10-15 slides)
●
Code in github (with proper 
environment + readme ﬁles)
●
You will need to submit the 
report, presentation, code and 
data at least 1 week before 
the discussion (upload on 
Moodle)
Data augmentation + SVD + CNN
[NOTE] Data augmentation + SVD + CNN
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 00b - Introduction.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 00b - Introduction
text:
Practical Activity: Project >> Individual Project
What’s inside of a good INDIVIDUAL project?
●
Introduction / problem statement
●
Motivation (why should we care? why is this problem interesting?)
●
Literature review (what has previously been done? discuss some similar 
approaches)
●
Model description
●
The experimental settings
○
Dataset(s) 
○
Competitors
○
Evaluation criteria
●
The results
○
performance achieved on the dataset(s)
○
additional analysis: sensitivity of (hyper)parameters
●
Discussion
○
Your ﬁndings (a part from the performance) are really important! 
○
points of failure as well!
○
Limitations
●
Tools and resources
●
Source code MUST run and be properly documented!
●
A report of 8 Pages maximum (excluding references) in CVPR format
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 00b - Introduction.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 00b - Introduction
text:
Practical Activity: Project >> Collaborative/Research Project
What’s inside of a good COLLABORATIVE/RESEARCH project?
●
Introduction / problem statement
●
Motivation (why should we care? why is this problem interesting?)
●
Literature review (what has previously been done? how your model differentiate?)
●
State your contributions with respect to the state of the art
●
Model description and rationale for the design choices
●
The experimental settings
○
Dataset(s) 
○
Competitors
○
Evaluation criteria
●
The results
○
performance achieved on the dataset(s)
○
additional analysis: sensitivity of (hyper)parameters and/or ablations
●
Discussion
○
Your ﬁndings (a part from the performance) are really important! 
○
points of failure as well!
○
Limitations
●
Tools and resources
●
Source code MUST run and be properly documented!
●
A report of 8 Pages maximum (excluding references) in CVPR format

Training Neural Networks: setting the LR
Idea 1:
Try lots of different learning rates and see what works “just right”
Idea 2: … do something smarter!
Design an adaptive learning rate that “adapts” to the landscape. Learning 
rates are no longer ﬁxed:
Can be made larger or smaller depending on:
●
how large gradient is
●
how fast learning is happening
●
size of particular weights
●
etc...
check this link for more details!

Training Neural Networks: Regularization
What is?
Is a set of strategies used to reduce overﬁtting 
(generalization error) while keeping the training error 
as low as possible.
Technique that constrains our optimization problem 
to discourage complex models.
Why do we need it? 
Improve generalization of our model on unseen data
Slide from prof. A. Amini

text:
Multi-Layer Recurrent Neural Networks
Credit Chris Manning
●
RNNs are already “deep” on one dimension 
(they unroll over many timesteps)
●
We can also make them “deep” in another 
dimension by applying multiple RNNs – 
this is a multi-layer RNN.
●
This allows the network to compute more 
complex representations
●
The lower RNNs should compute 
lower-level features and the higher RNNs 
should compute higher-level features.
●
Multi-layer RNNs are also called stacked 
RNNs.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences
text:
Multi-Layer Recurrent Neural Networks
●
Multi-layer RNNs in practice
○
Multi-layer or stacked RNNs allow a network to compute more complex representations
○
they work better than just have one layer of high-dimensional encodings!
●
The lower RNNs should compute lower-level features and the higher RNNs should 
compute higher-level features.
○
High-performing RNNs are usually multi-layer (but aren’t as deep as convolutional or feed-forward 
networks)
○
For example: In a 2017 paper, Britz et al. ﬁnd that for Neural Machine Translation, 2 to 4 layers is best 
for the encoder RNN, and 4 layers is best for the decoder RNN 
○
Often 2 layers is a lot better than 1, and 3 might be a little better than 2
●
Usually, skip-connections/dense-connections are needed to train deeper RNNs 
(e.g., 8 layers)
○
Transformer-based networks (e.g., BERT) are usually deeper, like 12 or 24 layers.
○
You will learn about Transformers later; they have a lot of skipping-like connections
Credit Chris Manning
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences.pdf

text:
RNN Training Tricks
(Pascanu et al., 2013; Bengio et al., 2013; Gal and Ghahramani, 2016;Morishita et al., 2017)
●
Mini-batch creation strategies 
(eﬃcient computations)
●
Clipping gradients (avoid exploding 
gradients)
●
Leaky integration (propagate 
long-term dependencies)
●
Momentum (cheap 2nd order)
●
Dropout (avoid overﬁtting)
●
Initialization (start in right ballpark 
avoids exploding/vanishing)
●
Sparse Gradients (symmetry 
breaking)
●
Gradient propagation regularizer 
(avoid vanishing gradient)
●
Gated self-loops (LSTM & GRU, 
reduces vanishing gradient)

text:
LSTM
LSTM networks were created with the following objectives in mind:
1.
Addressing the challenge of long-term dependencies encountered by recurrent 
neural networks (RNNs) caused by the vanishing gradient issue.
2.
Distinguishing themselves from traditional feedforward neural networks through 
the incorporation of feedback connections.
3.
Preserving valuable information from prior data within a sequence to facilitate the 
analysis of new data points.
4.
Demonstrating remarkable proﬁciency in handling data sequences like text, 
speech, and general time-series data.

Seq2Seq
Used to convert one sequence into 
another
●
Both sequences might have 
different lengths
●
Based on an Encoder-decoder 
architecture
●
Two RNNs (typically LSTMs or 
GRUs)
●
Can be deterministic or variational
Applications:
●
Machine translation
●
Question answering
●
Dialogue models 
(conversational agents)
●
Summarization
●
Code generation
…
output of the first -> input of the second.
[NOTE] output of the first -> input of the second.

text:
Seq2Seq: a bit of math
Points worth noting:
1.
The input/source embedding layer,     , 
and the output/target embedding layer,     
    , are two different embedding layers 
with their own parameters.
2.
Linear layer to convert a decoder’s hidden 
state      into one of the possible words      . 
3.
The word      is then (often) used as input 
for the next word generation.

text:
Additive Attention
Bahdanau et al. Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate - ICLR 2015
Bottleneck Problem:
“A 
potential 
issue 
with 
this 
encoder–decoder approach is that a 
neural network needs to be able to 
compress all the necessary information 
of a source sentence into a ﬁxed-length 
vector. This may make it diﬃcult for the 
neural network to cope with long 
sentences, especially those that are 
longer than the sentences in the training 
corpus.”
we are compressing the whole sequence into a small vector.
Add a method that allows the decoder to access the encoder.
[NOTE] we are compressing the whole sequence into a small vector.
Add a method that allows the decoder to access the encoder.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences.pdf

text:
Additive Attention
Image credit Shashank Yadav - Bahdanau et al. Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate - ICLR 2015
Attention mechanism helps to look at all hidden states from encoder sequence for 
making predictions, unlike vanilla Encoder-Decoder approach.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences.pdf

text:
Additive Attention
Image credit Shashank Yadav - Bahdanau et al. Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate - ICLR 2015
Question: 
But how does we decide which states are 
more or less useful for every prediction at 
every time step of the decoder?
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences
text:
Additive Attention
Image credit Shashank Yadav - Bahdanau et al. Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate - ICLR 2015
Question: 
But how does we decide which states are 
more or less useful for every prediction at 
every time step of the decoder?
Solution: 
Use a Neural Network to “learn” which hidden 
encoder states to “attend” to and by how 
much.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences.pdf

text:
Additive Attention: Alignment Computation
Image credit Shashank Yadav - Bahdanau et al. Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate - ICLR 2015
●
a is called “alignment model” 
and is a Feed-forward NN 
(linear 
layer)
●
The score is based on the 
decoder hidden state si−1 and 
the j-th hidden state of the input 
sentence
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences.pdf

text:
Additive Attention: Attention Computation
Image credit Shashank Yadav - Bahdanau et al. Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate - ICLR 2015
●
The score is converted to a 
probability distribution with a 
softmax
●
The probability αij reﬂects the 
importance of the annotation hj 
with respect to the previous 
hidden state si−1 in deciding the 
next state si and generating yi 
(the actual word)
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences.pdf

The context vector ci depends on a 
sequence of annotations (h1, · · · , 
hT) to which an encoder maps the 
input sentence.
Each 
annotation 
hi 
contains 
information about the whole input 
sequence with a strong focus on 
the parts surrounding the i-th word 
of the input sequence

Another RNN layer
5.
… whatever can be used to compute
 a Loss (fundamental for backprop)
F(ht)
Ct

1.
Classiﬁcation
2.
Regression
3.
…

text:
Multi-Layer Recurrent Neural Networks
Credit Chris Manning
●
RNNs are already “deep” on one dimension 
(they unroll over many timesteps)
●
We can also make them “deep” in another 
dimension by applying multiple RNNs – 
this is a multi-layer RNN.
●
This allows the network to compute more 
complex representations
●
The lower RNNs should compute 
lower-level features and the higher RNNs 
should compute higher-level features.
●
Multi-layer RNNs are also called stacked 
RNNs.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences2.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences2
text:
Multi-Layer Recurrent Neural Networks
●
Multi-layer RNNs in practice
○
Multi-layer or stacked RNNs allow a network to compute more complex representations
○
they work better than just have one layer of high-dimensional encodings!
●
The lower RNNs should compute lower-level features and the higher RNNs should 
compute higher-level features.
○
High-performing RNNs are usually multi-layer (but aren’t as deep as convolutional or feed-forward 
networks)
○
For example: In a 2017 paper, Britz et al. ﬁnd that for Neural Machine Translation, 2 to 4 layers is best 
for the encoder RNN, and 4 layers is best for the decoder RNN 
○
Often 2 layers is a lot better than 1, and 3 might be a little better than 2
●
Usually, skip-connections/dense-connections are needed to train deeper RNNs 
(e.g., 8 layers)
○
Transformer-based networks (e.g., BERT) are usually deeper, like 12 or 24 layers.
○
You will learn about Transformers later; they have a lot of skipping-like connections
Credit Chris Manning
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences2.pdf

training the gradient might vanish or explode! The result is that I will not learn long term dependencies :(

text:
RNN Training Tricks
(Pascanu et al., 2013; Bengio et al., 2013; Gal and Ghahramani, 2016;Morishita et al., 2017)
●
Mini-batch creation strategies 
(eﬃcient computations)
●
Clipping gradients (avoid exploding 
gradients)
●
Leaky integration (propagate 
long-term dependencies)
●
Momentum (cheap 2nd order)
●
Dropout (avoid overﬁtting)
●
Initialization (start in right ballpark 
avoids exploding/vanishing)
●
Sparse Gradients (symmetry 
breaking)
●
Gradient propagation regularizer 
(avoid vanishing gradient)
●
Gated self-loops (LSTM & GRU, 
reduces vanishing gradient)

text:
LSTM
LSTM networks were created with the following objectives in mind:
1.
Addressing the challenge of long-term dependencies encountered by recurrent 
neural networks (RNNs) caused by the vanishing gradient issue.
2.
Distinguishing themselves from traditional feedforward neural networks through 
the incorporation of feedback connections.
3.
Preserving valuable information from prior data within a sequence to facilitate the 
analysis of new data points.
4.
Demonstrating remarkable proﬁciency in handling data sequences like text, 
speech, and general time-series data.

text:
Seq2Seq
Used to convert one sequence into 
another
●
Both sequences might have 
different lengths
●
Based on an Encoder-decoder 
architecture
●
Two RNNs (typically LSTMs or 
GRUs)
●
Can be deterministic or variational
Applications:
●
Machine translation
●
Question answering
●
Dialogue models 
(conversational agents)
●
Summarization
●
Code generation
…

text:
Seq2Seq: a bit of math
Points worth noting:
1.
The input/source embedding layer,     , 
and the output/target embedding layer,     
    , are two different embedding layers 
with their own parameters.
2.
Linear layer to convert a decoder’s hidden 
state      into one of the possible words      . 
3.
The word      is then (often) used as input 
for the next word generation.

text:
Additive Attention
Bahdanau et al. Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate - ICLR 2015
Bottleneck Problem:
“A 
potential 
issue 
with 
this 
encoder–decoder approach is that a 
neural network needs to be able to 
compress all the necessary information 
of a source sentence into a ﬁxed-length 
vector. This may make it diﬃcult for the 
neural network to cope with long 
sentences, especially those that are 
longer than the sentences in the training 
corpus.”
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01c_1 - Deep Learning for Sequences2.pdf

text:
Summary
●
Vision Transformers have been a super hot topic the past ~ 3-4 years.
●
Very different architecture vs traditional CNNs
●
Applications to all tasks: classiﬁcation, detection, segmentation, etc
●
Vison transformers are an evolution, not a revolution.
○
We can still fundamentally solve the same problems as with CNNs.
○
Main beneﬁt is speed: Matrix multiply is more hardwarefriendly than convolution, so ViTs with same 
FLOPs as CNNs can train and run much faster
Credits EECS-498
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01d_2 - Transformers.pdf

Some of the most fundamental problems can be modeled with graphs.

text:
Why graphs are Harder?
IMAGES
●
Locality (neighbourhood).
●
Fixed number of neighbours.
●
Neighbours have a deﬁnite ordering.
GRAPHS
●
Locality (neighbourhood).
●
Variable number of neighbours.
●
Neighbours have no ordering.
We have this "certainty" in images
You can have a total order, we have a "top left" pixel. This does not happen in graphs.
[NOTE] We have this "certainty" in images
[NOTE] Some are loosely connected, some fully connected. The degree of a node,
[NOTE] You can have a total order, we have a "top left" pixel. This does not happen in graphs.

text:
Convolutional Neural Network invariances
●
Convolutional neural nets respect translational invariance
●
Patterns are interesting irrespective of where they are in the image
●
Locality: neighbouring pixels relate much more strongly than distant ones
●
Ok for images … but what about arbitrary graphs?
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
so regardless of where it is, as long as it sees the same pattern, it responds in the same way, irrespective of where it is.
[NOTE] so regardless of where it is, as long as it sees the same pattern, it responds in the same way, irrespective of where it is.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

text:
Isomorphism-preserving transformation
●
The nodes of a graph are not assumed to be in any order 
●
That is, we would like to get the same results for two isomorphic graphs
●
To see how to enforce this, we will deﬁne new terms:
Permutation invariance and equivariance
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
Isomorphic: a one-to-one mapping between the nodes exists.
Key requirements for isomorphic graphs.
[NOTE] Isomorphic: a one-to-one mapping between the nodes exists.
[NOTE] Key requirements for isomorphic graphs.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 01e - GNN
text:
Learning on sets: Setup
●
For now, assume the graph has no edges (e.g. set of nodes, V). 
●
Let xi ∈ ℝk be the features of node i.
●
We can stack them into a node feature matrix X of shape n x k:
●
That is, the i-th row of X corresponds to xi
●
Note that, by doing so, we have speciﬁed a node ordering!
We would like the result of any neural networks to not depend on this.
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
We have implicitly introduced a "total order" but there is no order! We need an order to 
represent it. Summation or average are linear and don't care about the order.
[NOTE] We have implicitly introduced a "total order" but there is no order! We need an order to represent it. Summation or average are linear and don't care about the order.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 01e - GNN
text:
Permutations and permutation matrices
●
It will be useful to think about the operations that change the node order
○
Such operations are known as permutations (there are n! of them) 
○
e.g. a permutation (2,4,1,3) means y1←x2, y2←x4, y3←x1, y4←x3.
 
●
To stay within linear algebra, each permutation deﬁnes an n x n matrix
○
Such matrices are called permutation matrices
○
They have exactly one 1 in every row and column, and zeros everywhere else
○
Their effect when left-multiplied is to permute the rows of X, like so:
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 01e - GNN
text:
Permutation Invariance
●
We want to design functions f(X) over sets that will not depend on the order
●
Equivalently, applying a permutation matrix shouldn’t modify the result!
●
We arrive at a useful notion of permutation invariance. 
We say that f(X) is permutation invariant if, for all permutation matrices P:
●
One very generic form is the Deep Sets model (Zaheer et al., NeurIPS’17): 
where 𝜓 and 𝜙 are (learnable) functions, e.g. MLPs.
○
The sum aggregation is critical! (other choices possible, e.g. max or avg)
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
The permutation is not impactful on the result of the function.
[NOTE] The permutation is not impactful on the result of the function.

text:
Permutation Equivariance
●
Permutation-invariant models are a good way to obtain set-level outputs
●
What if we would like answers at the node level?
○
We want to still be able to identify node outputs, which a permutation-invariant aggregator would 
destroy!
●
We may instead seek functions that don’t change the node order
○
i.e. if we permute the nodes, it doesn’t matter if we do it before or after the function!
●
Accordingly, we say that f(X) is permutation equivariant if, for all permutation 
matrices P:
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
function of permutation = permutation of function.
[NOTE] function of permutation = permutation of function.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 01e - GNN
text:
General blueprint for learning on sets
●
Equivariance mandates that each node’s row is unchanged by f. That is, we can think of 
equivariant set functions as transforming each node input xi into a latent vector hi: 
where 𝜓 is any function, applied in isolation to every node. 
Stacking hi yields H = f(X).
●
We arrive at a general blueprint: (stacking) equivariant function(s), potentially with an 
invariant tail---yields (m)any useful functions on sets! 
Here, ⨁ is a permutation-invariant aggregator (such as sum, avg or max).
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
compress all nodes in one representation. For the whole set.
transform the nodes independently, aggregate, transform the aggregation.
[NOTE] compress all nodes in one representation. For the whole set.
[NOTE] transform the nodes independently, aggregate, transform the aggregation.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

text:
Learning on graphs
●
Now we augment the set of nodes with edges between them.
○
That is, we consider general E ⊆ V x V.
●
We can represent these edges with an adjacency matrix, A, such that:
●
Further additions (e.g. edge features) are possible but ignored for simplicity.
●
Our main desiderata (permutation {in,equi}variance) still hold!
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

text:
Permutation invariance and equivariance on graphs
●
The main difference: node permutations now also accordingly act on the 
edges
●
We need to appropriately permute both rows and columns of A
○
When applying a permutation matrix P, this amounts to PAPT
●
We arrive at updated deﬁnitions of suitable functions 
f(X, A) over graphs:
Sets
Graphs

permutation matrix

text:
Locality on graphs: neighbourhoods
●
On sets, we enforced equivariance by applying functions to every node in isolation
●
Graphs give us a broader context: a node’s neighbourhood
○
For a node i, its (1-hop) neighbourhood is commonly deﬁned as follows: 
N.B. we do not explicitly consider directed edges, and often we assume i ∈ Ni
●
Accordingly, we can extract the multiset of features in the neighbourhood 
and deﬁne a local function, g, as operating over this multiset: g(xi , XNi)
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 01e - GNN
text:
A recipe for graph neural networks
●
Now we can construct permutation equivariant functions, 
f(X, A), by appropriately applying the local function, g, over all neighbourhoods:
●
To ensure equivariance, we need g to not depend on the order of the vertices in XNi
○
Hence, g should be permutation invariant!
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN

text:
What’s in a GNN layer?
●
As mentioned, we construct permutation-equivariant functions f(X, A) over graphs 
by shared application of a local permutation-invariant g(xi, XNi).
○
We often refer to f as “GNN layer”
○
g as “diffusion”, “propagation”, “message passing”
●
Now we look at ways in which we can actually concretely deﬁne g.
○
Very intense area of research!
●
Fortunately, almost all proposed layers can be classiﬁed as one of three spatial 
“ﬂavours”
* slides from P.Veličković - Theoretical Foundations of GNN
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

Features of neighbours aggregated with ﬁxed weights, cij

○

Useful for homophilous graphs 
and scaling up

When edges encode label similarity

text:
Graph Convolutional Networks
The goal is to learn a function of signals/features on a graph G=(V,E) which takes as input:
●
A feature description xi for every node i; summarized in a N×D feature matrix X (N: number of nodes, D: 
number of input features)
●
A representative description of the graph structure in matrix form; typically in the form of an adjacency 
matrix A (or some function thereof)
and produces a node-level output Z (an N×F feature matrix, where F is the number of output features per node). 
Every neural network layer can then be written as a nonlinear function 
H(l+1)=f(H(l),A) 
with H(0)=X and H(L)=Z , L being the number of layers.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 01e - GNN.pdf

Types of Biases
I.e. consider the task of predicting the center of mass of a solar 
system comprised of n planets, whose attributes (e.g., mass, 
position, velocity, etc.) are denoted by {x1, x2, . . . , xn}. For 
such a computation, the order in which we consider the 
planets does not matter because the state can be described 
solely in terms of aggregated, averaged quantities.

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification
text:
Datasets: MNIST
Credit CSC321
MNIST dataset of handwritten digits
●
Categories: 10 digit classes
●
Source: Scans of handwritten zip codes from envelopes
●
Size: 60,000 training images and 10,000 test images, 
grayscale, of size 28 × 28
●
Normalization: centered within in the image, scaled to a 
consistent size
The assumption is that the digit recognizer would be part of a 
larger pipeline that segments and normalizes images.
In 1998, Yann LeCun and colleagues built a conv-net called 
LeNet which was able to classify digits with 98.9% test accuracy. 
It was good enough to be used in a system for automatically 
reading numbers on checks.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification
text:
Datasets: Caltech101
Credit CSC321
Caltech101 was the ﬁrst major object recognition dataset, collected in 2003.
Design decisions:
●
Categories: 101 object categories; open the dictionary to random 
pages and select from nouns which were associated with images
●
Source: ﬁnd candidates with Google Image Search, hand-select the 
ones that actually represent the object category
●
Number of examples: as many as possible per category
○
most machine learning benchmarking is done using a ﬁxed number of training 
examples per category (usually between 1 and 20)
●
Normalization:
○
Scale to be 300 pixels wide
○
Flip so that object is facing the same direction
○
Rotate certain object categories because their proposed algorithm couldn’t handle 
vertical objects
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification
text:
Datasets: Caltech101
Credit CSC321
●
Beware of dataset biases. These are 
idiosyncrasies of a dataset resulting from 
how it was collected or normalized. 
●
An algorithm can appear to have good 
training and test error, but fail to generalize 
if the training data doesn’t resemble the real 
world. 
●
E.g., here are the averages of all the images 
from some of the categories. The sizes and 
locations are a lot more regular than you 
would expect in the “real world.”
Low intraclass variability.
[NOTE] Low intraclass variability.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification
text:
Datasets: ImageNet
Credit CSC321
●
ImageNet is the modern object 
recognition benchmark dataset. 
●
It was introduced in 2009, and has led to 
amazing progress in object recognition 
since then.
●
Used for the ImageNet Large Scale 
Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC), 
an annual benchmark competition for 
object recognition algorithms Design 
decisions
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification
text:
Datasets: ImageNet
Credit CSC321
●
Categories: Taken from a lexical database called 
WordNet 
○
WordNet consists of “synsets”, or sets of synonymous words 
○
They tried to use as many of these as possible; almost 22,000 
as of 2010. 
○
Of these, they chose the 1000 most common for the ILSVRC 
○
The categories are really speciﬁc, e.g. hundreds of kinds of dogs 
●
Size: 1.2 million full-sized images for the ILSVRC 
●
Source: Results from image search engines, 
hand-labeled by Amazon Mechanical Turkers 
○
Labeling such speciﬁc categories was challenging; annotators 
had to be given the WordNet hierarchy, Wikipedia, etc. 
●
Normalization: none, although the contestants are free 
to do preprocessing
●
Images and object categories vary on a lot of 
dimensions
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification.pdf

Credit CS231n
Consider a single layer 𝑦 = 𝑊𝑥
The following could lead to tough optimization:
- Inputs x are not centered around zero (need large bias)
- Inputs x have different scaling per-element (entries in W will need to vary a lot)
Idea: force inputs to be “nicely scaled” at each layer!

text:
AlexNet
Wu and He, “Group Normalization”, ECCV 2018                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   Credit CS231n
Details/Retrospectives: 
- first use of ReLU 
- used Local Response Normalization (LRN) layers (not common 
anymore)
- heavy data augmentation 
- dropout 0.5 
- batch size 128 
- SGD Momentum 0.9 
- Learning rate 1e-2, reduced by 10 manually when val accuracy 
plateaus 
- L2 weight decay 5e-4
Also the fist time being used to some avail

text:
ResNet
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     Credit CS231n
Training ResNet in practice:
- Batch Normalization after every CONV layer
- Xavier initialization from He et al.
- SGD + Momentum (0.9)
- Learning rate: 0.1, divided by 10 when validation error plateaus
- Mini-batch size 256
- Weight decay of 1e-5
- No dropout used
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification2
text:
Datasets: MNIST
Credit CSC321
MNIST dataset of handwritten digits
●
Categories: 10 digit classes
●
Source: Scans of handwritten zip codes from envelopes
●
Size: 60,000 training images and 10,000 test images, 
grayscale, of size 28 × 28
●
Normalization: centered within in the image, scaled to a 
consistent size
The assumption is that the digit recognizer would be part of a 
larger pipeline that segments and normalizes images.
In 1998, Yann LeCun and colleagues built a conv-net called 
LeNet which was able to classify digits with 98.9% test accuracy. 
It was good enough to be used in a system for automatically 
reading numbers on checks.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification2.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification2
text:
Datasets: Caltech101
Credit CSC321
Caltech101 was the ﬁrst major object recognition dataset, collected in 2003.
Design decisions:
●
Categories: 101 object categories; open the dictionary to random 
pages and select from nouns which were associated with images
●
Source: ﬁnd candidates with Google Image Search, hand-select the 
ones that actually represent the object category
●
Number of examples: as many as possible per category
○
most machine learning benchmarking is done using a ﬁxed number of training 
examples per category (usually between 1 and 20)
●
Normalization:
○
Scale to be 300 pixels wide
○
Flip so that object is facing the same direction
○
Rotate certain object categories because their proposed algorithm couldn’t handle 
vertical objects
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification2.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification2
text:
Datasets: Caltech101
Credit CSC321
●
Beware of dataset biases. These are 
idiosyncrasies of a dataset resulting from 
how it was collected or normalized. 
●
An algorithm can appear to have good 
training and test error, but fail to generalize 
if the training data doesn’t resemble the real 
world. 
●
E.g., here are the averages of all the images 
from some of the categories. The sizes and 
locations are a lot more regular than you 
would expect in the “real world.”
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification2.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification2
text:
Datasets: ImageNet
Credit CSC321
●
ImageNet is the modern object 
recognition benchmark dataset. 
●
It was introduced in 2009, and has led to 
amazing progress in object recognition 
since then.
●
Used for the ImageNet Large Scale 
Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC), 
an annual benchmark competition for 
object recognition algorithms Design 
decisions
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification2.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification2
text:
Datasets: ImageNet
Credit CSC321
●
Categories: Taken from a lexical database called 
WordNet 
○
WordNet consists of “synsets”, or sets of synonymous words 
○
They tried to use as many of these as possible; almost 22,000 
as of 2010. 
○
Of these, they chose the 1000 most common for the ILSVRC 
○
The categories are really speciﬁc, e.g. hundreds of kinds of dogs 
●
Size: 1.2 million full-sized images for the ILSVRC 
●
Source: Results from image search engines, 
hand-labeled by Amazon Mechanical Turkers 
○
Labeling such speciﬁc categories was challenging; annotators 
had to be given the WordNet hierarchy, Wikipedia, etc. 
●
Normalization: none, although the contestants are free 
to do preprocessing
●
Images and object categories vary on a lot of 
dimensions

text:
Batch Normalization
Credit CS231n
Consider a single layer 𝑦 = 𝑊𝑥
The following could lead to tough optimization:
- Inputs x are not centered around zero (need large bias)
- Inputs x have different scaling per-element (entries in W will need to vary a lot)
Idea: force inputs to be “nicely scaled” at each layer!

why is it [55x55x48] x 2?
-
Trained on GTX 580 GPU with only 3 GB of memory.
-
Network spread across 2 GPUs, half the neurons (feature 
maps) on each GPU.

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 02 - Image Classification2
text:
AlexNet
Wu and He, “Group Normalization”, ECCV 2018                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   Credit CS231n
Details/Retrospectives: 
- first use of ReLU 
- used Local Response Normalization (LRN) layers (not common 
anymore)
- heavy data augmentation 
- dropout 0.5 
- batch size 128 
- SGD Momentum 0.9 
- Learning rate 1e-2, reduced by 10 manually when val accuracy 
plateaus 
- L2 weight decay 5e-4
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification2.pdf

text:
ResNet
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     Credit CS231n
Training ResNet in practice:
- Batch Normalization after every CONV layer
- Xavier initialization from He et al.
- SGD + Momentum (0.9)
- Learning rate: 0.1, divided by 10 when validation error plateaus
- Mini-batch size 256
- Weight decay of 1e-5
- No dropout used
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 02 - Image Classification2.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 04 - Object Detection
text:
Feature extraction + classiﬁcation: Viola-Jones Face Detector
Viola and Jones. Rapid object detection using a boosted cascade of simple features. CVPR 2001
Credit CV3DST | prof. Leal-Taixé
●
A seminal approach to real-time object detection
●
Training is slow, but detection is very fast
●
Key ideas
○
Integral images for fast feature evaluation
○
Boosting for feature selection
○
Attentional cascade for fast rejection of non-face windows
●
Learning multiple weak learners to build a strong classiﬁer
●
That is, make many small decisions and combine them for a stronger ﬁnal 
decision
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 04 - Object Detection.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 04 - Object Detection
text:
Feature extraction + classiﬁcation: Viola-Jones Face Detector
Viola and Jones. Rapid object detection using a boosted cascade of simple features. CVPR 2001
Credit CV3DST | prof. Leal-Taixé
Feature Extraction:
●
The integral image computes a value at 
each pixel (x,y) that is the sum of the pixel 
values above and to the left of (x,y), 
inclusive.
●
This can quickly be computed in one pass 
through the image
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 04 - Object Detection.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 04 - Object Detection
text:
Feature extraction + classiﬁcation: Viola-Jones Face Detector
Viola and Jones. Rapid object detection using a boosted cascade of simple features. CVPR 2001
Credit CV3DST | prof. Leal-Taixé
Feature Extraction:
●
Let A,B,C,D be the values of the integral 
image at the corners of a rectangle
●
Then the sum of original image values 
within the rectangle can be computed: 
sum = A – B – C + D
●
Only 3 additions are required for any size 
of rectangle!
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 04 - Object Detection.pdf

text:
YOLO: You Only Look Once
Each cell predicts:
-
For each bounding box:
-
4 coordinates (x, y, w, h)
-
1 conﬁdence value
-
Some number of class 
probabilities
For Pascal VOC:
-
7x7 grid
-
2 bounding boxes / cell
-
20 classes
7 x 7 x (2 x 5 + 20) = 7 x 7 x 30 tensor = 1470 outputs
This parameterization 
ﬁxes the output size

text:
Problems of two and one-stage detectors:
Liu et al. “SSD: Single shot multibox detector”. ECCV 2016
Credit CV3DST | prof. Leal-Taixé 
Two-stage detectors:
– Classiﬁcation only work on “interesting” foreground regions (proposals, ~1-2k). Most 
background examples are already ﬁltered out.
– Class balance between foreground and background objects is manageable. 
– Classiﬁer can concentrate on analyzing proposals with rich information content
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 04 - Object Detection.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 04 - Object Detection
text:
Problems of two and one-stage detectors:
Liu et al. “SSD: Single shot multibox detector”. ECCV 2016
Credit CV3DST | prof. Leal-Taixé 
One-stage detectors:
– Many locations need to be analyzed (100k) densely covering the image 
-foreground-background imbalance
– Hard negative mining is useful, but not suﬃcient

DETR – end-to-end object detection with Transformers
Kirilov et al. End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers - ECCV 2020
CV3DST | Prof. Leal-Taixé
DETR uses a conventional CNN backbone to learn a 2D representation of an
input image.
The CNN could be frozen or updated in the pipeline.
[NOTE] The CNN could be frozen or updated in the pipeline.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 04 - Object Detection.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 04 - Object Detection
text:
DETR – end-to-end object detection with Transformers
Kirilov et al. End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers - ECCV 2020
CV3DST | Prof. Leal-Taixé
The model ﬂattens it and supplements it with a positional encoding before
passing it into a transformer encoder.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 04 - Object Detection.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 04 - Object Detection
text:
DETR – end-to-end object detection with Transformers
Kirilov et al. End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers - ECCV 2020
CV3DST | Prof. Leal-Taixé
A transformer ﬁrst encodes the given input, and then the decoder takes as input
a small ﬁxed number of learned positional embeddings, which we call object
queries, and additionally attends to the encoder output.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 04 - Object Detection.pdf

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 04 - Object Detection
text:
DETR – end-to-end object detection with Transformers
Kirilov et al. End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers - ECCV 2020
CV3DST | Prof. Leal-Taixé
Each output embedding of the decoder is passed to a shared feed forward
network (FFN) that predicts either a detection (class and bounding box) or a “no
object” class.
Again, the "boxes" are fixed so they are either assigned a class + position or not.
from image space to bounding box space.
[NOTE] Again, the "boxes" are fixed so they are either assigned a class + position or not.
[NOTE] from image space to bounding box space.

text:
Optical Flow
●
Input: 2 consecutive images (e.g. 
from a video)
●
Output: displacement of every pixel 
from image A to image B
●
Results in the “perceived” 2D motion, 
not the real motion of the object
●
Some applications: tracking, video 
recognition, motion magniﬁcation
Credit CV3DST | Prof. Leal-Taixé

text:
Articulated Pose Estimation
Deﬁnition: Localization of arranged keypoints (e.g. 
joints, limbs, parts, landmarks etc.) on a 
deformable object
What is “articulated” pose? vs non-articulated?
Articulate: The ability to tell parts apart from the 
whole, vs. holistic pose detection - knowing the 
overall orientation of the entire object.
What is “detection”/”estimation” in this case?
non-articulated
articulated
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI

text:
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI
Active Shape and Appearance Models
[Cootes et al 1992] (and more derivative 
papers) >10,000c
Big idea:
-
Object’s boundary (under any 
deformation) will follow a probable 
shape
-
Boundary is deﬁned by control points
-
Control points will fall in a distribution 
(point distribution model)
-
We can model the distribution with a 
lower-rank representation (PCA)
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 05 - Video.pdf

“deformation” mode:

text:

Active Shape and Appearance Models
Active Appearance Models [Cootes et al 
1998]
Big Idea
-
Instead of boundary look for 
appearance matching on the pixel 
intensity
-
Still using deformation model
-
Iteratively match a synthesized 
template to the image until 
convergence
AAMs were widely successful!

text:
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI
Active Shape and Appearance Models
What are the major drawbacks of AAMs?
-
Still using a statistical / distributional 
model
-
Model is limited: assumptive, biased
-
Computational costs
-
Sensitive to initialization
-
Holistic: Assume the whole object 
deforms linearly along the principal 
components
What can we do?
You
Asking

text:
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI
Deformable Parts Models
[Felzenszwalb 2008] (>3,800c)
Big ideas
-
Parts-based modeling instead of 
holistic (deformable) “templates”
-
Parts can move individually
-
Parts are connected in a holistic 
“spring” based model
-
Multi-scale detection
HoG - Histogram of Gradients

The original DPM was primarily an 
object detection model, rather than a 
pose estimation model.
What are some of the major 
drawbacks you can think of?
-
HoG features are arbitrary, biased
-
Viewpoints and orientation of the 
object matter a great deal
-
Doesn’t handle occlusion
What can we do?

text:
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI
Real-Time Pose Recognition from Single Depth Image
[Shotton et al 2011] >4,700c
Big ideas
-
Use random forests for part detection, 
very fast inference, visual features are 
learned
-
Using depth information (but works 
well on RGB [Rogez et al 2008]) with 
the MSFT Kinect sensor
-
Synthetic data generation
-
Using GPUs! (way before it was cool)
Popularized by the Kinect and original 
XBox
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 05 - Video.pdf

text:

Segmentation
Cootes 
et al ‘98
Shotton

Cootes

Felzenszwalb

doc_id: Image and Video Understanding/IVU - 05 - Video
text:
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI
Major Drawbacks
Existing methods, while highly capable, still 
had crippling downsides:
-
Rely on accurate Initialization 
(detection)
-
Iterative, sequential ﬁtting algorithms 
(convergence not guaranteed)
-
2D in most cases; 2.5D requires a 
special camera (expensive)
-
Can’t handle occlusions well
-
Arbitrary “hand crafted” features (bias)
-
Limited data (bias)
What shall we do?
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Image and Video Understanding\IVU - 05 - Video.pdf

text:
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI
The perfect pose estimation algorithm?
We’ve been at it for 20+ years.
How can we make the perfect pose 
estimation algorithm?
Requirements (desired):
-
Immediate detection (not init+ﬁtting)
-
Unbiased, as much as possible
-
Cheap, Fast
-
Handle occlusions gracefully
How would you build the perfect algorithm?
You
Asking

text:
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI
DeepPose
What have we learned?
-
Direct regression, e.g. asking the network 
to predict (x,y) positions, is possible
-
Deep CNNs are able to reason about the 
pose holistically, predict invisible joints 
without explicit algorithmic intervention
-
Multi-scale is important
What do you think are the downsides?
Hints: 
-
Is direct (x,y) regression Optimal?
-
What are some parts of the network that 
may be ineﬃcient?
You

text:
Credit prof. Shilkrot - Course 150CVI
DeepPose
What have we learned?
-
Direct regression, e.g. asking the network 
to predict (x,y) positions, is possible
-
Deep CNNs are able to reason about the 
pose holistically, predict invisible joints 
without explicit algorithmic intervention
-
Multi-scale is important
What do you think are the downsides?
Hints: 
-
Is direct (x,y) regression Optimal?
-
What are some parts of the network that 
may be ineﬃcient?
You
Asking
NO! an exact point is suboptimal, 
instead we would like to have a 
distribution around a point (think 
about annotation)
Sort of a gaussian on the point, don't
need to find the "exact" point.
[NOTE] Sort of a gaussian on the point, don't
need to find the "exact" point.

text:
input = context vector
This is small! You are compressing it in a small vector. How to fix it? Attention!
Also, the problem that as you go on, you will 
forget more and more about the context vector.
We are also missing spatial information; we would like something more localized. Before going to attention, we can 
break the feature map into smaller chunks, use those chunks to generate the output, or just go with attention.

text:
Information Retrieval
• Information Retrieval (IR) is about finding 
material (usually documents) of an unstructured
nature (usually text) that satisfies an information 
need from within large digital collections.
– These days we frequently think of web search first, 
but there are many other cases:
• E-mail search
• Searching your laptop
• Corporate knowledge bases
• Legal information retrieval
3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture0-intro
text:
IR Origins
•
The “information overload” 
problem is much older than you 
may think, much before the WEB
•
Origins in period immediately 
after World War II
–
Tremendous scientific progress 
during the war
–
Rapid growth in the number of 
scientific publications available
•
The “Memex Machine”  
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Memex
–
Conceived by Vannevar Bush, 
President Roosevelt's science advisor
–
Foreshadows the development of 
hypertext (the Web) and information 
retrieval systems
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture0-intro
text:
The Memex Machine
Consider a future device for individual use, which 
is a sort of mechanized private file and library. It 
needs a name, and, to coin one at random, 
“memex” will do. A memex is a device in which 
an individual stores all his books, records, and 
communications, and which is mechanized so 
that it may be consulted with exceeding speed 
and flexibility. It is an enlarged intimate 
supplement to his memory. 
It consists of a desk, and while it can presumably 
be operated from a distance, it is primarily the 
piece of furniture at which he works. On the top 
are slanting translucent screens, on which 
material can be projected for convenient reading. 
There is a keyboard and sets of buttons and 
levers. Otherwise, it looks like an ordinary desk.
(Vannevar Bush; As We May Think; 
Atlantic Monthly; July 1945)
Only to browse, not to search.
[NOTE] Only to browse, not to search.

– thus, becoming the preferred source of information finding

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture0-intro
text:
WSE definition
• A Web Search Engine is a complex software system 
designed to search for information on the World Wide Web 
– Users enter keywords or phrases (queries), and the WSE returns a 
ranked list of relevant websites
• A Web Search Engine mainly consists of:
– Crawler (Spider/Bot): Collects web pages
– Indexing System: Organizes, stores and indexes pages for fast 
retrieval
– Query processor: Determines the relevance of pages to a given 
query, and rank the relevant pages
WSE vs IR: 
Downloads pages and creates a database?
Permeates faster retrival
[NOTE] WSE vs IR:
[NOTE] Downloads pages and creates a database?
[NOTE] Permeates faster retrival

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture0-intro
text:
Generative AI and IR
• ChatGPT / Gemini [since 2022] are conversational  
chatbots based on Generative AI
– Large Language Model (LLM) 
– LLMs trained (through self-supervised learning) on huge text 
corpora to model the probability distribution of language
– chatbots generate responses, given a long enough input text (a 
prompt, expressed in natural language), token-by-token (terms or 
portions of terms) based on learned statistical patterns
– In principle, at inference time, they do not need to query an external 
database of documents … but they do when specific triggers occur
• For example, if a prompt requires real-time information, the chatbot
autonomously write a query for a search engine (e.g., for Google or 
Bing), retrieve the top results, and use them to synthesize the answer
• This tries to avoid hallucinations, but in this last case a chatbot
works like a  RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
all the possible next words given all the previous ones.
[NOTE] all the possible next words given all the previous ones.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture0-intro
text:
IR systems vs. Generative AI
• Search Engines vs. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
– IR systems (search engines) can now operate in a pipeline that in 
some cases includes Generative-AI
– In such architectures, retrieval provides relevant documents (or 
passages/chunks of these documents)
– In some cases, the IR system performs content synthesis and 
generation using LLM
• For example, AI overview by Google
– Issues:
• AI Overviews can hallucinate when retrieval returns irrelevant 
documents, especially for obscure or complex queries.
• Generative AI is expensive, and thus for efficiency reasons, it is not 
used for Navigational and Simple Queries
• In summary: if the query is too simple, too fresh, or also too risky, the 
classic "10 blue links" take over.
Might be due to IR failure too.
[NOTE] Might be due to IR failure too.
[NOTE] If something has happened a minute ago, chances are it's not indexd for LLM yet.
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

Actors in “Traditional” Web Search Engines
•
User’s perspective: accessing 
information
– Relevance (Effectiveness)
– Speed (Efficiency)
•
Search engine’s perspective: monetization
– attract more users
– increase the ads (advertising) revenue
– reduce the operational costs
•
Advertiser’s perspective: publicity
– attract more users to their site
– pay little
Keyword-based advertising
•
Users search → produces 
sorted list of links + sponsored 
ads
•
Advertisers bid on keywords 
(e.g., “best running shoes”).
•
Revenue = pay-per-click (PPC)
•
More clicks, more money
Note that traditional SEs were 
designed to drive web traffic
to publisher websites relevant 
to the query
Publisher struggled to appear 
on the top results of SERPs 
(SEO), but this model works 
only if the SE shows multiple 
blue links

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture0-intro
text:
AI impact on SE Business Models
1.
Advertising Evolution (Ads Inside the AI-generated Answer)
2.
Consumer Subscriptions (Freemium Search)
–
Massive computing power required by Generative AI: expensive wrt traditional 
searches
3.
Data Licensing (significant new cost for SE companies)
–
Old model, SEs scraped publishers' websites for free, and in exchange, sent them 
free user traffic
–
AI overview over search results often gives the user the answer without sending 
traffic back to the original website
–
Publishers ask for licensing deals (e.g., Reddit, News Corp) just to legally train their 
AI models and display their content in real-time as AI overview
4.
Zero-click searches are reshaping SEO (SE Optimization) strategies
–
up to 60-70% of local searches (services near to me) are now becoming "zero-
click”, as they are satified by AI-generated summary
–
small businesses can no longer just fight to be "Rank #1." They have to fight to be 
the source the AI quotes. This new strategy is called GEO (Generative Engine 
Optimization) or AEO (Answer Engine Optimization). Generative AI doesn't use the whole document
[NOTE] Generative AI doesn't use the whole document

The Web

demographics

understand the query intent)
– things are changing with generative AI and natural language long 
queries
– changing information needs during sessions
– little patience (few queries posed & few answers seen)

text:
Basic assumptions of Information Retrieval

text:
Let’s start with a simple Boolean
retrieval model 
(AND/OR/NOT operators)
• Which plays of Shakespeare contain the following words?
         Brutus AND Caesar  but NOT Calpurnia
• One could grep all of Shakespeare’s plays for Brutus and 
Caesar, then strip out lines containing Calpurnia?
• Why is that not the answer?
– Slow (for large corpora)
– NOT Calpurnia is non-trivial
– Other operations (e.g., find the word Romans near countrymen) 
not feasible
– Ranked retrieval (best documents to return)
• Later lectures
8
Sec. 1.1

Can’t build the matrix
• 500K x 1M binary matrix has half-a-trillion 0’s 
and 1’s.    
• 106 106 / 2  =  1012 / 2    <   240 / 2    (half Terabits, 64 GB)
• No more than one billion 1’s (since the 
collection only contains 1 G = 230 words)
– percentage of 1’s:     100 * (109  /  (1012 / 2)) = 0.2%
– the matrix is extremely sparse.
• What’s a better representation?
– Only record the 1 positions (1 G in our case)

text:
Initial stages of text processing
• Tokenization
– Cut character sequence into word tokens
• Deal with “John’s”
• Normalization
– Map text and query term to same form
• You want U.S.A. and USA to match
– Lowercase, etc. 
• Stemming
– We may wish different forms to match a common prefix
• authorize, authorization
• Stop words
– We may omit very common words (or not)
• the, a, to, of
• Finally, normalized terms are included in the IR system’s 
dictionary

text:
Set-intersection of two postings lists
(AND query with nextGEQ)

p1 = [1, 4, 5, 8]
p2 = [2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10]
def intersect(p1, p2):
answer = []
i = 0
j = 0
while i != len(p1) and j != len(p2):
if p1[i] == p2[j] :
answer.append(p1[i])
i = i+1
j = j+1
elif p1[i] < p2[j]:
i = nextGEQ(p1, p2[j])
else:
j = nextGEQ(p2, p1[i])
return answer
nextGEQ(p1, p2[j]) returns the 
pointer to the first docId in list p1 
which is Greater than or EQual to 
p2[j]
nextGEQ(p2, p1[i]) returns the 
pointer to the first docId in 
the list p2 which is Greater 
than or EQual to p1[i]
nextGEQ(p,d) can be realized with a linear search. We can improve its
implementation to skip posting with  Binary (Exponential) Search, but in practice 
skip pointers are used for (see later).

text:
Where do we place skips?
• Tradeoff:
– More skips → shorter skip spans  more likely to 
skip.  But lots of comparisons to skip pointers.
– Fewer skips → few pointer comparison, but then 
long skip spans  few successful skips.

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture1-boolean model
text:
Placing skips
• Simple heuristic: for postings of length L, use 𝐿 evenly-
spaced skip pointers     [Moffat and Zobel 1996]
– This ignores the distribution of query terms.
– Easy if the index is relatively static; harder if  L keeps changing 
because of updates.
• Researchers showed good performance gains from 
skipping
– wrt a system architecture designed to optimize for the large 
memory spaces and multiple CPU cores [Strohman and Croft (2007)] 
• Skip lists can be combined with compression of 
portions/blocks of the lists, thus avoiding decompressing 
skipped sub-lists/blocks
Sec. 2.3

text:
Set-union of two postings lists
(OR query)
41
def union(p1, p2):
    answer = []
    while p1 != [] and p2 != []:
        if p1[0] == p2[0] :
            answer.append(p1[0])
            p1 = p1[1:]
            p2 = p2[1:]
        elif p1[0] < p2[0]:
            answer.append(p1[0])
            p1 = p1[1:]
        else:
            answer.append(p2[0])
            p2 = p2[1:]
    if p1 == [] and p2 != []:
        answer.extend(p2)
    if p2 == [] and p1 != []:
        answer.extend(p1)
    return answer

takes O(x+y) operations, as the 
two lists must be completely 
scanned 
Crucial: postings sorted by docID

OR query processing are less efficient that

We cannot use nextGEQ nor skip pointers

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture1-boolean model
text:
Boolean queries: Exact match
• The Boolean retrieval model is being able to ask a 
query that is a Boolean expression:
– Boolean Queries are queries using AND, OR and NOT 
to join query terms
• View each document as a set of words
• Are precise: document matches condition or not.
– Perhaps the simplest model to build an IR system
• Primary commercial retrieval tool for 3 decades. 
• Many search systems you still use are Boolean:
– Email, library catalog, etc.
50
Sec. 1.3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture1-boolean model.pdf

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture1-boolean model
text:
Why Boolean Retrieval Works
• Boolean operators approximate natural language
– Find documents about a good party that is not over
• AND can discover relationships between concepts
– good party
• OR can discover alternate terminology
– excellent party, wild party, etc.
• NOT can exclude unintended meanings
– Republican party
(good OR excellent OR wild) AND Party AND 
NOT (Republican AND party)
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture1-boolean model.pdf

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture1-boolean model
text:
Why Boolean Retrieval Fails
• Natural language is way more complex
• AND “discovers” nonexistent relationships
– Terms in different sentences, paragraphs, …
• Guessing terminology for OR is hard
– good, nice, excellent, outstanding, awesome, …
• Guessing terms to exclude is even harder!
– Republican party, party to a lawsuit, …

good. it can be "bad party ......  good music"

language

No control over size of result 
set: either too many 
documents or none
– All documents in the result set 
are considered “equally good”

No support for partial matches

text:
Boolean IR Model 
+ new proximity and partial match operators
WestLaw   http://www.westlaw.com/
(Legal Information Retrieval)
• Largest “commercial” legal search service (started 1975; 
ranking added 1992; new federated search added 2010)
• Tens of terabytes of data; ~700,000 users
• Majority of users still use Boolean queries, although other 
methods are available
• Example query:
– What is the statute of limitations in cases involving the federal tort 
claims act?
– LIMIT! /3 STATUTE ACTION /S FEDERAL /2 TORT /3 CLAIM
• /s, /p, and /k ask for matches in the same sentence, same 
paragraph or within k words respectively, ! gives a trailing 
wildcard query 
• SPACE is disjunction, not conjunction! 
54
Sec. 1.4
[NOTE] Solves the problem of "good" and "party"
[NOTE] it can be limits, limitations, limit
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture1-boolean model.pdf

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture1-boolean model
text:
• Another example query:
– Requirements for disabled people to be able to access a workplace
– disabl! /p access! /s work-site work-place (employment /3 place)
– operator ! is a wildcard, a kind of placeholder for a substring
• Long, precise queries; proximity and partial match operators; 
incrementally query developing; not like web search
• Many professional searchers still like Boolean search
– You know exactly what you are getting
– The recall is very important in Legal IR, so people are interested in retrieving 
all possible relevant cases 
• But this doesn't mean that it actually works better….

– romans countrymen
• Each of these biwords is now a dictionary term
• Two-word phrase query-processing is now immediate.

text:
Simple picture – complications
▪Web crawling isn’t feasible with one machine
▪All the above steps need to be distributed/parallelized
▪Malicious pages
▪Spam pages – not crawled to avoid polluting the storage 
▪Spider traps – including dynamically generated (forcing an 
infinite number of download requests)
▪Even non-malicious pages pose challenges
▪Variance of latency/bandwidth to remote servers
▪Webmasters’ stipulations
▪How “deep” should you crawl a site’s URL hierarchy?
▪Site mirrors and duplicate pages
▪Politeness – don’t hit a server too often

▪Ensure it passes certain URL filter tests
▪Check if it is already in the frontier, or was already visited

▪Solutions
▪DNS caching
▪Batch DNS resolver – collects

text:
• Web partitioning
– hash-based partitioning: based on the MD5 hashes of 
either canonical URLs or host names
– site-based partitioning: all pages of a website are assigned 
to the same crawler node. 
Motivation: with URL/hash-based partitioning URLs 
managed by the same web host may be hashed to distinct 
partitions, thus leading to politeness issues as crawling 
decisions (made by individual nodes) are not coordinated
• Fault tolerance
– when a crawling node dies, its URLs are redistributed over 
the remaining crawler nodes
Web Partitioning and Fault Tolerance

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture16-crawling
text:
URL frontier: two main considerations
▪Politeness: do not hit a web server too frequently
▪Freshness: crawl some pages more often than others
▪E.g., pages (such as News sites) whose content changes 
often
These goals may conflict with each other.
Sec. 20.2.3
31

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture16-crawling
text:
Politeness – challenges
▪Even if we restrict only one thread to fetch from a 
host, the thread can hit it repeatedly
▪Common heuristic: insert time gap between 
successive requests to a host
Sec. 20.2.3
32

text:
• A state-of-the-art (continuous) web crawler maintains 
two separate queues for prioritizing the download of 
URLs
– discovery queue
– to download pages not previously downloaded, pointed 
by already discovered links
– tries to increase the coverage of the crawler
– refreshing queue
– re-downloads already downloaded pages
– tries to increase the freshness of the repository
Continuous Crawler, URL Prioritization, 
Refreshing

Local/cached copies of remote data sources to improve performance or

Web search engine copies locally portions of the web and then indexes them to 
help users navigate the web.

The remote source is updated independently without pushing updates
•
Client must periodically poll the source to detect changes and refresh its copy.

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture16-crawling
text:
• Repository refreshing
• Average freshness as two distinct policies:
1.
Average number of fresh pages as HIGH as possible: F(ei; t)
2.
Average age of pages as LOW as possible:  A(ei; t)
• When updating?
•
Uniform: regardless of the rates of changes
•
Proportional: more often if pages change more frequently
•
In a simulated environment, uniform is surprisingly better
•
To improve overall freshness, we must penalize pages changing 
too often

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture16-crawling
text:
• How do general-purpose SEs solve this the freshness issue?
• They use two or more refreshing queues, with different turnout 
times
• A queue for web sites to be refreshed several time a day
• A queue for web sites to be refreshed daily
• A queue for web sites to be refreshed weekly, for popular or 
relevant one (e.g., relevant wrt PageRank)
• A larger queue for pages to be refreshed every month or 
every few months
Revisit policy of Web pages

text:
Normalization: other languages
▪Accents: e.g., French résumé vs. resume.
▪Umlauts: e.g., German: Tuebingen vs. Tübingen
▪Should be equivalent
▪Most important criterion:
▪How are your users like to write their queries for these 
words?
▪Even in languages that standardly have accents, users 
often may not type them
▪Often best to normalize to a de-accented term
▪Tuebingen, Tübingen, Tubingen  → Tubingen
▪Crucial: Need to “normalize” indexed text as well as 
query terms identically
Sec. 2.2.3
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture2-vocabulary-postings.pdf

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture2-vocabulary-postings
text:
Stop words
▪With a stop list, you exclude from the dictionary 
entirely the commonest words. Intuition:
▪They have little semantic content: the, a, and, to, be
▪There are a lot of them: ~30% of postings for top 30 words
▪But the trend is away from doing this:
▪Good compression techniques (IIR 5) means the space for including 
stop words in a system is very small
▪Good query optimization techniques (IIR 7) mean you pay little at 
query time for including stop words.
▪You need them for:
▪Phrase queries: “King of Denmark”
▪Various song titles, etc.: “Let it be”, “To be or not to be”
▪Relational queries: “flights to Venice”
Sec. 2.2.2
source_path: G:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture2-vocabulary-postings.pdf

text:
Porter’s algorithm
▪Commonest algorithm for stemming English
▪The Porter stemming algorithm (1980) reduces English 
words to their morphological stems through a sequence of 
rule-based reductions.
▪Phases of reductions
▪phases applied sequentially
▪each phase consists of a set of commands
▪Sample convention: Of the rules in a compound command 
in a given phase, select the one that applies to the longest 
matching suffix to be removed

text:
How to input text into a DNN for IR
▪LLMs such as ChatGPT and GPT-4 also used for Neural-IR
▪They tokenizes input and output text using a technique 
called byte pair encoding (BPE)
▪LLMs learn to understand statistical relationships between 
these tokens, associated with vector embeddings
1. Initial Vocabulary: Initially, each character in the text is treated as a token
2. Tokenization: The algorithm iteratively merges the most frequent pair of 
consecutive tokens (characters or subwords) to form a new token. This process 
is repeated until a predefined vocabulary size is reached.
3. Building Vocabulary: As tokens are merged, the vocabulary grows, and the 
most frequent subword units become tokens in the vocabulary.

doc_id: Information rtrival and web search/lecture2-vocabulary-postings
text:
How to input text into a deep neural
network for IR
▪WordPiece Tokenizer for BERT 
1. Basic Tokenization: BERT first tokenize the input text into words using a 
basic tokenizer. This tokenizer typically splits the text on spaces and 
punctuation
2. WordPiece Tokenization: After basic tokenization, BERT applies WordPiece
tokenization. This method further breaks down words into smaller 
subword units called WordPieces. 
3. Vocabulary Building: The initial vocabulary consists of individual 
characters and some common subword units. BERT updates this 
vocabulary by learning which subword units are most useful for 
representing the language. Like BPE, also WordPiece exploits the 
frequency by merging the initial vocabulary.

▪Each vocabulary term is hashed to an integer
▪(We assume you’ve seen hashtables before)
▪Pros:
▪Lookup is faster than for a tree: O(1)
▪Cons:
▪No easy way to find minor variants:
▪judgment/judgement   [two different correct spellings)
▪No prefix search with wildcard  
[tolerant  retrieval]
▪If vocabulary keeps growing, need to occasionally do the 
expensive operation of rehashing everything

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪
Generalization of binary trees
▪
Nodes can have more than two children
▪
Because a range of child nodes is permitted, B-trees do not need re-balancing 
as frequently as other trees
▪
In a B-tree of order m, each nonleaf node has up to m children
▪
If a node has n <= m children, it contains n−1 keys 
▪
The keys act as separation values dividing its subtrees 
a-hu
hy-m

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪
Binary search trees are not good for spatial locality 
▪
a given node of a binary tree probably occupies only a fraction of any 
memory block (on disks or caches)
▪
B-trees are a way to get better locality by putting multiple elements into each 
tree node/memory block 
▪
B-trees were originally invented for storing data structures on disks, 
where locality is even more crucial than with main memory
▪
Nowadays B-trees may also be useful for in-memory data structures 
▪
Main memory is nowadays very slow relative to the processor, and thus 
effective cache exploitation is crucial for efficient algorithms

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪A B-tree of order m is a search tree in which each nonleaf node has 
up to m children
▪In-memory B-tree
▪We can choose m so that the pointers to the m children plus 
the m−1 elements fill out a cache line at the highest level of the memory 
hierarchy (where we can expect to get cache hits)
▪Each leaf node stores some number of elements: the maximum number is 
typically less than or equal to m (but can also be a multiple of m)

text:
Trees
▪Simplest: binary tree
▪More usual: B-trees
▪Trees, unlike hashtables, require a standard ordering of 
characters and hence strings … but we typically have one
▪Lexicographic order
▪Pros:
▪Solves the prefix problem (terms starting with hyp)
▪Cons:
▪Slower: O(log M)  [and this requires balanced tree]
▪Rebalancing binary trees is expensive
▪But B-trees mitigate the rebalancing problem

▪With 3-grams, considering the leading and trailing $

▪Two principal uses

▪Two main flavors:
▪Isolated word

▪Will not catch typos resulting in correctly spelled words
 
 
e.g., from → form
▪Context-sensitive
▪Look at surrounding words,

Document correction
▪Especially needed for OCR’ed documents
▪Correction algorithms are tuned for specific cases: rn/m
▪Can use domain-specific knowledge
▪E.g., OCR can confuse O and D more often than it would confuse O 
and I (adjacent on the QWERTY keyboard, so more likely 
interchanged in typing).
▪But also: web pages and even printed material have 
typos
▪Goal: index dictionary containing fewer misspellings

text:
Isolated word correction
▪PROBLEM STATEMENT
▪Given a lexicon and a character sequence Q, return the 
words in the lexicon closest to Q
▪What’s “closest”?
▪We’ll study several alternatives
▪Edit distance (Levenshtein distance)
▪Weighted edit distance
▪n-gram overlap (mainly to reduce the search space) 
▪Principles
▪Among alternative correct spellings for a mis-spelled 
query, choose the “nearest” one
▪If two correctly spelled queries are tied (or nearly tied), 
select the most common (in collection or query log)
